Title: NOTTINGHAM Indoor Climbing – Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at Nottingham Climbing Centre
Climbing evenings are held regularly and we tend to go to the local pub – The Lion for refreshments and a social gathering afterwards where they have a wide variety of beverages both alcoholic and non-alcoholic.
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals.
Nottingham Climbing Centre
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised and will need to book onto a future beginners event.  If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Title: Nottingham Bouldering - MONDAY - Beginners Welcome
Summary:
Swap trainers for climbing shoes and join OutdoorLads for an evening of INDOOR BOULDERING at Nottingham’s newest bouldering venue
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, either as an experienced climber or beginner, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals. There is a limit to the number of beginners that we can accommodate at each session. Each beginner needs to be supervised so we need to ensure there are enough experienced climbers to help you out. Climbing is great fun! It's a fab workout, in a social environment, and anyone can do it! Give it a go!
What is Bouldering?
It's a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes it an excellent activity for newcomers to the sport. No wonder it's often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
I'm new to Bouldering - can I come along?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
I've been Bouldering before?
As above, everyone attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are responsible for your own safety, actions and involvement at all times. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive.) You can find the
link
to the waiver and bouldering safety video
here
on Nottingham Climbing Centre's website.
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: BIRMINGHAM Indoor Climbing - MONDAY - Experienced
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an evening at Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre.
"EXPERIENCED" in this instance is those that have previously climbed & can answer YES to all three of these questions:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie in to a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly & confidently?
Please ensure you only sign up for this event if you
satisfy all three of the prerequisites as an experienced (independent) climber!
Never climbed before? Need a refresher on the basics? Answered NO to any of the three questions above? You will need to
attend one of the beginners (supervised) events
instead, where we will happily sign you in to be supervised (two people per leader maximum), show you the ropes & teach you everything you need to know to climb safely! Beginner (supervised) events are not offered every week.
If you are climbing with us for the first time, whether Experienced or Beginner, it is essential that you send a note to the Event Leader telling them about your previous climbing experience & your goals!
Bouldering
- Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer to do bouldering, you can sign yourself in & go bouldering (unsupervised) instead of, or as well as, roped climbing. Please meet the leader & the group at the start though; some others might wish to do bouldering too!
Please note that we will not teach lead climbing or lead belaying on this event -
we would suggest having some
lessons in Lead Climbing
by a qualified member of staff if this is something you're interested in pursuing!
Whilst this event finishes at 8pm, the centre is open until 10pm if you wish to continue climbing for longer!
If you are subsequently unable to attend, please cancel off the event as 'no-shows' are not only rude, but also frustrating for the leader & other attendees who may have travelled over an hour away to climb with you!
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
Indoor climbing centres offer the challenges of climbing that you'll find on natural rock crags - simply indoors! It's a fun way to stay fit, make new friends & develop your climbing skills.
Climbing
- Unlike Indoor Bouldering there is a certain amount of skill to be mastered, such as tying in knots & belaying a partner, as well as the climbs being higher than in bouldering. If you are new to climbing or would like a refresher, then this is just for you!! If you’ve climbed before, can safely tie into a harness & can competently belay, then there’s plenty of choices of top-rope & lead climbing on offer here.
Bouldering
- Instead of tackling long high routes, the 'problems' (as they are referred to) are shorter & focus on technique, balance, body positioning & sometimes power. With the different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, so it’s excellent (but not necessarily easier) for newcomers.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
has more than 75 lines with over 300 routes, set through some spectacular ground including its notorious 'stalactite'. It even features one of the few indoor 'Yorkshire VS cracks' in the country, to test your jamming skills. The bouldering rooms are just as interesting as its climbing routes, over 200 bouldering problems have been organised into graded circuits to provide something for everyone.
You MUST register via this event page before coming to the centre!
To climb, you will need to:
Complete a
Registration Form
(please arrive early to do this in-person the first time) & pay a Registration Fee:
Annual Registration
is
£5
or
Day Registration
is
£3
Pay an
Entry Fee
directly to the climbing centre:
Standard Entry
is
£12.50
&
Bouldering Only
is
£8.50.
Hire
climbing shoes (
£3.50
) & a climbing harness (
£3.50
) if you don't own your own.
Please see Redpoint's
website
for current prices & note that prices are subject to change.
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
PLEASE NOTE:
All those attending the event must also be aware of the climbing centre's own terms & conditions of participation & abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre's staff.
Photos:
taken & used with permission of
R. Casabuena
&
K. Jones
.
Difficulty & Distance: This event is for EXPERIENCED (independent) climbers only!!!

Title: BIRMINGHAM Indoor Climbing - MONDAY - Beginners
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an evening at Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre.
*** Beginner (supervised) events are not offered every week ***
If you have never climbed before (or need a refresher on the basics), then this BEGINNERS EVENT is ideal for you! We will happily show you the ropes & teach you everything you need to know to climb safely!
Event pages are split into
BEGINNER & EXPERIENCED.
Please ensure you sign up to the correct event as we can only supervise two beginners at once per leader.
This event is for beginner climbers (those needing to be supervised) only!
If you are competent with how to tie in & belay (safely & confidently), then you will need to
sign up to the accompanying experienced (independent climber) event
instead.
If you are climbing with us for the first time, whether Experienced or Beginner, it is essential that you send a note to the Event Leader telling them about your previous climbing experience & your goals!
Bouldering
- Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer to do bouldering, you can sign yourself in & go bouldering (unsupervised) instead of, or as well as, roped climbing. Please meet the leader & the group at the start though; some others might wish to do bouldering too!
Please note that we will not teach lead climbing or lead belaying on this event -
we would suggest having some
lessons in Lead Climbing
by a qualified member of staff if this is something you're interested in pursuing!
If you are subsequently unable to attend, please cancel off the event. Spaces for beginners are limited & 'no-shows' are not only rude, but also frustrating for the leader & other attendees who may have travelled over an hour away to climb with you!
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
Indoor climbing centres offer the challenges of climbing that you'll find on natural rock crags - simply indoors! It's a fun way to stay fit, make new friends & develop your climbing skills.
Climbing
- Unlike Indoor Bouldering there is a certain amount of skill to be mastered, such as tying in knots & belaying a partner, as well as the climbs being higher than in bouldering. If you are new to climbing (or would like a refresher), there are plenty of top-rope routes to choose from after learning the essential climbing & safety techniques with one of our friendly, experienced climbing leaders.
Bouldering
- Instead of tackling long high routes, the 'problems' (as they are referred to) are shorter & focus on technique, balance, body positioning & sometimes power. With the different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, so it’s excellent (but not necessarily easier) for newcomers.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
has more than 75 lines with over 300 routes, set through some spectacular ground including its notorious 'stalactite'. It even features one of the few indoor 'Yorkshire VS cracks' in the country, to test your jamming skills. The bouldering rooms are just as interesting as its climbing routes, over 200 bouldering problems have been organised into graded circuits to provide something for everyone.
You MUST register via this event page before coming to the centre!

directly to the climbing centre:

climbing shoes (

) & a climbing harness (
£3.50

Please see Redpoint's
website
for current prices & note that prices are subject to change.
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
PLEASE NOTE:
All those attending the event must also be aware of the climbing centre's own terms & conditions of participation & abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre's staff.

Title: Indoor Climbing Kendal - (Experienced Climbers)
Summary:
This event is intended as a safe space for the LGBTQ+ community; everyone will be warmly welcomed
Join us for a full day at one of the UK's tallest walls. With big over hangs and feature walls up to 25m high it offers both fun and more challenging routes!
Most of the centre is set up for lead climbing with a few top-rope setups so this sessions is better suited to experienced climbers. If you have done some leading at other centres you will be fine.
Climbing is the main focus of the event, but this is also a great chance to meet other members and build lasting friendships. Many Outdoor Lads members join through climbing, so if you're new to the group and want to know more, come along and chat over a cuppa during the tea and cake break.
I'm new to Climbing - can I come along?   If you've never climbed before but want to give it a try - then OutdoorLads run a weekly event in Manchester (and many other cities across the country!) where we're more than happy to show you the ropes  and teach you everything you need to know to climb safely, but unfortunately this event is for climbers who do not need to be supervised - and allows our volunteer leader to push themselves a little more. Therefore, if you answer NO to any of the following three questions, then please check out some of our other local climbing events which will be more suitable for you;
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie in to a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
Everything else you need to know
You will need to register with Kendal wall before your first visit to the centre but you only need to do this once. If you are an experienced climber, you can save time and do this online
here
, selecting the full registration option.
Photos provided by j.hutton
Difficulty & Distance: This centre has plenty of lead climbing walls making it more suitable for experienced climbers. Some top rope walls are available.

Title: Simon’s Seat & Valley of Desolation Circuit
Summary:
Join Mike and Jake on this walk featuring a dramatic waterfall, a pretty wooded valley and steep climbs to the summit of Simon’s Seat.
The Valley of Desolation given its name after a massive storm in 1826, is now a tranquil woody ravine featuring a beautiful waterfall. Simon’s seat is a high-gritstone outcrop providing expansive views over Wharfedale.
Not suitable for dogs because they are not permitted on Barden Moor and Barden Fell to protect nesting birds.
We will stop halfway for our packed lunches.
Distance: Approx. 7 miles
Image credits: All photographs taken by walk leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Moderate to challenging due to elevation gain (approx 530m)

Title: LONDON  BOULDERING (SUNDAY) + Optional Pub visit. 😃
Summary:
Come and join us for a chilled and fun Sunday bouldering session!
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the ‘problems’ as they are referred to, tend to be lower/shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn’t have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport. With climbs of various grades suitable for complete beginners to seasoned pros, you decide how much you wish to challenge yourself.
If you have not visited the Stronghold Centre before, it is recommended that you
pre-register online
prior to your first visit. Please watch the online introduction video as you may be asked a few easy questions to complete your registration at the centre.
We look forward to seeing you at what is always a fun event.
If you are new to bouldering you are welcome to join us at our regular weekly Thursday evening and Saturday morning indoor bouldering sessions which are open to all levels.
All those attending must also be aware of the centre’s own T&C’s etc. You are responsible at all times for your own safety, actions and involvement. As such you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Can't make this event?
We run weekly climbing and bouldering events in the South East, so why not check out what's coming up near you here:
http://outdoorlads.events/SouthEastIndoorClimbing
. Alternatively, stay updated on upcoming events using the social media channels below:
Telegram
(Climbing and bouldering only):
OutdoorLads (SE) Climbing
Facebook:
http://fb.com/OutdoorLadsSouthEast
Twitter:
@OdlSouthEast
IMPORTANT! - Participation Statement
You MUST complete a
Participation Statement
,
in addition
to booking your event space before attending an OutdoorLads event. You only need to complete this Participation Statement once, not for each event you attend.
Anyone with COVID-19 symptoms, even if they are extremely mild, or who has recently tested positive for Covid-19, is asked not to attend.

Title: The Hot Beds of Bucks
Summary:
Check out the hot beds over an exhilarating SE500 walk in the beautiful River Chess valley and surrounds.
The route begins crossing Chorleywood Common, a special, preserved area of land which is home to over 70 plant species, 50 bird species and 300 fungi, due to the acid heathland and chalk meadows. The route then continues through woodland down to the River Chess. The rest of the way comprises several ups and downs meeting the River and crossing it. The river itself is clear and clean: the chalk land keeps mud at bay! The valley is beautiful and home to further areas of ancient woodland and preserved wildlife species, bats and owls.
The village of Chenies contains an ancient church and manor house, which we pass. St Michaels church contains a specially decorated Norman font with interwoven vines. Chenies manor house has appeared in Midsomer Murders. The route takes three turns up and down the river, crossing at a fall in the managed levels.
Latimer house was once a rival to Chenies, with the two settlements held by different families. The small village includes a triangle shaped green and water pump: they have been pumping here for centuries! We then go up Flaunden Bottom to the hill and hamlet, where the route joins the Chiltern Way.
A descent takes us to the hot beds. Can you guess what the spicey hotness growing here is? It is reputed to be delicious and healthy, as well as trendy in eating places. The clean water of the river Chess makes this the centre for growing the hot stuff in England. Want to know more? Come and see!
The route completes via Church End and the east side of Chorleywood Common, giving an all round view of this beautiful valley. Check the
route
here.
Build your fitness with the exclusive Outdoorlads SE500 series of walks: walks in the South East with 500 metres of ascent over many ups and downs. Over the day, you will build muscle and lose calories: approx. 2100 calories are used up for this length of walk and ascent!

Title: Farnham Pub Supper Social
Summary:
Join us for a relaxed pub dinner at The Castle, a welcoming spot in the heart of Farnham. It’s a great chance to unwind over some hearty food and a drink or two, while catching up with fellow Outdoor Lads in a friendly, informal setting.
Expect classic pub fare and a good selection of drinks — perfect for an easygoing evening of conversation and laughs. As always, it’s all about good company, a laid-back vibe, and a simple recipe: tasty food, great people, and a fun night out. 🍽️🍻😊
To see all the walking events in the southeast, please click this
link
.
Photographs used with permission from ODL repository.
Difficulty & Distance: Social event

Title: Mid-Week Walk on the Edge of the Peak District
Summary:
Join us for a relatively easy and varied walk. We will pass along a section of canal, an interesting bridge, a former derelict industrial site transformed into a park, a disused railway and finishing with a stiff climb to take in he views over Greater Manchester and the Cheshire Plain.
We start with a short climb up to the Peak Forest Canal which we follow through to Disley before descending to follow the River Goyt to New Mills. Passing under the town via the Millennium Walkway and the Torrs Riverside Park, brings us to the Sett Valley Trail along the former Railway to Hayfield.
The last section of the walk will involve a climb to Cobden Edge, (The highest elevation in Stockport) where should the weather be favourable offers excellent views over to Kinder to the East and much of Greater Manchester and the Cheshire Plain to the west.
Descending back to the cars, we will have the option to stop at a pub for refreshments.
All photos taken by the Author
Well behaved dogs are welcome.
Difficulty & Distance: 13 km (8 miles), 400 m ascent

Title: Bike Week 2026: World Naked Bike Ride London
Summary:
Bike Week 2026 is taking place from June 9–15, and it's our chance to be part of the UK’s biggest celebration of cycling
Whether you ride every day or haven’t cycled in years, Bike Week is all about showcasing how cycling can fit into daily life - for everyone.
Join Outdoorlads and around 1000 other cyclists on the annual World Naked Bike Ride (WNBR) in London. It's a free, fun,  legal event, run by volunteers with the aim of making the world a better place for everyone. You can see a preview of the ride at
Video for participants - WNBR London
Cleaner environment, safer roads, healthier lifestyles, better attitudes
The ride demonstrates the vulnerability of cyclists while celebrating body freedom and is a protest against fossil fuels and car culture.
Bare as you dare
As it's a "naked" ride, clothing is optional. OutdoorLads requires you to wear a helmet and suitable footwear (trainers / cycling shoes)
There is
no pressure to be completely naked
. The event has a carnival feel, so you are encouraged to adorn yourself with colourful body paint and slogans, wear fancy dress, disguise yourself a bit (eg sunglasses, helmet, mask) or remain partially dressed – be as bare as you dare.
Many passers by and photographers who know about the event will take photographs before the event begins and along the route. There will be lots of people with cameras and phones. If you're at all uncomfortable with appearing naked in photos, you're welcome to wear shorts, speedos, etc. You may prefer to start the ride with your clothes on.  We stop soon after the start to allow everyone to join up, so that can be a good time to undress.
Photo credit
: All images taken/created by the ride reader and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: A fun leisure cycle

Title: BSC26: Ultimate White Water Rafting (Sat AM session 2)
Summary:
This is one of the events organised as part of
BIG Spring Camp 2026
.
Always a favourite BIG Spring Camp activity, white water rafting at the National White Water Centre means taking on the challenging Afon Tryweryn where you'll be swept through crashing waves and boulder strewn rapids on this fast flowing Welsh mountain river.
This two hour session takes you down the Upper Tryweryn’s grade 3-4 natural, Welsh mountain white water rapids, with enough time to usually fit in 4 exciting and challenging runs of the 1.5km section!
Curious?
Here's a video from BSC15
!
Please note that to attend this event, you must have a weight of under 115kg (18st) and be able to swim at least 25 metre
If this event fills and a waiting list forms, we will look to increase capacity wherever possible.
Photos: From OutdoorLads Members at BIG Spring Camp 2024 & 2025
Difficulty & Distance: A reasonable level of fitness will be required to paddle

Title: Marple to Mellor day walk
Summary:
Stockport is home to a variety of footpaths and national trails interspersed with suburban sprawl. As such we can quickly feel like we are in the open countryside even when we are close to built-up areas. On this 15.5 km (~9.6 mile) walk we will visit a variety of local points of interest, including:
Etherow Country Park, one of England's first country parks
The Peak Forest Canal and Marple lock flight
Greater Manchester's first vineyard
Mellor archeological site
Brabyns Park
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do
sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Image credits:
All images for this event were taken by the event leader and are used here with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: 15.5 km or about 9.6 miles, 400m ascent.

Title: Early Spring Camping in Alfriston
Summary:
After a several year hiatus, we return to Alfriston for early spring camping in the heart of the Lullington Heath National Nature Reserve.  The village, which was first mentioned in the Domesday Book, lies along the banks of the Cuckmere River and is home to four very nice pubs (two from the 14th century), which can serve as options for dinner.
Friday Afternoon:
Arrive any time after 13.30, look for the ODL Flag, pitch up, and settle in.  There is no Reception to check in with; someone will come find you.  You can cook at your tent, or we can wander over to any of the local pubs for dinner.  In the evening we'll light a campfire, break out the s'mores and try to stay warm!
Additionally there is a strict "no music after 10.00pm" rule that is rigourously enforced!
Saturday morning:
Should the weather cooperate, we will look into this just under
8 mile loop
around The Rails and along the banks of the Cuckmere River with a pub stop when we get back to Alfriston.  The other option is about the same distance but goes to the
Rathfinny WIne Estate
, with a walk through the vineyard and wine tasting.  A portion of this route (just under a half mile) is on a paved "C" road that has a pathway for at least part of the stretch.
Saturday Evening
After unwinding back at the campground, dinner can again be cooked around the tents or back to the village for dinner.  Options include
Ye Olde Smugglers Inne
(pub classics with locally sourced ingredients),
Poco
(primarily Italian with vegan, vegetarian and gluten-free options),
The George Inn
(several very nice vegetarian options) or The Six Bells (sadly no website).
Sunday
If the weather is decent, breakfast can be a bit relaxed but we need to be off site by midday.
Campsite Amenities
This site offers modern facilities, including:
A block containing toilets and 10 shower stalls;
Washing-up station for convenience;
Firepits can be hired at £15 and include the first bag of logs and firestarters.
Additional bags of logs are £9 each
Pack your tent, walking boots, and sense of adventure—we can’t wait to see you there!
There are only a small number of EHU's available but they cannot be booked in advance - so if you need one get there early!!  Additionally, they are coin operated - £4 for 24 hours.
New to Camping ?
If this is your first event, don't be put off if you lack some of the necessary equipment; just message me on
chris.keivit@outdoorlads.com
or on Telegram -
@chriskk_fafner
.
Campfires & BBQ's
: This event may include a campfire/BBQ at the site.  Whilst our event leaders will take safety precautions there is still a risk to members. Please consider your behaviour around the campfire for your own and others safety.
Dogs:
The site is dog friendly (kept on the leads) - at no additional cost!
Photos:
All photos have been provided by OutdoorLads members and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Moderate hiking through the South Downs

Title: Board Game Cafe - Birmingham
Summary:
An evening of board games, food, drink and probably polite company.  Join Outdoorlads for an evening at the popular board game cafe in Birmingham.
There's a small cover charge of £2.50 per person, per hour. This is paid when the leader books the table, so expect a message the day before to confirm your attendnace.  We will likely play in small groups of 5.
If the event is full, join the waiting list and I'll book a additional tables if there is enough interest.

Title: Harrogate Stray and Showground Stroll
Summary:
Join Mike on this walk which starts in the centre of Harrogate. The walk will encompass The Stray, a 200-acre protected area of open parkland which is famous for its crocuses and cherry blossom trees. We will also visit the Yorkshire Showground, home of the Great Yorkshire show, England’s largest agricultural show, which is held annually.
Well behaved dogs are allowed on this walk but would need to be on a lead for large parts of the route as we will be walking on footpaths and pavements in quiet residential areas. We will stop halfway for our packed lunches. After the walk there will be an opportunity to go to a local pub/café in Harrogate town centre.
Distance: Approx. 8-9 miles
Image credits: All photographs taken by walk leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Some inclines and declines but mostly flat

Title: Maghull to Liverpool canal walk (LLC#8)
Summary:
The linear walk from Maghull to Liverpool follows a stretch of the 204km (127.25 miles) Leeds & Liverpool Canal, which opened in 1816 after more than 40 years of construction.
This is the
final stage (8 of 8)
which is covering the full length of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
Starting in the leafy town of Maghull, the route moves from the quiet to the vibrant as we pass into the heart of Liverpool city.
There's still charm on the canal as we pass through green spaces in the urban sprawl, but as we close in on our destination we'll be passing through Bootle with many old warehouse, bridges and remnants of Liverpool's maritime heritage.
It’s a fitting finale to a journey that spans countryside, towns, and the industrial legacy of northern England - passing from one powerhouse city to another.
For anyone that wants to join, we'll have an optional pub trip in Liverpool before our return to celebrate the completion of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
We'll return by public transport (for those that need to get back to Maghull) - however the best option will depend on your individual onward travel plans.
Stage 1: Leeds to Bingley - 30 Aug 2025
Stage 2: Bingley to Gargrave - 20 Sep 2025
Stage 3: Gargrave to Burnley - 21st Feb 2026
Stage 4: Burnley to Blackburn - 21st March 2026
Stage 5: Blackburn to Adlington - 11th April 2026
Stage 6: Adlington to Parbold - 16th May 2026
Stage 7: Parbold to Maghull - 6th June 2026
Stage 8: Maghull to Liverpool - 27th June 2026
Dogs:
No dogs on this leg of the walk due to the length of walk and narrow towpaths which will be busy with cyclists and other walkers.
If the event is fully booked:
Please
join the wait list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
If this is your first OutdoorLads walk event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.
Image / photo credits:
All images are those of OutdoorLads leaders - with permission for OutdoorLads to use.
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance of 22 km (approx. 13.5 miles). Flat, on paved towpaths. Climb of 34m total. Approximately 6.5 hours including lunch.

Title: Parbold to Maghull canal walk (LLC#7)
Summary:
The linear walk from Parbold to Maghull follows a stretch of the 204km (127.25 miles) Leeds & Liverpool Canal, which opened in 1816 after more than 40 years of construction.
This is
Stage 7 of 8
which will span the full length of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
Starting in the charming village of Parbold, the route weaves through the heart of Lancashire’s countryside and heritage with a blend of rural tranquility and industrial echoes.
We'll take in a very peaceful stretch of the canal with wildflowers and even a sunflower field, but we're never far from the clues of the canal's industrial heritage. The path skirts around Ormskirk before heading south to Maghull where our walk finishes.
It's recommended to travel by public transport, but for anyone driving you'll need to check your return journey (more details under the map). The best option will depend on your individual onward travel plans.

Title: The Saltings and Moorings of Manhood - The Annual Nudist Beach Walk
Summary:
A manhood or two might well be seen on the isolated naturist beach we'll visit, but Manhood is also the name of the peninsula that stretches south from Chichester to Selsey Bill. It is a strongly horizontal landscape of contrasting blocks of colour. Wide golden wheat fields and broad brown mudflats are backed by big skies, while the regular rows of poplars, the massed masts of yachts and the sporadic spires of churches provide vertical punctuation. Much of the area has been reclaimed from the sea and is drained by the ditches we'll follow, along with the Chichester Canal. The scattered settlements domesticate an expanse so flat that despite walking 17 kilometres, we'll not climb at all.
The sights:
The Manhood Peninsula:
The southernmost part of Sussex and part of the Sussex coastal plain. Bordered to the west by Chichester Harbour and to the east by Pagham Harbour. 'Manhood' comes from the Old English
maene-wudu
meaning "men's wood" or "common wood", i.e. common land, so it isn't nearly as exciting as it sounds.
Chichester Canal:
Opened in 1822, its last load was transported in 1906. Runs 4.5 miles (7.2 km) from Birdham to Chichester. Some of its length is still navigable and the towpath is a popular route.
Hunston:
Not much to write about this village, but pretty Spire Cottage was built using stone reclaimed from Chichester Cathedral tower when it collapsed in 1861.
Sidlesham:
Set among cornfields are half a dozen scattered hamlets, the nicest being Sidlesham Quay beside Pagham Harbour, which looks like a miniature Bosham. We will have lunch here. The historic
Crab & Lobster
pub has a lovely garden.
Pagham Harbour:
A glorious, tranquil area of saltmarsh, mudflats, saltings (for salt extraction) and shallow lagoons. Precious for wildlife as it's an SSSI, a Local Nature Reserve and a Ramsar Site. You could see curlew sandpiper and shelduck on the mudflats and long-tailed tit and willow warbler in the hedges.
Church Norton:
Just the chancel remains from an old church, simple C13, with some fine tombs. The setting is the remarkable thing: surrounded by trees and almost on the shore, the leafy, isolated locale is rare on the Sussex coastal strip. The secluded shingle spit nearby is a nudist beach.
Swimming
This walk
does not
include swimming. If you choose to swim, you do so at your own risk as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance.
The sea water is always clean as there are no sewage outfalls nearby. You're invited to check the quality at Southern Water's
Beachbuoy
site or at the
Surfers Against Sewage
site.
The
route
(please note that this is a linear walk. A bus will take us back to Chichester)
:
We'll leave Chichester by way of the canal and walk along it to Hunston. At Hunston, we'll walk to Hunston Church along Church Lane. We'll then use footpaths to go to South Mundham and use Fisher Lane to Fisher Farm. Further footpaths will take us to Bramber Farm and Honer Farm. We'll then walk around Paghan Harbour to Halsey's Farm and then pretty Sidlesham Quay for lunch. We could also have a drink at the Crab & Lobster pub. Further walking around Pagham Harbour will bring us to the hinge of Pagham Harbour Spit at Church Norton for a break. Footpaths and then a walk along Grange Lane and Rectory Lane will take us to the B2145 and the bus stop.
Getting the bus back:
The No. 51 Stagecoach bus departs for Chichester every 30 minutes (at 33 and 53 minutes past the hour). It takes around 30 minutes to walk from the beach to the bus stop. The fare is £3, payable by any means.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them apart from during very hot weather. There is sea for them to splash in but little shade. Dogs are permitted at Church Norton Beach but there are no poop bins. A dog off the lead must be obedient.

Title: Cycling Bridleways and Lanes of Cheshire
Summary:
A
leisurely social bike
ride
into the Cheshire countryside heading out to the Holmes Chapel area.
The majority of this ride will be on quiet rural roads, with a few short off-road sections, we will pass through villages of Goostrey, Swettenham  (where skillful riders can attempt a ford crossing) & Chelford to name a few, precise route to be determined on the day, to include a cafe stop for lunch
This event is around
30 miles
and is reasonably flat except for a few short climbs when crossing river valleys.  Whilst the majority of the route is on quiet lanes, please note we do pass through Wilmslow and Holmes Chapel on some busier roads.
We have plenty of time to complete the route and we will take it at a relaxed pace and no one will be left behind.
Road cyclists:
Please be aware some paths might be a little rough and bumpy for thin tyres, but doable none the less.
Mountain bikers:
The majority of the route will be on paved surfacing, so knobbly tyres will be a little over kill for the ride, but don't worry it will be at a slow pace.
Pace and Terrain
This will be a leisurely cycle suitable for those of all levels of fitness. The majority of the route is on quiet rural lanes, with some short off road sections. This event is open to cyclists of all abilities and bike types.
If you're not a regular cyclist, please do make sure your bike is in good condition - check the tyres are well inflated, chain is oiled, and you've got a spare inner tube, drinks and snacks with you.

and

Title: An evening Leisure Cycle around the Airport
Summary:
A leisurely social bike
ride
from the suburbs of South Manchester  out to Cheshire Lanes and around the perimeter of the Airport.
The majority of the ride is on roads, but we will follow a short section of the TransPennine Trail along the River Mersey and a bridleway around the airport perimeter.
This event is around 20
miles
and is reasonably flat except for crossing the Bollin Valley
We have plenty of time to complete the route and we will take it at a relaxed pace and no one will be left behind.
Bring
Lights just in case of any unexpected delays.
Road cyclists:
Please be aware some paths might be a little rough and bumpy for thin tyres, but doable none the less.  some sections of mud can be expected on the trails.
Mountain bikers:
As much as possible we will try and keep off roads. Some navigation on-road will be unavoidable.
Pace and Terrain
This will be a leisurely cycle suitable for those of all levels of fitness. The majority of the route is off road on dedicated Cycleways, Bridleways and quiet rural lanes.  This event is open to cyclists of all abilities and bike types. The route is flat with the biggest climbs on approach to bridges!!
If you're not a regular cyclist, please do make sure your bike is in good condition - check the tyres are well inflated, chain is oiled, and you've got a spare inner tube, drinks and snacks with you.

Title: Evening Leisure Cycle - Reddish Vale & Peak Forest Canal
Summary:
Join us on this leisurely round trip of 15 miles from Levenshulme into Tameside &Stockport
Everyone (including beginners) are welcome. We'll be riding at a gentle pace of 8 to 10 miles per hour and, as always, no one will get left behind.
Starting from Levenshulme in South Manchester, we will join the Fallowfield Loop for a short distance before descending to Reddish Vale Country Park to join the Trans Pennine Trail following the River Tame.
On leaving the Trans Pennine Trail, we join the Peak Forest Canal which is followed to Romiley, along this section we pass through a long tunnel,
(A good light is required if cycling through the tunnel)
The majority  of the route is completely traffic free, with only about 3km on quiet residential roads. There will also be some short climbs out of the River Valley.
The terrain is best suited to hybrid or mountain bikes, though it can be done on a road bike. Some parts of the trail are uneven and can be quite muddy if wet. Please check that your bike is roadworthy before the ride, as you will be responsible for dealing with your own mechanical issues, should they arise on the day.

Title: Isle of Purbeck Camp
Summary:
Join us on this smaller camp near the sea on one of the South-West's most beautiful coastlines. Explore the Dorset 'Isle of Purbeck' with its rolling hills, industrial heritage, rocky bays and caves, 3 mile long sandy beach (part naturist), heritage steam railway, and the mysterious Corfe Castle!
The area offers stunning coastal and country scenery. Walking along the South West Coast Path gives breathtaking views over the coastline and the area is also the eastern gateway to the Jurassic Coast.
The Jurassic Coast is England's first Natural World Heritage Site. The story of this coastline began 250 million years ago. The rocks record the Mesozoic era - the ‘Middle Ages’ of life on Earth - comprising the Triassic, Jurassic and Cretaceous Periods of geological time and they burst with the fossils of dinosaurs. Today the spectacular geology is beautifully exposed and accessible within the World Heritage Site.
The campsite has everything we need to have a great time, there are free showers and toilets as well as dish washing facilities along with a breakfast option for those who prefer not to cook.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are welcome at no additional cost, provided they are under control .
Campervans
:  Welcomed, dependant upon site availability and at additional cost.  Please contact the OutdoorLads office on
support@outdoorlads.com
or 01614200001 to enquire.
Photos:
Provided by Outdoorlads Members.
Difficulty & Distance: Walking 7 or 9 miles camping with coastal walking generally flat

Title: Suffolk Sands, Big Skies & Saxon Secrets
Summary:
You are invited to a weekend of camping in the ancient county of Suffolk.
Suffolk offers a very different kind of walking experience to the South East. Instead of hills, ridgelines, and busy, well-trodden routes, you get open landscapes, big skies, and a real sense of space. The terrain is flatter but more varied than you’d expect—coastal paths, heathland, forest, and marsh.
Weekend Itinerary
Friday Evening - Arrival
Arrive, pitch up, and settle in. Relax and unwind around the campfire, getting to know your fellow campers.
Saturday - 14 mile circular walk
Suffolk unfolds in soft layers of sand, sky, and story, as we wander from the quiet charm of Shottisham through landscapes shaped by time—past Suffolk Punch horses in Hollesley, across the RSPB marshes, and out to the wild coastline of Shingle Street with its Martello towers standing watch. This is not a walk of steep climbs but of space and atmosphere, where wildlife, coastal winds, and centuries of history come together under vast, open skies.
Saturday Evening
As the day winds down and the Suffolk sky turns golden, we’ll settle into a relaxed evening of conversation and easy company.
Sunday
After packing away and off site by midday, there will the option for a shorter afternoon walk for those that are interested, or you can explore some of the local history such as Sutton Hoo (National Trust) which is 10 mins away.
Campsite Amenities
This campsite is a small, sustainable, family run small site offering modern facilities, including a working smallholding with animals (alpacas, goats, pigs, chickens). Guests can often help with feeding animals. The site offers direct access to walking routes through heath, forest, and fields. There is a mix of tent pitches and also electrical hook-ups for campervans.
2 toilet/shower blocks (modern + older units, hot water, including accessible toilet)
Dishwashing areas and wash-up points
Drinking water points around the site
Shared Fridges for use (located at dishwashing area)
Food & Drink
On-Site Option:
The campsite sells home-reared free range sausages, bacon and eggs from their rare breed pigs and hens; home-made jams, jellies, chutneys and honey; firewood and fire lighters
There is an onsite cafe, which on Fridays evenings offers woodfired pizza's please check out the menu here
Blue Rabbit Cafe
Evening Options:
Cook your own feast at your tent
The
Sorrell Horse Inn
, a 16th century pub is within 5 minutes walk of the campsite offers  food and of course, local suffolk cider.
Pack your tent, walking boots, and sense of adventure—we can’t wait to see you there!
New to Camping ?
Don't worry we have a useful guide to help,
what do I bring camping?
Transport:
Lift sharing or using public transport is strongly encouraged for this event, due to how the campsite operates. Please make an effort to offer a lift or find one from others signed up, where practical. Getting a train and then a lift from the station from another attendee also encouraged.
Campervans:
VW-style camper vans are welcome, although an additional charge will apply and will be paid locally. If you're bringing a van, you can camp alongside others in the group who are in tents. Plus, you can park right next to your camping area—no long hauls with heavy gear!
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs on leads are welcome, the campsite does require an additional charge which will be payable locally.
Photos:
All photo's were taken by the event leader in April 2026, and permitted for use by ODL.
Difficulty & Distance: The suffolk terrain is relatively flat, however we will be walking 14 miles on Saturday.

Title: BIG Spring Camp 2026
Summary:
🏔️ BIG Spring Camp 2026: The 20th Anniversary Blowout!
Get ready to pitch up, dress up, and celebrate!
BIG Spring Camp
is back for 2026, and it’s a massive one—we are officially
20 years old!
We’re returning to our stunning home in the heart of Eryri (Snowdonia). Imagine a weekend with full use of a rugby club base, surrounded by dramatic peaks, adrenaline-pumping adventures, and a few hundred of your newest best friends. Whether you want to conquer a mountain, get wet in a gorge, or just take it easy, this is the weekend to do it.
🎂 Twenty Years of OutdoorLads
Since 2006, we’ve been getting folks outdoors, and we aren't slowing down. Expect some special 20th-anniversary surprises throughout the weekend. We’ve had two decades of mud, sweat, and beers—let's make this the biggest celebration yet! Lots of surprises and we're changing many things up this year, including the activities on offer,  as we make this THE one to remember for the next 20 years..  We can't think of a better place to be to celebrate 20 years of OutdoorLads!

🍺 The Clubhouse & The Outdoor Inn
The clubhouse is our hub. By day, it’s a spot for coffee and watching people tumble off inflatables. By night, it’s a legendary party space.
In the clubhouse you'll also find the facilities like bathrooms and showers, and generally where you'll find the event organisers if you have any questions.
The Outdoor Inn is our more quiet bar area, located around the back of the clubhouse in a big marquee. A good place to chill out, play some board games, or just catch up with
that
guy you haven't seen since this time last year!
Saturday night in the clubhouse this year is... ✨ I C O N S ✨
Come as your favourite icon! Whether it's a gay icon, a famous landmark, the My Computer icon... Whatever it is though, it better be iconic.
🍔 Food & Fuel
No need to survive on cereal bars alone. You can do it yourself by your tent (just keep those stoves and BBQs off the grass!). Or, you can use our clubhouse catering. We'll have a range of hot breakfasts, delicious evening meals, snacks and grab-and-go packed lunches for your adventures.

Campervans/Caravans:
Space is extremely limited (approx. 20 spots). As this is a rugby club, there's no EHU or waste disposal facilities.
Book here >
🙋‍♂️ New to OutdoorLads?
If you're new (or relatively new) to OutdoorLads and worried about not knowing anybody when you get to BIG Spring Camp, then worry not! You can join one of our smaller new member camps, led by some of our amazing, approachable volunteers, who'll help introduce you to others on the first evening. You'll camp together with other new members, and have use of an event shelter for socialising near your tent. Just makes that intro to the event and to OutdoorLads that much easier!

📜 Rules to Keep Everybody Happy
The
OutdoorLads Code of Conduct
applies to everyone at all times.
Respect the grass:
This is a rugby club first and foremost. So, no glass, no dogs,  no BBQs/stoves on the grass, and no open fires (other than the 'official' campfire).
Drugs:
Zero tolerance to illegal substances. Anyone suspected of having drugs will be asked to leave immediately with potential of police involvement.
Smoking and vaping:
Not permitted anywhere in the clubhouse or marquee.
Quiet Time:
Noisy fun in the bars; quiet in the camping fields between
23:00 and 07:00
. Please note that tents have zero soundproofing.
Spacing:
2 meters between tents for fire safety.
Waste:
Sort your recycling before binning - this is the law in Wales. Don't leave any rubbish lying around for others to clear up. Large items like broken camping chairs need to go home with you to be disposed of.
Vehicles:
No cars outside the permitted car park areas. No EV charging on site.
Day visitors
: There are no day passes available. You must be booked on to this event to attend BIG Spring Camp for any duration of time.
Spread the cost:
If you would prefer to pay in monthly instalments, please
contact the office team
(or
raise a support ticket
) and we can discuss the options available to you. There will be additional terms and conditions to agree to before you commit. You won't pay anything extra to pay in instalments. The full cost must have been paid by 1st May 2026.
OutdoorLads Foundation:
Don't forget that full and concession members who may be experiencing physical, mental or financial challenges can apply for support with the costs of events themselves, or associated spend, to enable fuller participation. Further details on the
Foundation pages on our website
.
Photos:
Provided by OutdoorLads members and volunteers, and used with permission.
Dogs:
This event is not suitable for dogs and they are not permitted on site at any time.
Difficulty & Distance: BSC is as easy or hard as you make it - plenty for everyone!

Title: BSC26: Gwydir Castle - Gardens, Ghosts & Cream Tea (Sun)
Summary:
This is one of the events organised as part of
Big Spring Camp 2026
.
If the thought of anything involving a hill, a harness, or sustained physical effort is making you feel faintly unwell, this one is for you. This event is for anyone who wants a gentle, later start to the day with something worth getting out of your tent for.
A cost of £27 is payable to the venue on the day which includes admission to the house and gardens, a guided tour, and cream tea.
Barely a stone’s throw from camp,
Gwydir Castle
is a Tudor fortified manor house just a mile down the road. Rebuilt by the Wynn family in around 1490 as the seat of their family, Gwydir emerged as one of the most important cultural and political powerhouses of Wales. The site, surrounded by Grade I listed gardens, is steeped in history dense enough to keep you occupied without having to walk very far or very fast. It is, despite the name, not actually a castle in any meaningful sense and stands as a shining example of ‘build it grand enough and nobody questions your naming choices’. Royalty visited, an American newspaper tycoon once had the dining room dismantled and shipped across the Atlantic, and the grounds have been firmly occupied by peacocks since 1828. There is a lot to take in at whatever pace suits a Sunday morning.
We will meet at camp, walk down together, and start by exploring the gardens at our own pace. We will then have a guided tour of the house from the owners followed by a cream tea.
For accessibility, please bear in mind that large areas of the garden are gravel and some parts of the house have uneven flooring.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list.  Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
Photos used by kind permission of Gwydir Castle.

Title: BSC26: Elidir Fawr & Y Garn Mountain Walk (Sat)
Summary:
This is one of the events organised as part of
Big Spring Camp 2026
.
Challenging Mountain walk - 14km  6/7 hours of mountain hiking on and off path and involves 1100m of ascent.
Elidir Fawr (Electric Mountain) is scarred by the historical slate mining, and contrasts with Y Garn. The initial ascent of Elidir Fawr at 924m is a challenging 800m uphill walk to its summit. The reward is some of the finest views over the Arfon plain towards Anglesey and the surrounding mountain ranges of northern Snowdonia.
We will pass Marchlyn Mawr reservoir, which is the upper reservoir for Dinorwig Hydro-Electric power station, a pump storage facility hidden inside the mountain.
Water from this lake flows through huge tunnels into the lower reservoir Llyn Peris, which is used to generate electricity at times of peak demand.
The walk is on marked paths with some scree paths but nothing technical, however this is a challenging walk which requires a good degree of fitness to muster the energy to then conquer Y Garn at 947m.
However, all this effort is worth it for some of the most spectacular views of the neighbouring mountains, before a steep gradual descent back to Nant Peris.
*Please note this walk has been marked as difficult due to the nature of the mountainous terrain* You will need a good level of fitness ability for this walk.
Is this your first walk event with OutdoorLads? Great news! Please find more information on this here.
http://outdoorlads.com/events/my-first-day-walk
All photos taken by the leader, Ian and permission given for use by OutdoorLads.
Difficulty & Distance: Mountainous terrain - Steep ascent/descents - Good level of fitness required

Title: Portsmouth at War, Southsea at Play, the Lads at Sea
Summary:
As this event includes a bespoke
boat tour
, it is £11.50 for Full/Concession Members, and the usual £12.50 day walk cost for PAYG Members (the public would ordinarily pay £13.00 to join a boat tour).
Portsmouth was granted city status in 1926, so on this walk around its most attractive and absorbing areas, we'll see it at a moment of significant civic pride. We'll see the city at war: the historic dockyard with the ships
Warrior
(1860),
Victory
(1759) and the
Mary Rose
(1545), and Southsea Castle from where Henry VIII saw the latter ship sink. We'll see Southsea, Portsmouth's seaside suburb, at play: its esplanade, funfair, pier and rock gardens. We'll see Portsmouth Harbour from the water too, as I have chartered a boat to take us on an exclusive hour-long tour. We'll see ferries and frigates and aircraft carriers and cruise ships, and we'll see why Portsmouth is home to two thirds of our naval fleet.
What you could do in Portsmouth and Southsea after the walk:
Sunbathe on a beach nearby: Head back to the Hot Walls in Old Portsmouth. I will have shown you where this is. And Portsmouth also has a nudist beach.
Explore the ships in the Historic Dockyard: Click
here
. 12-month tickets are £51, although there is a cheaper afternoon ticket if arriving after 14.00, I believe.
Shop in Gunwharf Quays: There's a range of outlet shopping to be had with many big brands.
Ascend the Spinnaker Tower in Gunwharf Quays: Click
here
. Tickets are £15.50 (or £10.50 if bought online).
Go the Blue Reef Aquarium: Click
here
. Day tickets bought online are £15.95
The sights and
route
(please see this at the Ordnance Survey website)
:
Starting at Portsmouth Harbour Railway Station, we'll walk northwest along The Hard, to enter, having had our bags checked, the...
1. Historic Dockyard:
Established from the late C17, greatly expanded in the Georgian and Victorian periods. A large and coherent complex of naval buildings and basins, of outstanding historical importance, though architecturally varied. The Historic Dockyard is the publicly-accessible part of HM Naval Base Portsmouth. Visitor attractions include the ships
Warrior
,
Victory
and the
Mary Rose
. The Rope Houses are among the earliest cast iron-framed buildings in Britain, landmarks in industrial design. The Historic Dockyard is free to enter and walk round, although the ships in it aren't.
And continuing deeper into the dockyard, we'll see...
2. HMS Warrior:
1860, by Isaac Watts and Thomas Lloyd. Iron-hulled, armoured warship. A landmark in naval engineering. Built in response to the launching of the steam-powered ship
Napoleon
by France in 1850,
Warrior
's arrival was hastened by the launch of France's
Gloire
in 1859. It began an arms race between France and Britain that lasted for a decade.
Warrior
never saw action and became obsolete in 1873 when Britain launched the mastless HMS
Devastation
. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to board it.
Then, further on...
3. HMS M.33:
One of the last surviving ships from the Great War. Built in 1915 under the direct orders of Winston Churchill, then First Lord of the Admiralty, HMS
M.33
is the only British ship still standing from the Gallipoli Campaign (1915-1916).
And close by...
4. HMS Victory:
1759, by Sir Thomas Slade. Three-decker ship of the line and Nelson’s flagship at Trafalgar. A masterpiece of C18 naval architecture. Now at risk of collapsing under its own weight and mostly enclosed in scaffolding, its restoration will be complete in 2030. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to board it.
And finally in the Dockyard...
5. The Mary Rose Museum:
2013, by Wilkinson Eyre Architects. A dark, angular envelope enclosing the conserved hull of Henry VIII’s carrack from 1545. A dramatic and controlled space enclosing its central exhibit: most of the starboard side and keel of the ship, lifted from the bed of The Solent in 1982. The artefacts recovered are a Tudor time-capsule. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to enter the museum.
We'll then leave the Historic Dockyard and walk along to Hard to enter...
6. Gunwharf Quays:
A redevelopment of 1990s–2001 on the former HMS
Vernon
site, a naval scrapyard and ordnance supply base. A mixed commercial ensemble of limited architectural distinction, but red brick was used in deference to conservation interests. Dominated by the Spinnaker Tower (2005, 170 m), the sweeping, sail-like form of which provides a crude but beloved landmark on the harbour skyline. Survivals from before redevelopment include the Vulcan Building (No. 1 Storehouse), 1814–15, a long brick naval store by the Admiralty, with austere but refined industrial character, now adapted to commercial use. A crane for lifting shells, torpedoes and ships' figureheads evoke sense of place. The redevelopment is highly successful commercially and socially, opening up a prime piece of waterfront.
We'll then have our boat trip at 12.15 around...
7. Portsmouth Harbour:
A large natural harbour, its size and double high tide (owing to the narrowness of the entrance) most advantageous for shipping. Also protected by the Isle of Wight across The Solent. An important RAMSAR wetland site but developed all round the edge. The commentary on board the charter boat will indicate the naval base, the cruise ship terminal, the tropical fruit import quay and the submarine museum at Gosport. No cruise ships will be in on the date, but ferries will be seen in abundance. The Navy doesn't publish lists of ships present, but Portsmouth is home to the aircraft carriers
Queen Elizabeth
and
Prince of Wales
and the Type-45 Destroyers
Daring, Dauntless, Diamond, Dragon, Defender
and
Duncan
.
Disembarking the boat, we'll follow the Millennium Walkway round to Gunwharf Road and White Hart Road and we'll reach Feltham Row, a walkway beside...
8. The Camber:
Taking its name from the River Camber (now built over), the site of the medieval and Tudor dockyard. Now the home of the city's modest fishing fleet.
By going along northwest along Broad Street then round Bath Square and Tower Street we can see see some of...
9. Old Portsmouth:
The area covered by the original medieval town of Portsmouth. Situated in the south west corner of Portsea Island. The roads still largely follow their original layout. Plenty of distinctive maritime character but mostly rebuilt after wartime devastation. A very pleasant part of the city.
In particular, we'll enjoy...
10. Portsmouth Point/Spice Island:
Historic waterfront quarter associated with victualling and sailors’ taverns. Much rebuilt, post-war, but retains a degree of maritime character. The inspiration for Thomas Rowlandson's rollicking engraving Portsmouth Point and hence Sir William Walton's overture of the same name. Great views over the Harbour to Gosport here.
Ascending onto the defensive ramparts at the Hot Walls we'll pass through the...
11. Round Tower:
1418, rebuilt later C15 in stone. Cylindrical harbour defence, visually more striking than the Square Tower, its curved form well suited to its exposed position. A feature familiar from news broadcasts where the city gathers to watch naval vessels departing and arriving.
And then through the...
12. Square Tower:
1494, later modified. A solid, rectilinear artillery tower guarding the harbour entrance. Plain but powerful, with thick masonry and minimal ornament.
This is all by the Millennium Trail and promenade which we will descend to the High Street to see...
13. The Dolphin:
A fine C18 pub in which Nelson stayed, scratching his name with a diamond ring on a window pane.
And opposite...
14. Portsmouth Cathedral:
Parish church of St Thomas dating from 1180, chancel dating to C13. Largely rebuilt after Civil War devastation in 1642, including choir and tower rebuilt and cupola added in 1660s after the Restoration. Made cathedral 1927. Nave extended 1930s by Charles Nicholson in a simplified Norman-Byzantine idiom. The rebuilding interrupted by war, it was only finished in 1991. Modest in scale; an interesting and pleasing blend of post-medieval survival and interwar ecclesiastical design.
Then we'll head southeast down Pembroke Road, Duisburg Way and Clarence Parade to enter the town of Southsea and...
15. Southsea Common:
Open ground established from the C18 as part of a defensive glacis, later landscaped as a public amenity. Expansive and informal, valued more for setting and views across the Solent than for designed features. The large English elm trees are a notable asset, protected from Dutch elm disease by Portsmouth's isolation.
We'll follow a path (Serpentine Road) to emerge at Clarence Esplanade where we'll turn left to see...
16. Landing Craft LCT 7074:
1944. The last surviving Landing Craft Tank from D-Day operations, now restored and linked with the D-Day Museum. Purely functional naval engineering, its interest entirely historical.
At the D-Day Museum we'll head south to...
17. Southsea Castle.
1544, for Henry VIII, attributed to Richard Lee. A low, angular artillery fort of early modern type, later altered but demonstrating advances in gunnery made in C16. Vantage point from which Henry saw his flagship Mary Rose capsize in 1545. Rugged and compact, retaining much of its original defensive character. Stumpy lighthouse with foghorn. Free to enter, which we will. We can also have tea or coffee here.
We'll then proceed northwest along the promenade to see the new...
18. Sea defences:
A £185 million coastal defence scheme is rebuilding 4.5 km of seafront to reduce flood risk to over 10,000 properties. Works include widening the beach with imported shingle, raising promenades, and installing new barriers. Ongoing phases (2024–2027) involve closures and upgrades, improving resilience against storms and rising sea levels. The stretch at Clarence Esplanade we'll walk along was only opened in April.
And further along Clarence Esplanade we'll see...
19. World War I and World War II Memorials.
Naval Memorial, 1924, by Sir Robert Lorrimer. Impressive and abstract, with naval motifs sometimes likened to a columna rostrata (with ships' prows protruding at the top). Identical to memorials at Chatham and Plymouth. Flanking additions after 1945 by Sir Edward Maufe, carefully handled to preserve the unity of the ensemble. The 2-metre statues of service personnel in various garbs are imposing.
Further still along Clarence Esplanade, we'll reach...
20. Southsea Hoverport:
The journey to Ryde takes 10 minutes and is (the operators claim) the only year-round commercial hovercraft service still operating in the world. Hovertravel operates a pair of craft capable of 40 knots.
We'll then go past Clarence Pier along...
21. Long Curtain and Battery:
C17 sea defences, an earthwork and masonry rampart. Altered, but legible as part of the continuous line of coastal fortification stretching around Old Portsmouth.
Well then descend the fortification to the...
22. Royal Garrison Church:
Domus Dei, founded 1212 as hospice and chapel. Later adapted for military use. The marriage of Charles II of England to Catherine of Braganza took place here in 1662. Nave left roofless after 1941 bombing, chancel restored. A moving ruin, its open shell of a nave contrasting with the intact chancel.
Walking up Penny Road and then turning left along Pembroke Road, we'll the turn right up the High Street past...
23. Buckingham House (Ye Spotted Dogge):
Late C15 or early C16. Timber-framed, with later alterations; one of the oldest survivals in Portsmouth, refronted C18. Traditionally associated with John Felton, who killed George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham (1628), favourite of James I of England.
And further up...
24. Portsmouth Grammar School:
Founded in 1732, from when the present restrained but suave frontispiece dates. Selective private school. Alumni are known as Old Portsmuthians and include Roger Black, Mel Stride, Murray Gold and Ant Middleton. And...
At Cambridge Junction we'll turn left and walk along St George's Road past...
25. Landport Gate: 1760–63:
Modest Portland stone triumphal arch forming part of the fortified enceinte (enclosure of naval buildings). A restrained classical composition with rusticated base and central arch, its original defensive context lost.
We'll then walk north along Ordnance Row back to The Hard and the railway station. Then you are welcome to do as you wish. There are some suggestions below.
Dogs:
This walk is partly suitable for dogs. There are stretches where they can walk extensively on and off the lead, but they are not permitted on the beaches we'll pass, nor are they permitted in the naval base. Hot weather could make the walk exhausting for them.
(All images were taken by the leader.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 9 km (5.5 miles); relief is flat; terrain is gravel, tarmac and concrete.

Title: Sheffield Bouldering - All Abilities (beginners welcome)
Summary:
Swap trainers for climbing shoes and join OutdoorLads for an evening of INDOOR BOULDERING at The Climbing Hanger in Sheffield.
Cards/contactless ONLY  - this venue does not accept cash
If you haven't been to The Hangar before, please click
here
to pre-register. Click on the link, scroll down, select Sheffield and follow the on-screen instructions. Completing the waiver prior to arrival will get you climbing faster.
What is Bouldering?
It's a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes it an excellent activity for newcomers to the sport. No wonder it's often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
I'm new to Bouldering - can I come along?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
I've been Bouldering before?
As above, everyone attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you responsible for your own safety, actions and involvement at all times. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by the event leader / venue.
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: Let's Go To Moses Gate and Drink Water!
Summary:
Join Andy and OutdoorLads for a stunning
Saturday
stroll around Moses Gate and Drinkwater Country Parks, via Nob End and Clifton Country Park.
Back for 2026 !
Starting at Moses Gate Country Park, the route begins in a diverse natural landscape made up of woodlands, wetlands, and open meadows. The park, also known locally as Crompton Lodges, is rich in wildlife, with several lodges (small lakes) that attract birds such as herons, ducks, and kingfishers. Well-maintained paths and peaceful surroundings make it an ideal starting point, offering a calm introduction before the journey unfolds.
From here, we join the historic Manchester, Bolton & Bury Canal, a largely restored waterway that provides a scenic and tranquil corridor through the landscape. Following the towpath, we pass through Nob End, a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest. This area is particularly notable for its unusual plant life, shaped by the region’s industrial past, as well as its striking views over the River Irwell and surrounding countryside.
Continuing onward, the route leads toward Clifton, where we join sections of the Irwell Sculpture Trail—the largest public art trail in the UK. Along this stretch, the natural riverside environment is enhanced by a series of sculptures and installations, creating a unique blend of art and nature as we walk.
Drinkwater Park-
The journey then brings us to Drinkwater Park, a spacious and varied green space known for its open fields, riverside paths, and quiet woodland areas. This is an ideal spot to pause for lunch, with plenty of scenic areas along the River Irwell to relax and take in the surroundings. The park also offers expansive views and a sense of escape despite its proximity to urban areas.
After the break, we retrace our steps, enjoying the scenery from a new perspective as we make our way back along the same route, returning to Moses Gate Country Park to complete the journey.
Bolton’s largest nature reserve
At 750 acres this is Bolton’s largest nature reserve. Home to mill lodges, woodland and plenty of heritage, wildlife meets industry in a peaceful site where there is always something to see.
This area is an
SSSI
(
Site of Special Scientific Interest)
. It’s a conservation designation used in the UK to protect areas that are important for their wildlife, plants, geology, or landforms.
In simple terms, it means the area is considered
nationally important
for science and nature.
What makes a place an SSSI?
A site might be designated as an SSSI because it has:
Rare or endangered plants or animals
Unique habitats (like wetlands, heathlands, or ancient woodlands)
Important geological features (rocks, fossils, land formations)
For example, Nob End is an SSSI because of its unusual plant life, which developed due to the area’s industrial past.
Why are SSSIs protected?
SSSIs are legally protected to:
Prevent damage from development or pollution
Manage land in a way that supports wildlife
Preserve them for research, education, and future generations
Can people visit them?
Yes—many SSSIs are open to the public, and you can walk through them. However, visitors are usually expected to:
Stick to paths
Avoid disturbing wildlife
Not remove plants, rocks, or other natural features
So when you pass through an SSSI on your route, you’re walking through an area that’s considered one of the UK’s most valuable natural environments
This walk is mainly flat - approximately 10 miles.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
All photos taken by Growler
