Summary:
GET SOCIAL WITH OUTDOORLADS! Great for new and regular members!
If the event is full, please
add yourself to the waitlist
, and we will amend our booking with Rowan's if availability permits.
Ten pin bowling and optional pub social which will now take place on a regular monthly basis, as the 1st event was so well received by the attendees. All are welcome on this event, whether you’ve bowled before or you’re a complete beginner. Title: 2-Day Beginner Navigation Course
Summary:
About the Event
Learn the fundamentals of map reading and outdoor navigation in a supportive, social environment. Over two days, you’ll gain practical skills that will enable you to confidently plan and follow your own walking routes.
This course blends short indoor sessions with hands-on experience in the stunning surroundings of the Peak District.
What You’ll Learn
How to read and interpret maps
Using “handrail features” like paths, walls, and boundaries to guide your route
Measuring distance accurately
Understanding and using grid references
Planning your own walking routes
The basics of using a compass
Combining map and compass skills to navigate effectively
By the end of the course, you’ll move from relying on friends or guidebooks to confidently navigating your own adventures.
How the Weekend Works
Each day is structured to maximise both learning and enjoyment:
Morning:
We begin with a relaxed “classroom” session over breakfast at the cosy cafe
in Edale
, right in the heart of the Peak District. This is where you’ll pick up key concepts before heading outdoors.
Daytime:
Put your new skills into practice in the surrounding hills, with guidance and support from your instructor.
Afternoon Wrap-Up:
Return to the café for a well-earned slice of cake while the group reflects on the day’s learning and experiences.
Who Is This For?
Complete beginners to navigation
Walkers wanting to build confidence outdoors
Anyone looking to refresh rusty navigation skills
No prior experience is required—just enthusiasm and a willingness to learn.
Event Cost
This event is free to Full And Concession Members
of OutdoorLads. The £25 fee will be refunded back to you once you attend and complete the whole event.
The event cost is £25 for PAYG Members, representing the standard £12.50 / day of OutdoorLads walking events. Still a bargain!
Event full?
Please join the waiting list. Places usually open up as the event gets nearer
Please note this a joint event with the Met Police Mountaineering Club.
Event images provided by event Leader
Difficulty & Distance: Walking on and off path through national park, good walking fitness required. 10km distance walked each day.

Title: An evening with Leeds Male Voice Choir
Summary:
Join us for a memorable evening as we continue to celebrate 110 years of Leeds Male Voice Choir and bring in ‘The Sounds of Summer’.
With an exciting variety of music including pop favourites, showtunes, and sacred works - the concert promises to be a thrilling evening celebrating the past and inspiring the future, with familiar faces from OutdoorLads in the choir.
Tickets can be purchased online:
https://leedsmalevoicechoir.co.uk/tickets
or on the door. The cost is £12.00.
We will go for a drink at a nearby pub after the concert.
Image credits: Stage Shots Photography and Leeds Male Voice Choir. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.

Title: The Greatest Showman Sing-A-Long
Summary:
Join us for a social evening of entertainment as you watch and sing along with The Greatest Showman on a huge open-air screen on the lawn in front of the grand stately home.
The movie that has sold out cinemas all over the world! Inspired by the imagination of P. T. Barnum, this incredible musical celebrates the birth of show business and tells the story of a visionary who rose from nothing to create a spectacle that became a worldwide sensation.
Pack some food to share, bring a blanket or camping chair, and sing-along with The Greatest Showman. Plus, music to enjoy before the movie with a soundtrack of specially curated songs.
Tickets can be
purchased online
from Adventure Cinema and cost around £17.00. We will sit together (location to be arranged on site).
Further information about the event can be found on
Adventure Cinema's event page
, but please note:
Gates open at 6pm and the show runs betweeen 7.30pm and 9.15pm
Alcohol must be purchased on-site only, but there is an on-site bar
No dogs, except assistance or guide dogs
No glass
Images created by AI.

Title: Ashurst's Beacon and Beacon Country Park
Summary:
We'll make our way along the Leeds to Liverpool canal before branching off across fields towards Ashurst's Beacon, which was built in 1798 as part of a warning system during the Napoleonic Wars.
From there, we'll walk the short distance to Beacon Country Park, where we'll stop for lunch.
We'll then head back towards the canal and follow its course into Parbold for more refreshments.
In total, the walk is about
9 miles
.
Photos:
All taken by the walk leader, feel free to reproduce
Difficulty & Distance: Reasonable length (9 miles) and a climb up to Beacon Country Park

Title: Leisure Cycle - Manchester to Salford Quays and Chorlton
Summary:
Join Andrew for a relaxed Sunday bike ride from Manchester city centre to Salford Quays and Chorlton, returning to our starting point via the Fallowfield Loop
This is an urban bike ride, mostly on traffic free routes with some short sections of road. The ride will take in some of Manchester's historic industrial heritage and more recent redevelopments as well as green open space. We will start in the city centre and head along the River Irwell towards Salford Quays and MediaCityUK which were developed from the old Manchester Docks. The ride will take us past various sights of interest including the Lowry, MediaCityUK, Imperial War Museum North, Gnome Island and Old Trafford Football Ground before heading to Chorlton where we will join the Fallowfield Loop.  From here we will then pass through Alexandra Park and Hulme Park before returning to the city centre via Castlefield and the Rochdale Canal.
New riders are most welcome!
Ride Overview:
Distance:
Approx. 14 miles
Pace
: Leisurely, around 8–10 mph with stops. No one will get left behind.
Ascent:
230ft in total (mostly flat with a few gentle inclines)
Surface:
a mixture of riverside paths, canal towpaths and cycleways which may be uneven in places, with minimal on road.
Estimated ride time:
around 2 - 2.5 hours of cycling plus a 30-minute refreshment stop
Suitable for all levels of fitness and anyone looking for a leisurely social ride
Essentials to Bring:
A roadworthy bike:
Hybrid, gravel, or mountain bikes are best suited to this ride as it is largely off-road. A road bike will be fine although there are a few short uneven sections where it might be advisable to dismount and walk.
Helmet:
required for all OutdoorLads cycling events
Suitable clothing including a waterproof. Layers are recommended.
Spare inner tube or puncture kit
Water and any snacks you need
Card/cash for the café stop
Don't forget your bike lock if you have one.
Photo credits: All photos taken by the event leader and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: A leisurely cycle ride

Title: Leeds Male Voice Choir head for the Borders
Summary:
Join us for a series of concerts as we celebrate 110 years of Leeds Male Voice Choir. With an exciting variety of music including pop favourites, showtunes, and sacred works, the concerts promise to celebrate the past and inspire the future, as well as featuring some OutdoorLads members in the choir.
As part of their tour of the Borders, Leeds Male Voice Choir will be performing three concerts:
Friday 29 May at 7.30pm, at Berwick Parish Church, TD15 1DF
Saturday 30 May at 2pm, at Kelso Old Parish Church, TD5 7DH
Saturday 30 May at 7.30pm, at Melrose Parish Church, TD6 9LJ
Please mark yourself as attending this event, but tickets for each specific concert can be purchased online via
https://leedsmalevoicechoir.co.uk/tickets
or on the door of each venue (subject to availability). The cost is £12.00.

Title: Bike Week 2026: Leisure Cycle to Monton
Summary:
Bike Week 2026 is taking place from June 9–15, and it's our chance to be part of the UK’s biggest celebration of cycling
Whether you ride every day or haven’t cycled in years, Bike Week is all about showcasing how cycling can fit into daily life - for everyone.
Join Paul on this leisurely social bike ride starting from Central Manchester.
The 17 mile circular route starts with a brief venture through the heart of the City Centre, passing the Palace Theatre, the iconic Central Library and the Opera House on our way to Salford Central station via Spinningfields. Next, we will head out past Salford Cathedral and Salford University to discover the safer cycling route that runs along the busy East Lancs Road towards Worsley.
The route then follows a former railway loopline to Monton, where we have the option of a pub/snack stop. Finally, we will join the Bridgewater Canal which takes us past the Trafford Centre and Old Trafford all the way back to the city centre.
Pace and Terrain
This will be a leisurely ride, suitable for all levels of fitness. We'll aim to maintain a steady pace of around 8-10mph.  The route is relatively flat with level surfaces, apart from a few short, cobbled bits. Suitable for any roadworthy bike. Please make sure your bike is in good condition - check the tyres are well inflated, chain is oiled, and you've got a spare inner tube, drinks and snacks with you.
The first part of the ride includes roads, but once out of the city centre we have the option of using shared cycle/footpaths and cycle/bus lanes which offer protection from the traffic. The second part of the ride, once we are on the loopline is almost all off road, There's just a short on-road stretch where we will have to leave and rejoin the Bridgewater Canal to cross the Barton Road Swing Bridge.
Photos
taken by the ride leader and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: A gentle leisure cycle

Title: Outwood Bound
Summary:
Join Paul on this leisurely bike ride from Manchester to Bolton and back.
Everyone (including beginners) is welcome. No one will get left behind. We'll ride at a gentle pace of 8 to 10 miles per hour for about 3 hours, but the event timing is longer to allow for the natural stops and starts of the ride, plus a short lunch stop in Bolton.
There are frequent trains between Bolton and Manchester, so there's the option of leaving/joining the ride in Bolton if you fancy a shorter cycle of less than 2 hours. If joining us from Bolton, please book onto the event as usual, then message our ride leader Paul for details of where to meet and the approximate meeting time.
Route information
Starting from Central Manchester, we will ride along National Cycle Network (NCN) Route 55 to Bolton. This follows roads with cycle lanes as far as Eccles (or there's a slightly longer off-road option along canal paths and dedicated cycle paths). We'll then use the quiet roads in the leafy suburb of Ellesmere Park to get to Monton. There we join the former Monton Green to Bolton Railway line, known as the Roe Green loop line which keeps us mainly off road until we reach the centre of Bolton.
Our return leg continues mainly off road to NCN Route 6 and the Outwood Trail, another former rail ay line running between Radclliffe and Prestwich. After passing through Drinkwater Park to the outskirts of Salford, we will keep following NCN Route 6 (which has on and off road options) back to the centre of Manchester.
Although much of the route is traffic free or on quieter roads, we may encounter the odd busy road. There will also be some fairly gentle climbs along the way. See the
location
section below for a link to the proposed route map and elevation chart.
The terrain is best suited to hybrid or mountain bikes, though it can be done on a road bike. Some parts of the trail are uneven and can be muddy if wet. Please check that your bike is roadworthy before the ride, as you will be responsible for dealing with your own mechanical issues, should they arise on the day.
Weather Conditions
Cold, wet and windy weather conditions aren't conducive to sociable cycling, and part of the route has been known to flood after very heavy rain. So, if the weather forecast for the day indicates extreme conditions, the event may be postponed with as much notice as possible provided to participants.
All photos taken by OutdoorLads leaders and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: This is a 28 mile gentle leisure cycle. Some attendees may prefer to cycle half of the  route (14 miles) and catch a train for the other half.

Title: Spanning the Humber: Hessle to Barton Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Chris for a 10-mile (16 km) walk along the Humber Estuary, including a crossing of the Humber Bridge with wide views across the water. Opened in 1981, the bridge has a main span of 1,410 metres and was once the longest single-span suspension bridge in the world. It remains one of the longest bridges you can walk across in the UK.
After crossing, the route continues into Barton-upon-Humber and through Far Ings National Nature Reserve. We then visit the remains of the old cement works on the foreshore before walking beneath the Humber Bridge and stopping for a drink at the Viking Way Café, located at the start of the 147-mile Viking Way trail.
We then head back across the bridge towards Hessle. On the north side, we pass under the bridge again and walk down to the Humber foreshore. There is another stop at The Country Park Inn before continuing through Humber Bridge Country Park and returning to the start point.

Title: Theatre Night: Breaking the Code
Summary:
A Saturday evening theatre trip to St George’s Hall in New Mills where The Martyr's Players present 'Breaking the Code' by Hugh Whitemore.
Step into the extraordinary life of Alan Turing — the brilliant mathematician, codebreaker, and unsung hero who helped change the course of history, all while attempting to grapple with his homosexuality.
'Breaking the Code' is a powerful and moving play that explores Turing’s groundbreaking work at Bletchley Park during WWII, alongside his deeply personal story of identity, secrecy, and resilience.
A story of genius. A story of secrets. A story that still resonates today. The play features and is directed by OutdoorLads member and award-winning actor, Phil Gibbons.
This event is for the 7.30pm performance on Saturday 16 May, but if you’re unable to join us, the play is running from Wednesday 13 May.
Book your own ticket!
Tickets are £12 (excluding booking fee) and are available
here
(or from Juice Graphics Design & Print Ltd in New Mills, for anyone local) and need to be booked by each member in advance.
Recommendation for seats is the red and green sections (the performance takes place in the round) as has limited seating per performance. The leader has booked seats C1 and C2 in the red section.
The TicketSource website shows each performance as a separate page, so make sure you book for the 7.30pm performance on Saturday 16 May!
The venue only seats 44 (and tickets are already on general sale), so be quick as once tickets are gone, they’re gone!
Photos provided by The Martyrs Players, who have given permission for their use by OutdoorLads.

Title: Un Ferro Di Cavallo Italiano
Summary:
Join Andy and the OutdoorLads for una Serata Italiana, 'an Italian evening' !
Join us for a peaceful Mediterranean inspired serene
Sunday
evening as we visit La Roma Italian restaurant, followed by the relaxing atmosphere of the Horseshoe !
La Roma warmly welcomes every guest with genuine Italian hospitality, promising not just a meal but a memorable dining experience from the moment guests arrive. The friendly and attentive team takes pride in creating a relaxed and welcoming atmosphere where everyone feels at home, whether joining for a casual lunch, a romantic evening, or a special celebration with family and friends. Every visit is designed to capture the spirit of Italy — warm, vibrant, and centred around great food and good company.
Traditional Italian and English Dishes:
Serving a wide selection of traditional Italian and English dishes, La Roma celebrates authentic flavours prepared with care and passion. From classic recipes passed down through generations to beautifully presented modern favourites, each dish is crafted using quality ingredients to deliver comforting, satisfying flavours that keep guests coming back time and time again.
Perfectly situated in a picturesque setting overlooking the historic Ringley Bridge and the tranquil waters of the River Irwell, the restaurant offers a truly unique dining backdrop. Guests can enjoy stunning views while they dine, adding an extra touch of charm and character to the experience. During warmer months, the outdoor seating area allows diners to fully appreciate the scenic surroundings, making it an ideal spot to relax with friends, enjoy a leisurely meal, or simply unwind with a glass of wine as the river flows gently nearby.
La Roma’s extensive menu offers something to suit every taste and occasion. Pizza lovers can choose from a wide variety of freshly prepared options, each baked to perfection with delicious toppings and authentic Italian flavours. Pasta enthusiasts will find an impressive selection of classic and contemporary dishes, rich in flavour and expertly prepared. Alongside these favourites, the menu features an excellent range of meat and fish main courses, ensuring there is always something new and exciting to discover.
Seafood
Seafood is a particular speciality at La Roma, with carefully selected ingredients transformed into beautifully balanced dishes that highlight the freshness and natural flavours of the sea. Whether you are craving comforting Italian classics or looking to explore something new, every plate is prepared with attention to detail and a passion for quality.
At La Roma, dining is more than just eating — it is an experience to be enjoyed, shared, and remembered long after the last bite.
See the La Roma website and menu
here...
After enjoying our Italian experience, we can nip into the Horseshoe nearby...a cherished landmark and one of the oldest pubs in Radcliffe, proudly standing for over 300 years. Rich in history and character, the Horseshoe has long been a place where people come together to relax, share stories, and enjoy genuine hospitality in a setting steeped in tradition.
Nestled in the historic village of Ringley, directly opposite the peaceful flow of the River Irwell, the Horseshoe holds a fascinating place in local history. In years gone by, it served as a scheduled stopping point for Manchester-bound canal packets travelling along the waterway. Passengers would eagerly disembark to stretch their legs and enjoy refreshments, while welcoming landlords ensured weary travellers were well looked after before continuing their journey. This legacy of warmth and hospitality remains at the heart of the pub today.
Historic charm...
The Horseshoe combines historic charm with a comfortable and relaxed atmosphere, making it an inviting destination for both locals and visitors alike. Inside, guests will find plenty of welcoming indoor space filled with character, creating the perfect setting for social gatherings, quiet catch-ups, or simply unwinding after a long day. Outside, the spacious beer garden offers a pleasant place to enjoy the surroundings, providing a relaxing environment whatever the season, whether basking in sunshine or enjoying crisp fresh air.
Today, the pub is proudly run by Eileen and Andy, who took over the Horseshoe in 2017 and have continued its long-standing tradition of friendly service and community spirit. Their passion for creating a warm and welcoming environment is evident in every detail, ensuring that everyone who walks through the door feels like part of the pub’s story. With a genuine commitment to hospitality and a love for bringing people together, they look forward to welcoming both familiar faces and new visitors alike.
At the Horseshoe, history, atmosphere, and friendly company come together to create a place where memories are made and traditions continue — a true village pub with a timeless sense of welcome.
Visit the Horseshoe website
here...
Please let the leader know if there is any special occasion such as a birthday or another celebration !

Title: Fresh Air & Fairly Flat-ish: A Surrey Circular
Summary:
This circular walk starts in Milford and winds its way through peaceful countryside toward Bagmoor, with fresh greenery, birdsong, and that unmistakable “everything’s finally waking up” feeling in the air. The paths weave through woodland and open stretches, offering a relaxed, scenic start that eases you into the day.
From there, the route opens onto the heathland of Witley Common and skirts the edge of Thursley Common. It’s the kind of place where you might pause to take it all in—ostensibly for the views, but really just to justify another snack break.
As always, this is a friendly walk, and newcomers are very welcome. A great chance to get outdoors, stretch your legs and meet other Outdoor Lads.
Total distance is 16 km (10 miles) with a 136 m (447 ft) of ascent spread throughout the route. We will be walking over dirt, grass and tarmac.
Dogs
This walk is suitable for dogs, but keep in mind that we have quite a few stiles. There are some open spaces but we will mainly be walking by the canal.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
(Photos taken by Felipe Rogatto or used from ODL repository with permission).
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is 16 km (10 miles) with a 136 m (447 ft) of ascent. We will be walking over dirt, grass and tarmac.

Title: NOTTINGHAM Indoor Climbing – Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at Nottingham Climbing Centre
Climbing evenings are held regularly and we tend to go to the local pub – The Lion for refreshments and a social gathering afterwards where they have a wide variety of beverages both alcoholic and non-alcoholic.
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals.
Nottingham Climbing Centre
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised and will need to book onto a future beginners event.  If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Title: Norden to Bury -GM12
Summary:
This is stage 12 of 20 on the Greater Manchester Ringway, a 200 mile (322km) walking trail exploring all 10 boroughs of Greater Manchester. You can find out more about it
here
.
Highlights include:
Views over the chain of reservoirs in the Naden valley.
Knowle Hill, topped by its wind farm, with panoramic views that sometimes extend all the way to Snowdonia
Deeply Vale, a remote valley featuring waterfalls and evocative industrial remains
Birtle village with its pretty cottages, church and two fine pubs
Chesham Woods, a lovely area of mixed woodland on the edge of Bury
Burrs Country Park, featuring dramatic sculptures, riverside paths and passing steam trains.
Note that parts of the trail may be damp, muddy and slippery at any time of year and especially after periods of wet weather.
This is a linear walk
. See the options in the location details below.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
New Members
Please feel free to sign up as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don't hesitate to message me, or go to the
My First Event
page.
Photos by kvnmnc.
Difficulty & Distance: Around 21km (13 miles), 484m ascent

Title: Southampton Shores and Moors
Summary:
Join me in this fantastic urban parks walk exploring Southampton's shore. Our walk will start in Queen's Park, cross the Itchen Bridge, pass through the Weston Shore, Westwood, Netley Abbey, have a stop in an amazing bakery for doughnuts, then continue to Royal Victoria Country Park to reach the lovely town of Hamble-le-Rice for a lunch break. Make sure you visit Pirate Pasties to try an authentic Cornish pasty. From there we will be taking the ferry across the River Hamble and walking along the riverside, seeing donkeys and finishing our walk at the Swanwick Shore car park. This is a linear walk, but we can get the X5 bus swiftly back to Southampton city centre. The walk starts in Queen's Park, but for people who come by car we can meet up just over the Itchen Bridge at the Oakbank Road card park which is SO19 9DT.
New members are always welcome to join; it would be great to see you there. If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the 'My First Event' page on the OutdoorLads website.
Waitlist: even if the event is booked up, please add yourself to the waitlist. Spaces invariably become available as the date of the event approaches and participants drop out due to other commitments.
Dogs welcome.
(
All pictures taken by the leader
)
Difficulty & Distance: 6 miles and mostly flat.

Title: Chorlton to Chorlton, via Chorlton
Summary:
Join Andy and the OutdoorLads for a
Saturday
stretching of the legs to Chorlton and back !
Back for 2026!
Join us for a wonderful day out as we explore one of Manchester’s most vibrant and characterful neighbourhoods and its beautiful green surroundings. Our journey begins at the tranquil
Chorlton Water Park
, where lakeside views and abundant wildlife set the perfect scene for the start of our adventure. From here, we’ll follow the scenic paths along the River Mersey, enjoying fresh air, riverside landscapes, and the changing natural beauty that makes this stretch of Manchester so special.
Our walk will lead us to the charming
Didsbury Village
, a much-loved destination known for its leafy streets, independent boutiques, and welcoming atmosphere, where we’ll pause for a relaxed lunch break and time to explore.
After lunch, we’ll hop aboard the Metrolink for a comfortable ride back to Chorlton, where the day continues with well-earned refreshments at one of the area’s many popular cafés or pubs before returning to our starting point.
Before setting off, we’ll also take a short detour to visit a nearby hidden gem — a small but fascinating place of local interest that adds an extra touch of discovery to the day.
About Chorlton-cum-Hardy
Located around three miles southwest of Manchester city centre, Chorlton-cum-Hardy blends village charm with a lively, creative spirit. Its history stretches back over a thousand years, with evidence of an Anglo-Saxon settlement dating to the 9th century. Once a rural township surrounded by farmland, Chorlton has evolved into one of Manchester’s most desirable and distinctive suburbs while still retaining its strong sense of community.
Today, Chorlton is widely regarded as one of the city’s most vibrant neighbourhoods — known for its independent mindset, green spaces, and thriving cultural scene. The area is home to a wealth of independent shops, artisan bakeries, delis, coffee houses, traditional pubs, and trendy bars, giving it a relaxed yet cosmopolitan feel. Its streets are lined with Edwardian and Victorian homes, and nearby nature reserves and waterways offer an escape into nature just minutes from urban life.
Over the years, Chorlton has been home to many well-known Mancunians from the worlds of music, television, and the arts, helping to cement its reputation as a creative hub with a welcoming, inclusive atmosphere.
Whether you come for the walk, the scenery, the history, or simply good company and great food, this promises to be a memorable day out — and a perfect way to experience the unique charm of Chorlton and its surroundings.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
All photos taken by Growler

Title: Goyt Valley & Shining Tor Walk
Summary:
Join Ian on this fantastic walk in the Derbyshire Peak District, highlights of the walk include Shining Tor which is the highest hill in Cheshire at 1,834ft. Errwood Hall ruins, once a magnificent 19th century manor owned by the wealthy Grimshawe family.
From here the walk takes us down & around the shoreline of Fernillee Reservoir.
The latter part of the walk takes us across farmland with fine views over the Goyt Valley & beyond back to the start point.
Dogs are welcome
but owners must ensure they are kept under control at all times!
Waitlist:
If this event is fully booked and you really want to attend, add yourself to the waitlist because places often become available. If you are on the list but can no longer come, please cancel your attendance as soon as possible to allow waitlisted people to attend in your place.

Title: May London Social - in the West End
Summary:
Welcome to 2026!
Our monthly London pub socials are the perfect gateway to Outdoor Lads and our busy diary of brilliant events!
Typically our socials have a rich mix of regulars, occasionals, and newbies, not only from London and the South East, but dropping in from across the country.
We will have space to ourselves in this friendly venue, and you'll be very welcome to indulge in a soft drink even though we are meeting in a bar!
Whether it's hiking, camping, cycling, climbing, or a range of other activities - we do it!  Outdoors is our focus, but our socials are the chance to catch up with lads you've already met, find out about the group, and meet new members and event leaders.

Title: Big Tree Country Weekend
Summary:
Travel to the heart of Perthshire, where the lowlands meet the highlands in what is known as Big Tree Country!
We have
three nights
away to discover the delights of the area, and probably get out for a few walks..... For the mountain goats Schiehallion (Fairy Hill), Glen Lyon round and the Ben Lawers range are nearby, or there are plenty of easier options in the Tay Forest Park such as the Birks of Aberfeldy or along the River Tay by Kenmore to the shore of Loch Tay. You could even take a short drive and discover the excitement of the village of Dull - twinned with the USA town of Boring & Bland in NSW Australia as part of the 'Trinity of Tedium'!

Title: 3 Night hostel high in the hills
Summary:
A
three night
hostel over the Early May Bank Holiday weekend on the Pennine Way, in this very popular and comfortable hostel!
Come and enjoy spending your bank holiday in this stunning location, with fellow OutdoorLads. This weekend promises superb views of the North Pennines AONB, great walks in this beautiful landscape, and hopefully the late Spring weather will be kind to us. The area offers a variety of challenge in the walks, but we will get out for at least one proper fell walk on the Saturday - it's a great area for a really top-notch walk!
Alston shares the title of highest market town in England (along with Buxton) - even though there is no actual market held anymore - sitting high on the Pennine Way, with Northumberland to the east and the Eden Valley and on to the Lakes Fells to the west. Alston was historically a centre for mining, with lead, zinc, iron, copper and coal found in the vicinity. Remains of all can be seen in the area if you know where to look. It has also been used for filming of League Of Gentleman's Royston Vasey.
Our accommodation is a recently renovated, comfortable hostel right in the village, with facilities (shops, cafes, pubs) on the doorstep should we need them. There are a range of different sized bedrooms, comfortable social spaces, cycle storage and outside space to sit. We will be aiming to have a "proper" fell walk as the main activity on the Saturday.  This will be decided upon taking into account the weather and group wishes. Details of the walk will be shared at the Friday welcome meeting.
As we have a bonus day, options for Sunday include a shorter walk, but we could also be checking out the South Tynedale narrow gauge railway (35 mins to Slaggyford costs around £16 for day rover ticket), or heading over to
Nenthead
to see the evidence of past mining (lead etc in the area)- unfortunately there are no underground visits available on our weekend.
The  evenings will be sociable and relaxed so you may want to bring board games, cards etc. Someone is sure to bring Uno !
Dogs:
usually a "no dogs hostel, they have kindly relaxed the rules and allowed a max of 2 dogs on this event. The leader will be taking his dog, the other has also now been taken. Therefore we sadly cannot accommodate any other doggy requests on this occasion.
Photos:
Photos used by OutdoorLads with full permission of the leaders
Difficulty & Distance: The walks will more be of fell than mountain terrain

Title: Elenydd Escape: Remote Wales Weekend
Summary:
Ty’n Cornel sits out in the
Elenydd
... in the
Cambrian Mountains
of
Mid-Wales
... surrounded by open moorland, reservoirs and big, rolling hills in every direction.
It’s a really beautiful part of
Wales
... wide open, quiet and a bit different to the busier areas of Eryri or the South Wales mountains. You’ve got long views, winding tracks and plenty of space to just get into a steady rhythm of walking and take it all in.
Over the weekend we’ll head out for a group walk exploring the area. Other activities may be on offer depending on the leaders available.
Evenings at the hostel will be a mix of food and whatever people fancy... board games, cards or just a good catch up after being out on the hills.
If the skies are clear, it’s also a great spot for stargazing... proper
dark
skies
and more stars than you usually get to see.
If you’re after a weekend with great scenery, a decent walk and a good group to spend it with, this should be a good one.
Dogs
Sorry, Dogs are not allowed in this hostel.

here
.

Title: To the castle on the hill
Summary:
Distance is 11.4 miles, elevation 2085ft. Terrain- quiet roads, dirt tracks, farmland, mountain tracks.
Join me on my first walk for ODL in this beautiful part of Wales. We will start from Llandeilo railway station, cross the river Towi, through the woodland nature reserve of
Coed Tregib
were we will join some of  the Heart of Wales line trail through farmland and hills until we reach the castle perched on a hill.
After lunch at the on site café (feel free to bring a packed lunch) we will walk through ancient woodland before ascending the mountainside to loop around
Carreg Cennen Castle
affording magnificent views of the ruins (weather permitting) before making our way back to the station.
Note there will be insufficient time to explore the ruins on this walk , perhaps you could come back another time if your interest is piqued.
Click here for the route

Title: Pang Valley: Where A Future Queen Hails From & Once Roamed
Summary:
Join us for a superb 12-mile walk through a tranquil corner of the West Berkshire countryside 🌿
Set between Reading and Newbury, this route explores the Pang Valley, centred on the River Pang, a classic chalk stream rising near Compton and flowing south to meet the River Thames at Pangbourne. The landscape is defined by rolling downland, wooded slopes, and charming villages including Stanford Dingley, Bradfield, and Bucklebury, all of which feature along the route. The Pang’s clear waters support wildlife such as trout, water voles, and kingfishers.
Berkshire is well known for its royal associations—hence “Royal Berkshire”—largely due to Windsor. However, this quieter western area has a more modern connection: Catherine, Princess of Wales, who grew up in Bucklebury. Her engagement to Prince William brought increased attention to the village, where the Middleton family still live today. She was known to enjoy walking and cycling in the vicinity as well as visiting The Pot Kiln pub in nearby Frilsham, which we will pass en route (though not stop at as it's a bit posh!).
Don't worry if you are not fans of the Royal Family, especially in light of recent events, as I won't be banging on about them during this walk. This area is worth visiting despite the royal connection, and I had indeed done this walk in the 90's well before Bucklebury gained wider attention, and appreciated the area for its peace and understated beauty, and the fact that it has remained largely unspoilt.
The Route:
Starting from Chapel Row, we will immediately travese part of Bucklebury Common before heading north into more open and hilly countryside to Bucklebury. We will walk through the village, past the church, cross the Pang for the first time and continue northwards through a largely open and agricultural landscape. Later we enter a more wooded area, part of the Yattendon Estate, pass the Pot Kiln Pub at Frisham Common before turning back to walk southwards through more woodland and farmland to arrive at Stanford Dingley.
We will have lunch at Stanford Dingley, where there are some benches available, and are spoilt by having the choice of 2 pubs for a drink stop, either the Bull Inn or the Old Boot Inn. After lunch the remaining 6 miles is very much around the Pang valley, so we start following the course of the River Pang which eventually comes into view as we get to Bradfield, where you will get glimpses of the large school buildings and cricket ground. After walking through the village we start to loop back towards Chapel Row, taking paths more towards the top of the valley and traversing a number of quiet wooded areas in the process.
The route can be found
here
:
The Sites:
Chapel Row
A small, scattered hamlet centred around a pub, café, and village green. A notable feature is the avenue of oak trees along Bradfield Road: the first were planted to commemorate the visit of Elizabeth I to Bucklebury in 1568, with further trees added to mark later royal visits by Elizabeth II and, most recently, Princess Anne, following a gift from the late Queen to the Queen Mother for her 80th birthday.
The unusual name of the pub, the Bladebone, stems from a local legend of a mammoth bone belonging to a creature that once terrorised the area before being killed. In reality, the bone is believed to be from a whale discovered in the Kennet Valley in the 17th century; the pub sign is said to incorporate the original bone encased in copper.
Bucklebury Common
A large expanse of open land characterised by a mix of heathland, woodland, clearings, and grazing cattle.
Bucklebury
Often regarded (unofficially) as the capital of the Pang Valley, Bucklebury has origins dating back to Saxon times. Its prominence grew when Reading Abbey, which held the manor, built a residence here for the Abbot. The manor later passed to the Winchcombe family, who held it for around 150 years until the early 18th century; their influence helps explain the frequency of royal visits.
The original manor house was destroyed by fire in 1830 and subsequently demolished. The village church is Norman, with a particularly fine south doorway featuring ornate carvings of flowers and faces. In more recent history, Bucklebury is known as the home village of the Middleton family.
Stanford Dingley
Our lunch stop, this village feels notably secluded and largely unchanged. The old mill dates from Norman times. The name “Stanford Dingley” derives from an early Lord of the Manor. The church has Saxon origins, although the present building largely dates from the 13th century and retains medieval tiles and wall paintings from that period.
Bradfield
Dominated by Bradfield College, a prestigious independent school set within extensive grounds. During term time, the village can be busy with students and staff. Founded in 1850, the college is notable for its open-air Greek theatre, which remains in use and is widely regarded. Notable alumni include David Owen, Sir John Nott (politicians), Sam Thompson (Made In Chelsea), Nick Higham (journalist), and Gus Atkinson (Cricketer).
River Pang
A clear chalk stream rising near Compton, flowing through the Pang Valley before joining the Thames at Pangbourne. It is ecologically significant, supporting species such as trout, water voles, and kingfishers, and contributes greatly to the area’s tranquillity.
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, there are some road sections, crossings. stiles and some fields with farm animals (especially sheep in this area) where they will need to be on a lead. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos taken by the leader 30.03.26 and permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12.5 miles, paths of grass, dirt and tarmac, moderately undulating with a total ascent of 300 meters

Title: Bike Week 2026: World Naked Bike Ride Manchester
Summary:
Bike Week 2026 is taking place from June 9–15, and it's our chance to be part of the UK’s biggest celebration of cycling
Whether you ride every day or haven’t cycled in years, Bike Week is all about showcasing how cycling can fit into daily life - for everyone.
Join Outdoorlads and 100s of other cyclists on the annual World Naked Bike Ride (WNBR) in Manchester. It's a free, fun,  legal event, run by volunteers with the aim of making the world a better place for everyone.
We'll meet at 6.30pm, ready to join the ride round the city centre which starts around 7.00pm and takes about 90 minutes at a very gentle pace.
Cleaner environment, safer roads, healthier lifestyles, better attitudes
The ride demonstrates the vulnerability of cyclists while celebrating body freedom and is a protest against fossil fuels and car culture.
Bare as you dare
As it's a "naked" ride, clothing is optional. OutdoorLads requires you to wear a helmet and suitable footwear (trainers / cycling shoes)
There is
no pressure to be completely naked
. The event has a carnival feel, so you are encouraged to adorn yourself with colourful body paint and slogans, wear fancy dress, disguise yourself a bit (eg sunglasses, helmet, mask) or remain partially dressed – be as bare as you dare.
Many passers by and photographers who know about the event will take photographs before the event begins and along the route. There will be lots of people with cameras and phones. If you're at all uncomfortable with appearing naked in photos, you're welcome to wear shorts, speedos, etc.
Photo credit
: wnbrmanchester
Difficulty & Distance: A fun leisure cycle

Title: Hanborough to Blenheim & Evenlode Valley Walk (Lowland)
Summary:
(This walk has been prepared & written by
Jeremy Mohamed
as his assessment event as he seeks to become a Lowland & Hill Walks leader with OutdoorLads. Your support for Jeremy is sought)
This is a walk I’ve come to know well over the last few years — one I’ve mostly done alone — and I thought it would be nice to share it.
We’ll start in Long Hanborough and follow the community path into the
Blenheim Estate
, crossing into the parkland near Bladon before heading towards the lakes and cascades. This opening section adds a quieter approach into the estate before joining the main circular route.
We’ll then leave the estate and follow the River Evenlode out into the surrounding countryside, where the landscape opens up into fields, meadows and quieter paths. We’ll pass the remains of the
North Leigh Roman Villa
before continuing along riverside paths, with occasional glimpses of the railway line cutting through the valley.
Along the way, I’ll share a few stories and lesser-known bits about the area — the kind of things you won’t get on an official tour — just enough to add a bit of colour to the landscape as we go.
It’s a varied countryside walk combining estate parkland, river scenery and open Oxfordshire landscape — a route shaped by repetition, familiarity and a lot of wandering.
Approx. distance: 8.5 miles
Terrain: Fairly flat with the occasional short incline
Total ascent: Approx. 100m
Surfaces: Mix of estate paths, tracks and field paths
Dogs
Dogs are welcome, but must be kept on a lead within Blenheim Estate grounds.
Hanborough Station is just over an hour by direct train from London Paddington.
Photos
: AI-generated by leader, or courtesy of Lloyd Thomas.
Difficulty & Distance: Fairly flat terrain with the occasional short incline. The distance makes this a moderate walk overall.

Title: An Suidhe wind farm gravel cycle
Summary:
An Suidhe wind farm gravel cycle
Gravel cycling began over 100 years ago when the high passes in the tour de France were dusty roads with no tarmac. Gravel cycling features in many modern-day road cycling races such as the Strade Bianche and Paris-Roubaix.
Scotland is well served for Gravel routes with forestry commission land and new windfarm developments being popular destinations. This event features a mixture of both.
Join us on another route from the Gravel Rides Scotland book. The route takes us high above Lochs Fyne and Awe, with hopefully good views of the Argyll area.
Although fairly short there is considerable climbing involved on this cycle. A reasonable level of fitness and confidence on tricky surfaces is required.
An indication of the route is below. There are add on options.
https://www.strava.com/routes/3436440541145350300
NB: This event is not suitable for dogs.
The pictures shown are all from previous OutdoorLads gravel events (with the exception of MVDP showing off his gravel skills at the recent Benidorm cyclocross World Cup!).

Title: River Dee, Chester City Walls and the Dukes Drive 2026
Summary:
An easy walk along the River Dee at Chester including a walk round the city walls.
We will be meeting at the River Dee at Eccleston an interesting village in the Duke of Westminster’s estate. We will walk into Chester along the riverside path for about 4 miles, passing Earls Eye Meadows and crossing the river on the Queens Park Suspension Bridge.
We will stop for lunch at the Groves, a delightful riverside area. There will be an opportunity to go to a local cafe for anyone who hasn’t brought a packed lunch. After lunch we will walk round the city walls and will see lots of Roman remains, the cathedral and the famous racecourse. For those who don’t want to walk round the walls there will be time for shopping instead.
We will then cross the River again and walk along the Dukes Drive back to Eccleston and our starting point.
If you are planning to use public transport I'm sure it will be possible for someone to meet you at the railway or bus station.
Don't worry if all the places have gone, sign up anyway and you will go on the waiting list. Then if someone pulls out you will get a place.
All photos taken by Andrew
Difficulty & Distance: 8 miles 6 hours

Title: Nine Edges Derbyshire Challenge Walk
Summary:
"If your dreams do not scare you, they are not big enough." (Ellen Johnson Sirleaf)
“The best moments in our lives are not the passive, receptive, relaxing times . . . The best moments usually occur if a person’s body or mind is stretched to its limits in a voluntary effort to accomplish something difficult and worthwhile” (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990).
A challenging, linear day walk of about 35 km (22 miles) with 933 m (3,061 feet)
of total ascent traverses nine different gritstone edges in the Peak District. The walk will take about 10 hours to complete including several stops along the way.
We traverse the following Peak District edges:
Derwent Edge
Stanage Edge
Upper Burbage Edge
Lower Burbage Edge
Froggatt Edge
Curbar Edge
Baslow Edge
Gardom’s Edge
Birchen Edge
The highest point is Back Tor at 538 m (1766 feet) near to the start of the walk. The route features great views across the High Peak from the edges, and it includes Derwent Dam, the Longshaw Estate, and woods between Baslow and Gardom’s Edge. The route takes us to the trig points and/or Ethels of
Back Tor, High Neb, Stanage Edge, Birchen Edge, Lost Lad, and White Path Moss
.
We begin at Ladybower Reservoir from Fairholmes car park, past Derwent Dam then up to Lost Lad and Back Tor at the start of Derwent Edge. From here we continue on to Stanage Edge, completing the two longest edges first.
Next, we traverse Upper and Lower Burbage Edges, before heading, via the Longshaw Estate, onto edge number five, Froggatt Edge. The next two, Curbar Edge then Baslow Edge, are beautifully linked.
Finally, we walk through beautiful woods to the final two, Gardom’s Edge and Birchen Edge. The last edge is just over 1 km from the trig point to the car park at the end of the walk.
Note
, this is a linear walk
. We will rendezvous
at the end point leaving half of the cars there to travel together to the start point to begin the walk. At the end of the walk we will convey the drivers who left their cars back to the start point. The drive from the finish at Birchen Edge car park to Fairholmes car park is 16 miles, about 30-minute drive, via Hathersage.
There are limited options to do this walk via public transport. The nearest train station to the start is Bamford and at the end of the walk it’s Grindleford station, which is closer to Froggatt Edge, so quite a distance away. The walk leader will facilitate a lift from and back to Derby for anyone who needs it.
Facilities on the walk
There are public toilets available at the start of the walk at Fairholmes car park and also when you come down from Lower Burbage Edge and walk through the Longshaw Estate. There’s also a small shop/cafe you can buy takeaway food at Fairholmes.
In addition, The Grouse Inn pub is open for takeaway food/drinks before Froggatt Edge where we could stop for refreshments and a toilet stop.
At the end of the walk, there are no toilets, but The Robin Hood pub allows us to finish in style.
We will walk at a reasonable average pace of about 3.5 kph (2.2 mph) with regular stops along the way, as well as longer breaks for a packed lunch and evening meal (you will need to bring both with you). This is a long and challenging walk but it is not a race!
There is a link to the
OS Maps
route for members, below.
Dogs are welcome
but they must be capable of walking the distance and owners must ensure dogs are well nourished and kept under control at all times.
This is a very challenging walk,
published in plenty of time to prepare as necessary.  Personal safety and wellbeing is paramount, so arrangements will be put in place to 'rescue' participants, if necessary. You must ensure you are fit and well enough to undertake the walk unaided, with suitable footwear. It is 35 km (22 miles) over 1200 metres of ascent within about 10 hours.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

Title: An Evening with the Stars in Nottinghamshire
Summary:
We have secured a booking for a group visit to
Sherwood Observatory
.
Since its recent re-opening, the Observatory has been booked solid with group tours so we are pleased to offer this opportunity in Autumn 2026.
The visit promises a 90 minute adventure you'll never forget including a chance to ask anything you want to know about space and beyond. Additionally there will be:
a 20+ minute planetarium show and presentation by the expert team
a captivating talk in the lecture room that will spark your imagination
a thrilling opportunity to gaze at the stars and planets or our closest star, the Sun, depending on the season, (weather dependent)
a tour of the radio astronomy facilities
a visit of exhibition in the Science Centre to learn more about human space flight and the history of the Reservoir
Photos: Sherwood Observatory and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: Sit and watch the stars go by. The site is broadly accessible but visiting the main telescope involves climbing some steps

Title: Edward Carpenter Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Mark and Laurance for a scenic walk from the edges of Sheffield into the beautiful Cordwell Valley as we trace the route Edward Carpenter took between the city and his rural retreat at Millthorpe. This isn't just a hike; it’s a bit of a pilgrimage. We’ll be visiting the home where Carpenter lived openly with his partner, George Merrill, and where he famously hosted figures like E.M. Forster and George Bernard Shaw.
Route Highlights
Totley Hall Lane: Our starting point, leading us away from the city into the green "lung" of the Peak District fringes.
The Cordwell Valley: A stunning, often overlooked valley of ancient woodland and rolling fields.
Millthorpe: We’ll stop near Carpenter’s house where he lived the "simple life," grew his own veg, and handmade his famous sandals.
The Royal Oak: A perfect spot for a quick refreshment in the hamlet of Millthorpe.
Smeekley Wood: We will head back to Totley via this historic woodland
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Waitlist:
If this event is fully booked and you really want to attend, add yourself to the waitlist because places often become available. If you are on the list but can no longer come, please cancel your attendance as soon as possible to allow waitlisted people to attend in your place.
Image Credits: Most images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Edward Carpenter Public Domain,
https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=399785
Edward Carpenter and Merrill By Unknown author - Own work, Public Domain,
https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=52263389
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 15km (9.5 miles) with 435 m (1430 feet) of ascent. Approximately 5 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: Theatre Night: Boarding School Life as a Homo
Summary:
A relaxed Friday evening theatre trip to the Burton Taylor Studio, one of Oxford’s smaller and more intimate venues.
We’ll be seeing
An Adequate Abridgement of Boarding School Life as a Homo
— a sharp, fast-paced solo show exploring identity, sexuality and growing up in the British boarding school system.
We’ll meet beforehand for a quick drink at the Red Lion, then head over to the theatre together. After the show, we’ll grab some casual food nearby — something easy like Nando’s or similar.
Tickets are £14 (or £12 for certain concessions) and should be booked individually via the
Oxford Playhouse website
.
You are booking for the performance on Friday 24th April at 19:30.
Tickets are low cost, but the venue is small so early booking is recommended.

Title: Isle of Portland Climbing and Walking Weekend (3 nights)
Summary:
Join us for an exciting
three-night
weekend of climbing and walking on the tiny island of Portland, Dorset, well-known for its rugged beauty, long coastline, and incredible climbing. Our accommodation is located across the street from the beach, so a lovely setting in which to enjoy the summer!
David (
david209)
will lead the outdoor climbing events on Saturday and Sunday. Spaces are limited to 14 climbers on each day, including two leaders. It is a great opportunity for newer members and novice climbers to meet and learn from more experienced OutdoorLads.
Please sign-up to your preferred
climbing
events separately (below):
Saturday climbing
Sunday climbing
Peter (
MaccMan
) will lead the walking events on Saturday and Sunday.
Please sign up to your preferred
walking
events separately (below):
Saturday walk
- Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Sunday walk
- Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
The bunk rooms are simple, each with a small sink and private shower. Bedding and towels are provided. There is a spacious communal lounge and kitchen, as well as toilets, all on the first floor.
Please note
, this is a three-night hostel event from Friday evening to Monday morning to allow two full days of activities over the weekend.
Waitlist:
If an event is fully booked, please
add yourself to the waitlist.

cancel your attendance

Title: A Day of Climbing in Portland - Saturday (Beginners Welcome)
Summary:
Join
David
and
Neil
on the stunning island of Portland for some adrenaline-pumping sport climbing! This is a fantastic opportunity to get out on the famous limestone cliffs overlooking the English Channel. Whether you're a seasoned sport climber or have been leading indoors and want to venture on to some real rock, this event is perfect for you.
For those who haven't done sport climbing before, there will be a quick tutorial session at the start of the event, you just need to be competent at tying in, top rope belaying and have a pair of climbing shoes. If you've done three or more indoor climbing events in recent history, that's about all the experience you need. If you are unsure about the difficulty of the event, or whether you have necessary skills & experience, please message the event leader.
On this day, we will explore some of the best climbing areas that Portland has to offer. There will be a range of grades available to cater to all levels of experience, so if you haven't climbed outdoors before, just let the organiser know when you sign up so we can make sure we have a good mix of experiences.
The event is part of an OutdoorLads hostel weekend, with some members taking part in a walking event. After the day's climbing, we will return to the Bunker Hostel for a well-deserved rest and enjoy a friendly and sociable evening. Make sure that if you'd like to stay in the hostel, you
book the hostel event separately here.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
Photos:
Taken by OutdoorLads members and used with full permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Climbing requires a reasonable level of fitness and agility

Title: A Day of Climbing in Portland - Sunday (Beginners Welcome)
Summary:
Join
David
and
Neil
on the stunning island of Portland for some adrenaline-pumping sport climbing! This is a fantastic opportunity to get out on the famous limestone cliffs overlooking the English Channel. Whether you're a seasoned sport climber or have been leading indoors and want to venture on to some real rock, this event is perfect for you.
For those who haven't done sport climbing before, there will be a quick tutorial session at the start of the event, you just need to be competent at tying in, top rope belaying and have a pair of climbing shoes. If you've done three or more indoor climbing events in recent history, that's about all the experience you need. If you are unsure about the difficulty of the event, or whether you have necessary skills & experience, please message the event leader.
On this day, we will explore some of the best climbing areas that Portland has to offer. There will be a range of grades available to cater to all levels of experience, so if you haven't climbed outdoors before, just let the organiser know when you sign up so we can make sure we have a good mix of experiences.
The event is part of an Outdoorlads hostel weekend, with some members taking part in a walking event. After the day's climbing, we will return to the Bunker Hostel for a well-deserved rest and enjoy a friendly and sociable evening. Make sure that if you'd like to stay in the hostel, you
book the hostel event separately here.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
Photos:
Taken by OutdoorLads members and used with full permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Climbing requires a reasonable level of fitness and agility

Title: Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk near Dorchester that includes Maiden Castle, a length of the South Dorset Ridgeway and the Hardy Monument!
The route is 11.5 miles / 18.5 kilometres, with 430 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
Maiden Castle
is the largest and most impressive Iron Age hillfort in Britain, covering an area bigger than 50 football pitches.  Work on the complex defensive site began about 2,400 years ago and continued for 300 years.  When it was first built, the gleaming white chalk ramparts would have towered over the surrounding landscape.  At the hillfort’s peak of development, hundreds of people lived there in a settlement of thatched buildings. Many had central hearths for cooking, warmth and light.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route until a lower level path was created.  It now provides an alternative, higher level, route with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his novels.
The highpoint of today’s walk is the impressive
Hardy Monument
, built in 1844 in memory of Vice-Admiral Sir
Thomas Masterman Hardy
, Captain of HMS Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar.  Hardy is remembered because, amongst other things, it was in his arms that Nelson died, saying the immortal words ‘Kiss me, Hardy’.
The Hardy Monument is a stone tower, built on the summit of Black Down and designed to look like a spyglass, which Admiral Hardy would have used on board ship.  It is 22 metres / 72 feet high and may be open for us to climb its 120 steps when we pass by.  On a clear day you can see Devon and the the Isle of Wight, both of which are around 40 miles / 65 kilometres away, and to the north Pen Hill in the Mendip Hills, which is a similar distance away.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available closer to the event date.

Title: Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk along part of Chesil Beach, returning along part of the South Dorset Ridgeway!
The route is 9 miles / 15 kilometres, with 300 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
One of the most significant features along Dorset’s 95-mile
Jurassic Coast
is
Chesil Beach
.  The name Chesil is derived from the Old English
ceosel
or
cisel
, meaning gravel or shingle.  Over 18 miles / 29 kilometres long and up to 220 yards / 200 metres wide, the beach is estimated to be 100 million tonnes of pebbles.  It rises to 16 yards / 15 metres above sea level.  Thought to date back some 6,000 years, the beach is the result of onshore currents and rising sea levels.  It is moving inland at a rate of 6 yards / 5 metres per century.
Interestingly, the pebbles are naturally graded in size from fist-sized near Portland (at the eastern end of the beach) to pea-sized at Bridport (at the western end of the beach). The pebbles are mainly a mix of flint and chert.  The beach shelters the
Fleet
, which is Britain’s largest tidal lagoon.  The water is up to 3 metres deep. The Fleet is home to many wading birds and the
Abbotsbury Swannery
.  This is a managed colony of nesting swans, thought to have been established by monks at the former monastery of Abbotsbury.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route (the route around Portland was only created in 2003).  It now provides an alternative route to walking over the pebbles of Chesil Beach, with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his stories.
The walk starts and ends in the village of
Abbotsbury
, which is known for its picturesque historic buildings and thatched cottages.  It was the site of a monastery, which fell victim to King Henry VIII’s dissolution of monastic establishments in the mid-16th century.  There are some remains of the abbey, including a large tithe barn (built circa 1400) and St Catherine’s Chapel, a 14th century pilgrimage chapel that stands on a hill between the village and the coast.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available closer to the event date.

Title: Mid-Summer Camping on the Canal
Summary:
Instead of our traditional July cider pub camping, join ODL as we visit a canal-side pub, between Pewsey and Devizes.  This is a new location for us, on the banks of the Kennett and Avon Canal, and not far from the Alton Barnes White Horse.
There is live music Friday night; currently scheduled to appear are The Blacksmiths, a 5 piece Hampshire based rock & folk band.  Here is a
video
of their song
Cam Ye' O'er Frae France.
Hikes will be confirmed closer to the event but may include this 9.75 mile
Vale of Pewsey
loop, this 8.25 mile
White Horse and All Cannings
loop, or for a real leg-stretcher this 12.5 mile
Honeystreet to West Kennet
loop.
Should you wish to not cook, the pub has a full menu (available Friday until 20.00, Saturday from 12.00 to 14.30 and 17.30 to 20.30 only; 12.00 to 16.00 Sunday) including burgers, "light bites", salads and traditional pub favourites.  A full drinks menu is available throughout the day.
Some of the site rules are:
Quiet hours are between 23.00 and 07.00
Campfires and barbecues are allowed, but must be raised off the ground
Tents must be a minimum of 3 metres apart
Cars can be parked at the pitches
If this is your first event, don't be put off if you lack some of the necessary equipment; just message me on
chris.keivit@outdoorlads.com
or
james.couldry@outdoorlads.com
and we will see what can be worked out!
Dogs:
The Inn's camping area is dog-friendly although they must be kept on a lead at all times (you
must
advise
support@outdoorlads.com
to book your four-legged friend, there is no additional charge).
Caravans and motorhomes:
Are welcome, subject to site availability; if you need an EHU, there is an additional charge of £6 per night (no charge if no EHU is needed). You
must contact
support@outdoorlads.com
to reserve for campervans and caravans. Fire drums are available for hire, and fire wood is available for purchase.  I will confirm closer to the event if we are able to bring in our own wood.
Want to Get Out More?
Why not check out the South Wales and South West Calendar
here
.
Campfires & BBQ's
: This event will include a campfire/BBQ at the site.  Whilst our event leaders will take safety precautions there is still a risk to members. Please consider your behaviour around the campfire for your own and others safety.
Photos:
provided by Outdoorlads Members.
Difficulty & Distance: Canalside Camping

Title: Summer Solstice Weekend Camping in the Cotswolds
Summary:
A lovely campsite nestled below the Cotswold escarpment. It's on a fruit farm which also has an onsite cafe and shop. Nearby are a ruined abbey, medieval church frescoes, idylic Cotswold hamlets and villages (used for film/tv period dramas), grand country houses with landscape features, a heritage steam railway (also used for film/tv) and a pub we can walk to across the fields. There is a village store within two miles for any supplies you need.
If we are blessed with good weather there are stunning views down the Vale of Evesham looking out towards the Malverns and into Wales. We will see and hear the steam train whistling and puffing its way through the landscape.
We have two evenings on this idyllic site and Sunday 21st will be the Summer Solstice - the longest day of the year. Late chats around the fire-pit but please note the owners ask there be no noise after 11pm and that all firewood is purchased from the onsite shop.
Lots of walking opportunities from the campsite, almost entirely off road and whilst the escarpment itself is only about 200m we may ascend and descend this multiples times. Opportunities for a pub stop on the way back and/or an extended lunch break of your choice in a nearby picture-postcard-perfect town with cafes, pubs, delis and a supermarket. There are a couple of optional exciting possibilities for our main walk on Saturday - these will incur an extra cost to be paid direct to the venue. More info will be published here once I can make a reservation for us.
There are also excellent road cycling routes in the area including extended flatter sections and hill climbs if that is your thing but this is not officially part of the event and at your own risk/judgement.
Dogs:
Dogs are welcome on the campsite but are subject to an additional charge of £1 per night each paid directly to the campsite on arrival. Dogs will need to be kept under control throughout the event. You must let me know if you intend to bring your dog as we must pre-inform the owners.
Gazebos:
Please note this campsite makes an additional charge for Gazebos.   You are welcome to bring one but will need to pay the fee of £14 per night directly to the campsite on arrival. I will bring one big gazebo/canopy for our camp.
Want to Get Out More?
To be informed of all our South Wales events please click on this Telegram link
http://outdoorlads.events/SouthWalesNotifications
Photo credits:
All photos taken by OutdoorLads members and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: The Cotswold escarpement has a hieght of around 200m. We may ascend and descend multiple times. Walking routes involve crossing styles in fences and stone walls.

Title: London Bouldering Morning - All Abilities welcome!
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London!
If you have not been to one of the London Climbing Centres you will need to pre-register
HERE
. You only need to register once to use any of their 8 centres in London.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
First time boulderer?
If you have never tried bouldering before, the event leader can give you a short introduction to get you started and give a few a few pointers on technique. After which we will join in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Experienced boulderer?
Simply register with the wall and turn up on the day!
As above, all attending the event must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible for your own safety and so should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Difficulty & Distance: With climbs at every grade from complete beginner to seasoned pro, you decide how hard it gets!
