Title: Don't get "bowled" out at the Oval!
Summary:
Come spend the day at the beautiful Kia Oval, home of the Surrey County Cricket Club, and host to the very first Ashes match way back in 1882 (England lost that 3 day contest by 7 runs, by the way...)!
Today's battle won't have the historic implications of that match 144 years ago, but will pit county against county, as the home team Surrey Men welcomes Hampshire Men.  This is Day One of the four day
Rothesay County Championship
against Hampshire, and is expected to be a cracking good day of "
red ball
" cricket!  Fans are even allowed onto the pitch during the innings break.  Even if you know nothing about the game (runs are scored by the batters running back and forth between the wickets - 1 point for each successful "run", or by hitting the field boundary ropes - 4 points if the ball rolls to the ropes; 6 points if the ball goes over the ropes without touching the ground) the social aspect of the day is enjoyable.
Seating is all general admission, at a cost of £15 (tickets
must
be bought in advance) and tickets can be purchased
here
.  We will all meet at the Hobbs Gate entrance to The Oval, which is less than a 5 minute walk from Oval Northern Line station, and a 10 minute walk from Vauxhall station.
There is no ODL cost to join this event; you will only need whatever spending money you wish.  If the match is called due to inclement weather we may pop round to a local pub.
If the event page is full, please waitlist and I can add you!  I've indicated 10 attendees primarily so I can gauge interest but can always increase the numbers!
Photos are courtesy of
www.unsplash.com
and
www.outdoorlads.com
and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Sitting watching cricket all day!

Title: Fiskerton, Thurgarton, a Train and the Trent
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow and largely flat 11.5km / 7 mile walk through Nottinghamshire villages and along the River Trent.
This walk starts and finishes at two different railway stations with a stop for a late lunch at a well regarded riverside pub.
Thurgarton was once so important it was the name of an
ancient subdivision of Nottinghamshire.
Now it is a quiet rural village.
On the way we pass two lakes and a village cricket pitch - a match was well underway when the route was recently recced.  The river section is long and gentle, with traditional Trent white clapper gates. We are walking part of the Trent Valley Way.
It's a linear walk - you will either need to park near one of the stations or arrive by train. Please make sure you read the details available to logged-in users below.
If this is your first ODL event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
Image Credits:
All images provided by the walk leader and permission is granted to ODL for their use.
Difficulty & Distance: A 11.5km / 7mi walk with a total ascent of... 7m

Title: Birding Day in Lancashire and optional 5 mile walk
Summary:
Our day will be a perfect opportunity for anyone already a keen birder or an absolute beginner to explore the iconic birds and wildlife on one of Lancashire's prestigious reserves. We will hope to see and hear many common species up close, as well as birds including Marsh Harriers, Egrets, Bitterns, a variety of ducks and also the possibility of watching Otters and Red deer.
The afternoon will give a choice of further exploring the birdlife, or take the alternative option of a five mile walk with
Stephen
.
The latter 6 photos are for the walk
This 5 mile walk will be on the flat mainly with one steep climb near the beginning.
There will be a lot of woodlands on the walk. Passing Leighton Hall, a stone circle , Haweswater and walking through The Trough , the remain of an old quarry.
On our return we will reconvene with the other birders
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do
sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
**Cost to non – RSPB members is £10
to access the main reserve in the morning.
No dogs are allowed on the reserve.
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: Easy well maintained paths in the morning. More uneven paths in the afternoon.Morning – one mile. Afternoon - (birding option) two miles, (walk option) five miles.

Title: Wick, Upton Cheney and Bitton
Summary:
Join Andrew for this 9.5 mile walk covering the Golden Valley and villages of Wick, Upton Cheney, Swineford, North Stoke and Bitton. We will begin by in Swineford before ascending to North Stoke before continuing to Upton Cheney and Wick, where we will most likely eat lunch. We will then walk parts of the Monarch's Way to Bitton before returning to Swineford. There are some lovely views on this route. There will be an optional pub stop at the end of the walk.
All photos were taken by ODL members and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for future use.
Dogs are welcome to join us on this event but we do ask the following:
Please appreciate the fact that not everyone is a dog owner or lover- especially when we stop to eat
Please ensure you adhere to the Countryside Code at all times - see
(Keeping Dogs Under Effective Control)
If your dog is uncontrolled and strays in open land frightening other animals or livestock, the leader is supported by the OutdoorLads board of trustees to ask you to leave the event as this is not acceptable behaviour
We cannot guarantee that this route is dog friendly - there may be styles to carry your dog across.
Your dog needs to have the stamina to keep up with the group. This may be an issue on hot days

Title: BSC26: New Member Camp Area
Summary:
Our biggest annual event can appear a little overwhelming, but don't panic or be put off from joining us.
If you're new (or relatively new) to OutdoorLads or
BIG Spring Camp
and worried about not knowing anybody when you arrive, then worry not! This is a super friendly event, and through taking part in activities, volunteering, or joining in the fun on-site, it's so easy to get to know others at the event.
You can also join one of our smaller 'new member camps', led by some of our amazing, approachable volunteers, who'll help introduce you to others on the first evening. You'll camp together with other new members, and have use of an event shelter for socialising near your tent.
What to expect;
You will be camping within a small defined area with other newbies with a central gazebo.
A couple of our leaders will be camping with you, happy to help put up tents, answer any queries and be a welcoming face to OutdoorLads.
On arrival on the Friday, there will be welcoming drinks and nibbles within the gazebo.
You will have access to the gazebo throughout the weekend.
Though the leaders welcoming duties end on the Friday, the gazebo will become a focal point through out the weekend, with members often cooking and chatting throughout the weekend.
You will need to sign up for the
BIG Spring Camp 2026
event page first.
If you're new to OutdoorLads and fancy a stop off in Chester en-route, check out
this social event the night before
in Chester!
Photos provided by OutdoorLads Members and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: BSC is as easy or hard as you make it - plenty for everyone!

Title: BSC26: Caravan / Camper / Motorhome pitch
Summary:
This is a
supplement
for a pitch for a campervan, caravan or motorhome at
BIG Spring Camp 2026
.
You must have booked onto the main BIG Spring Camp event at the time of booking - if you have not done so, your campervan place will unfortunately be cancelled.
Unless you have a confirmed place on this page, please do not bring a camper van, motorhome or caravan to this event, as we will not be able to accommodate you.
The rugby club has reserved tarmac space to move onto if the weather is bad and the limit on numbers is also for insurance reasons.
Because this venue is a rugby club, and not primarily a campsite, there's no EHU or water supply at these pitches, and no waste disposal point on site (so it will all have to go back home with you).
Please note that the BIG Events Team (the small team of committed volunteers that run this event) are able to reserve their spaces here before bookings open, as it benefits us all if they have the best possible night's sleep!

Summary:

Title: Bridgewater Canal Spring Stroll: Nature, Heritage & Pizza
Summary:
Join Amir and the OutdoorLads for a relaxed spring walk along one of Greater Manchester’s most scenic canal corridors, combining peaceful waterside paths, fascinating industrial heritage, and a great Italian finish!
We’ll begin at the RHS Bridgewater car park, setting off south towards the historic Bridgewater Canal. Once we reach the canal, we’ll follow the tranquil towpath through the beautiful surroundings of Bridgewater Park, enjoying calm waters, wildlife, and open countryside views as we walk.
Continuing along the canal, we’ll pass the marina at Boothstown before heading further west towards Astley and Whitehall Meadows. Along the way we’ll discover reminders of the region’s rich mining heritage, including Lancashire Mining Museum and the impressive mining headgear tower, standing as proud landmarks of the area’s industrial past.
After exploring the area, we’ll retrace our steps along the canal and return to our starting point.
To round off the day, those who fancy it can join us for a drink or bite to eat at the lovely Stables Café in RHS Bridgewater complex. This relaxed Italian café serves excellent coffee, light drinks (including limited alcoholic options), and some very good pizzas if you’re feeling hungry after the walk.
Optional Visit: RHS Bridgewater Gardens
If you’d like to extend your day, you can also join us to explore the nearby RHS Bridgewater Gardens.
Early spring is a particularly lovely time to visit — flowers are starting to bloom, the gardens are vibrant, and pollen levels are still relatively low in the air.
You can book your visit here:
Book RHS Bridgewater tickets online
Booking online is recommended as it’s cheaper than buying tickets at the entrance and guarantees your entry slot.
Ticket Prices
Adult (online pre-booked): from £16.80
Adult (at the gate): £17.80
Dogs
Well-behaved and socialised dogs are welcome on this walk.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
If you’re planning to visit RHS Bridgewater Gardens after the walk, it’s worth noting their dog policy.
For the protection of the plant collections and visitor experience, dogs are generally not allowed inside RHS Bridgewater Gardens. The only exception is registered guide, assistance or support dogs, which are always welcome.
Your First OutdoorLads Walk Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page.
Waitlist
: If this event is fully booked and you want to attend, just add yourself to the waitlist, because places inevitably become available closer to the date.
Photos by Amir and used with their permission.
Difficulty & Distance: 5.6 miles. Mostly flat canal towpaths and park trails

Title: A Wilde Time In Reading @ The Picture of Dorian Gray
Summary:
Join us for a cultural afternoon supporting a local independent theatre for a production of one of Oscar Wilde's famous novels. Reading is synonymous with Oscar Wilde who was imprisoned at Reading Jail (hence the poem the Ballad Of Reading Gaol) after being convicted for gross indecency with other men in 1897, and served a 2 year sentence.
Tickets:
As this is a local production it is a very affordable event with general tickets just £16.55 (and concessions are available).
Please note that you are responsible for buying your own tickets in advance, which you can do by clicking
here
ensuring that you select the Saturday afternoon production. Tickets are on a first come-first serve basis, you can chose you own seats (we won't be able to sit together but it's a very small venue) and as this is the last night of the production I would suggest that you purchase asap.
Picture Of Dorian Gray:
This is the wording from the Progress Theatre's own page:
'The Picture of Dorian Gray tells the story of a beautiful young man who makes a Faustian bargain: offering his soul for his portrait to age and bear the toll of his sins, while he remains eternally youthful.
In this inclusive production we witness Dorian’s descent into decadence, corruption and depravity. Yet he remains unblemished whilst his portrait manifests his age and degradation.
Our cast and crew are drawn from diverse parts of our community, adding richness and meaning to this vibrant adaptation of Oscar Wilde’s groundbreaking work and respecting and incorporating Wilde’s allusions to gay relationships within the work'.
Reading's Progress Theatre:
Located near the Reading University Campus, the theatre is the oldest in Reading, opening in 1946, is self funded and run by volunteers. With just 96 seats it's an intimate venue and prides itself as an accessible and inclusive space.
Difficulty & Distance: N/A

Title: April London Social at The Rising SE1
Summary:
Our monthly London pub socials are the perfect gateway to Outdoor Lads and our busy diary of brilliant events. For April, we're returning to The Rising in Elephant & Castle!
If you’re feeling hungry and fancy a bite, The Rising partners with Yard Sale Pizza, so you can order delicious pizzas to enjoy on the premises. They’ll even provide plates and cutlery.
Typically our socials have a rich mix of regulars, occasionals, and newbies, not only from London and the South East, but dropping in from across the country.
Whether it's hiking, camping, cycling, climbing, or a range of other activities - we do it!  Outdoors is our focus, but our socials are the chance to catch up with lads you've already met, find out about the group, and meet new members and event leaders.
Look forward to seeing you!
Is this your first ODL event?

Title: 1000m Malvern Circuit Challenge
Summary:
This is one of the toughest walks in the hill walks category, with as much ascent as a mountain like Snowdon, and incredible views across swathes of England. The scenery is breath-taking, and the flora, geology and traditions fascinating - we will touch on those along the way.
The full walk is a circuit of 14K including 1000m of ascent. The first half of this goes to Wyche. If you wish to do half of the walk (which is 6K and 600m ascent) you may leave here and take a bus back to the car park (details below).
The challenge begins with a steep ascent above North Quarry and onto
North Hill
, followed by a steep descent into Green Valley. After this warm up, a gentler panorama around the eastern side of the hills gives us time to see some of the features of the plants and rock to be found. Note the pink granite in places, and the Penny Wort clinging to cracks.
Next, it’s a steep climb right to the top of
Worcestershire Beacon
, at 425m the highest point on the Malvern Hills. If visibility is good, we’ll have a go at recognising hills on the horizon. It is said even Exmoor is visible on a clear day.
After this we walk alongside Shire Ditch, one of many ancient earthworks on the hills dating back over 2000 years. A steep descent follows, zig-zagging to a disused quarry and clear, still lake.
Malvern quarries
provided excellent hard road stone, and this quarry was even searched for gold, but did not yield enough for commercial mining. Shales and silts here contain fossils 435 million years old. The rocks are very hard and good for use in construction.
After a second quarry, there is a scrambly slope up to re-join the ridge line. We descend to the cutting at Wyche where there are toilets and a shelter, if needed, for our lunch stop. You’ve now covered just over half way.
Continuing, we make a circuit of
Perseverance Hill,
then follow the ridge line northwards. After a mile or so, we descend westwards to find a
spout
, one of the most popular collecting points for the Malvern waters, reputed to have healing properties. It’s then an ascent back to Worcestershire Beacon, for our second visit to the highest point during the day.
The authors
Tolkien and C.S. Lewis
were good friends and frequented the Malvern Hills together. The gas lamps in Great Malvern are a reminder of Narnia. As Tolkien walked the same paths as you are now taking, he imagined the White Mountains of Gondor and other scenes famous in his Lord of the Rings trilogy.
We now complete the ridge northwards over
Sugar loaf hill, Table Hill and End Hill
, descending down a zigzag path to the Clock Tower where we started.
Check your progress with these stages:
Location                               Covered               To go
North Hill                             1.16K 237m        12.44K 803m
Green Valley                       1.79K 237m        11.81K 803m
Worcestershire Beacon  3.48K 484m       10.12K 556m
Quarry lake                         4.52K 488m       9.08K 552m
Wyche Toilets                     5.90K 601m       7.70K 439m
Perseverance Hill              6.79K 658m       6.81K 382m
Wyche cutting                    7.76K 682m       5.84K 358m
Spout                                    9.43K 802m      4.17K 238m
Worcestershire Beacon  10.4K 952m        3.20K 88m
Finish                                   13.6K 1040m
Follow the
route
on OS Maps.
At the finish you will be awarded a Champion’s Medal for taking part in this demanding and rewarding circuit. You can rightly feel proud of your achievement in completing one of the very best hill walks in Britain, and have a well deserved drink and meal having burned off around 3000 calories over the walk!
If you wish to do half the walk, you can come as far as Wyche, from where
Bus 675
(depart 1333  or 1542) takes you back to the car park. Pay contactless.
Difficulty & Distance: 14K with 1000m of height gain and brisk pace

Title: Welsh boathouse three-night adventure
Summary:
Join us for an unforgettable long weekend retreat (three nights!) in a remote and idyllic coastal hostel in Ceredigion at the stunning Ty Cwch. Nestled in the picturesque cove of Cwmtydu, this unique accommodation offers breathtaking ocean views, glorious sunsets, and dark skies perfect for stargazing. Cwmtydu is on one of the most spectacular stretches on the Ceredigion Heritage Coast, with picturesque towns and villages, a wealth of wildlife, and a large variety of plants and insects.
Cardigan Bay has the largest population of bottlenose dolphins in Europe, seals come on to Cwmtydu beach to have their pups, there’s an abundance of sea birds nesting on the cliffs, (lesser black backed gulls, oyster catchers, guillemots, razorbills, kittiwakes, fulmers, shags, and peregrine falcons), and the next valley is home to one of the few sites for the pearl-bordered fritillary butterfly in Wales.
Expect plenty of time for clifftop walks, coastal exploration, and enjoying the company of your fellow OutdoorLads in this truly magical setting. Don’t miss out on this one-of-a-kind retreat!
We’ll have two and a half days to explore the stunning coastline and relax in this unique boathouse made from repurposed shipping containers for a perfect fun nautical theme. With fully insulated and heated living spaces and outdoor infra-red heaters under canopy, its an ideal for enjoying an evening sun-downer while soaking in the beauty of the Welsh seascape. Nemo will be mixing up some optional sea-themed cocktails too!
Feast with Nemo on a weekend of fabulous food including a barbecue, delightful desserts and fun entertainment.
With its incredible location and a touch of ‘Nemo’ magic, this promises to be an extra special summer 2026 getaway. Don’t miss out—sign up now for a long weekend of adventure, relaxation, and memorable moments on the Welsh Coast!
Dogs:

Title: Cruise the Gt Orme on the Waverley Steamer
Summary:
The Waverley
is the last sea going paddle steamer in the world.  Built on The Clyde in 1947 to replace her name-sake that was sunk off Dunkirk in WW2, she now tours the UK bringing delight to all who sail on her. A trip on the Waverley is an unforgettable experience, not just for the coastal views - look out for Puffins, Seals and other widlife, but for the mesmerising engine room with public viewing galleries.  The ship is powered by an oil fired triple expansion steam engine that drives the paddles on the port and starboard beams.
The ship has bars, cafes restaurants and plenty of indoor and outdoor seating.  The lack of vibration from propeller propulsion and Diesel engines makes the experience all the nicer. It is licensed to carry around 800 passengers - there's plenty of space, and undergoes a major re-fit in dry dock every year,
Come on board, sit back with a beer and soak up the scenery as she glides around the N Wales coastline.
The Waverley has been used previously for transport to multiple Outdoorlads walking events on the Isle of Wight, and the event leader has travelled on it many times, so if you have any questions drop him a line, or refer to the company website FAQs.
IMPORTANT: OUTDOORLADS DOES NOT BUY TICKETS FOR THE WAVERLEY,
but you need to purchase your own ticket at this link
Waverley Cruises
Tickets are £45 pp. (Dogs £5). Further info in the meeting up section.
These afternoon cruises usually sell out, so if you want to go, book now.
ONCE YOU HAVE BOUGHT A TICKET, To say that you are coming, please register on this event,
If there are any spare tickets on the day, these are sold on board.
Tickets are collected when you disembark.
If the trip is found to be sold out, registration on the event will be closed, so once you have a ticket please book a place here.  There is no Outdoorlads fee for Social and cultural events.

Title: Coastal Suffolk Adventure: Boat Trip & Peninsula Walk
Summary:
Consider this maybe as more of a day out rather than just another walk!
Come join our trip from Essex to Suffolk and back, including a return boat trip* to a lovely coastal peninsula, around which we will do a 10.6 mile (17km) walk. Much of it is through part of the Suffolk and Essex Coast Heaths national landscape and we'll be taking in two estuary rivers.
(*Please note there is a charge of £15 per person payable for this event to cover the cost of the return boat trip needed to get to and from the Peninsula. The usual non-subscribed walk event fee of £12.50 will not be charged in addition for this event)
Our day will commence initially on the Essex coast in the historic maritime port town of Harwich. Originating in the 13th Century, this is now the second busiest ferry and freight port in the UK. We however, will be embarking on a very much smaller craft (as pictured) from the quiet little Harwich Half Penny Pier Harbour for a 10-minute trip across the water to the Suffolk coastal Shotley Peninsula.
From the attractive Shotley Point Marina, we'll then commence our circular walk. Initially heading along the harbourside and onto the outskirts of the small town of Shotley Gate, we'll then follow the banks of the Estuary River Stour. We'll follow the path as it meanders along the estuary banks until we head off across some lovely open Suffolk countryside across to the other side of the peninsula.
We'll then arrive at the small village of Chelmondiston - from where we'll head through some scenic low undulating woodland to get to the very pretty and rural estuary hamlet of Pin Mill. Aside from being a very cute place, Pin Mill is known best for its pub on the little quayside: The Butt and Oyster.
Of course, we'll make a stop here to take in quayside views and some liquid refreshment. The pub is quite small and very popular for food, so eating there wouldn't be practical for us sadly, but we'll find somewhere in the vicinity to also enjoy our packed lunch.
Once refuelled, we'll make out way through the beautiful area known as the Cliff Plantation which, as the name suggests, is an old plantation woodland on some low-level cliffs alongside the Estuary River Orwell. Through the trees, there are glimpses of the water and some interesting wooden houseboats as we walk along the riverside path.
The continuing estuary side route then takes us on some boardwalks and paths with wide open views of the Estuary River Orwell. As we get closer back to our start point, there are impressive views of the busy Felixstowe Shipping Port across the other side of estuary, creating a unique mix of the rural path we'll be on with the industrial container port across the water.
As we arrive back at Shotley Point, we’ll wander through much more of the marina this time with great views of the boats and harbour activity.
Depending on timings, we should then have time for another refreshment stop at a bar in the marina before we catch our return boat trip back across to Harwich.
*Note - the high-level view photos of the Marina and the Butt and Oyster Pub setting are drone shots. But nevertheless, the views we'll get on the walk are lovely!
(Please note that the boat crossing is subject to weather conditions.  Should the boat company need to cancel crossings I will message attendees around 48 hours before to offer an alternative linear verison of the walk with a different start location)

Title: Spring bluebells in Essex
Summary:
Join me on this 12km/7.5-mile circular walk, to hopefully see a carpet of bluebells, plus other sites and delights in North Essex.
We will start the walk in the village of West Bergholt close to Colchester. From the village, we will head towards Hillhouse, a gorgeous Georgian home, and the church of Old St Mary. From here, we can visit Hillhouse Wood. Managed by the Woodland Trust It is a hidden gem to see a carpet of bluebells and a large patch of wild garlic and other wild flowers. After visiting the woods, we will head outbound into the countryside towards and through the village of Fordam, round and then back to the start following a section of the River Colne. Along the way, we may see a few farm animals. We'll finish with an obligatory pub stop at the Queen's Head for those who want to join.
Is it dog friendly?
Yes, this walk should be suitable for dogs. There are a few horse fields we will head pass, so please keep your dog on the lead for that section.
Don't waste that space!
Spring always means lots of new and existing members excited to be getting back out more. Please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else

Title: Railway Sleepers to Castle Keepers
Summary:
Longer days mean time for a slightly longer walk. This reasonably flat journey sees us starting at Christ's Hospital and following a disused railway line to West Grinstead. From there we turn off and work our way towards Knepp Castle ruins and estate - a massive site with lots to take in. The final leg will be working our way through a mixture of terrain until we end up back where it all started. Our walk will offer glimpses in to the past and a decent amount of variety.
Click on this
Link
to view the route (opens OS Maps)
A Few Features:
Christ's Hospital School
We think of Henry VIII as a bit of a tyrant. The founding of this school back in 1552 shows us his nice side. He wanted the disadvantaged to have an equal chance at trying to achieve in education. To this day the school is unique in being one of very few where most students have received a bursary. The grounds are vast and and it still has links to other prestigious sites around the country.
Christ's Hospital Station
Sadly, this station was never able to achieve it's potential. A number of platforms and junctions were built to cater for the school. The planners envisaged pupils arriving each day by train. However, the school opted to be a boarding school only so making the station largely pointless. The Beeching cuts in the 1960s pretty much decimated the area and the station was reduced to two simple up and down platforms.
Downs Link
Originally Christ's Hospital Station would have had a line towards Arundel, one to Shoreham and one to Guildford. When the later two were closed during the Beeching cuts the old track bed became footpaths. It is now a decent flat path for walkers and cyclists linking Guildford in the north with Shoreham on the coast. There has been talk of reopening the line from Horsham via Christ's Hospital to Guildford, but nothing has progressed past the "talking" stage. On our walk we will pass through one old station called West Grinstead. This still has a platform, a signal and an old train carriage.
West Grinstead Church
As with many churches in the area this one dates from the 11th century. Various bits have been added over the years but it offers a fascinating glimpse in to the past.
Knepp Castle and Estate
The original Knepp Castle was built in 1125 and has links to William the Conqueror. The site has an interesting past with typical treachery. One king visited the site to see his friend. He then went away and thought about things and decided actually he'd like the castle for himself. So he returned and killed his good friend and starved his wife to death! Only a small part of the castle remains but the fact any of it is still there is an achievement in itself. A new 'modern' castle has been built further on the large estate and this now houses the landowners. They have instigated a massive plan to really get back to nature. There are efforts to replant the area and everything is wildlife focused.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks. I just ask that owners please ensure they are kept on a lead as appropriate, especially around livestock and when near busy roads. Please, also remember that not everyone is a dog lover.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.

Title: Ardrossan to Portencross Castle
Summary:
Ayrshire Coastal Path: Ardrossan to Portencross.
6.5 miles plus 2 miles to West Kilbride Station.
https://portencrosscastle.org.uk/
An easy walk between the town of Ardrossan and the small hamlet of Portencross, where a 14th century castle awaits your arrival! All on the flat and a mix of surfaced paths, grassy tracks and sandy beach. Some muddy parts on the approach to the Castle.
There is on-street parking on
Montgomerie Street, Ardrossan
and a public car park at
Portencross
(KA23 9QA). No public transport to Portencross. There are train services between Ardrossan and West Kilbride which is 2.3 miles away from Portencross, making the walk closer to 9 miles. Local bus services also stop on
A78 near Overton Drive
, which is 1.7 miles from Portencross.
Dogs welecome, under control and on the lead where livestock are encountered.

Title: London Ten Pin Bowling with Optional Pub Social 🎳😃
Summary:
GET SOCIAL WITH OUTDOORLADS! Great for new and regular members!
If the event is full, please
add yourself to the waitlist
, and we will amend our booking with Rowan's if availability permits.
Ten pin bowling and optional pub social which will now take place on a regular monthly basis, as the 1st event was so well received by the attendees. All are welcome on this event, whether you’ve bowled before or you’re a complete beginner. If you’ve never bowled before come along and have some Fun! I’m happy to show/demonstrate how I bowl. Pretty average!?
Come join me and the other guys for an evening of ten pin bowling fun in north London.  We have a group booking for 19.00 Friday night at Rowan's Ten Pin Bowl in Finsbury Park. All Welcome! Please arrive and check in by 18.45 so we have time to get all set up! We have two games per person booked.
PLEASE NOTE:- There is an admission fee to Rowan’s (Not included in the event charge). This is payable upon entry.
FRIDAY 10:30am - 7:00pm     £1.00.                                                                                  7:00pm - close       £5.00
THE T&C’S OF ROWAN’S ALSO REQUIRE PHOTO ID ON FRIDAY & SATURDAY EVENINGS. (Obviously probably dependent on what age you appear to be).
You DO NOT need to book with Rowan's. I will make the booking prior to event once appropriate attendee numbers are known.
The cost is £20.40 (Fridays) per player for 2 games of ten pin bowling.

Title: Durham Pride - Saturday Social
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for our first social event at Durham Pride
Please note that there is an entrance charge. This currently starts at £9.38, including a £1.38 booking fee. Tickets can be purchased via

Come and join us for a few drinks, a natter, hopefully make a few new friends and catch up with people you have met before. In addition to having a drink and a bite to eat (if you want), we’ll enjoy the performances. You will also be able to find out about forthcoming ODL events.
If you’re not familiar with Durham, it’s a wonderful city to explore on foot. Make sure you include the cathedral. The Pride march starts there and winds down through the town to The Sands, where the Pride party takes place.
So come and join OutdoorLads in Durham for a fabulous afternoon!

The venue is dog-friendly. I will not be bringing my dog, Finn, as he’s terrible at sitting still

Title: SE500: Wandering the Wonders West of Wycombe & Whereabouts
Summary:
Summary:
This is an
SE500 walk
, meaning the route includes over
500 metres of total ascent
, providing a proper leg stretch with four notable climbs. The full circuit will burn in excess of
2,200 calories
.
Starting from
Saunderton railway station
at 11am (due to the train times), convenient for those travelling by public transport, this rewarding
Chilterns
walk explores the
Bradenham and Hughenden Valley
area. Highlights include the picturesque village of
Bradenham
, known for its cricket green and historic cottages, the wooded landscape of
Hughenden Valley
, and the grounds of
Hughenden Manor
, the former home of
Benjamin Disraeli
.
The route also passes through the historic village of
West Wycombe
, noted for its Georgian architecture and distinctive hillside landmarks.
The Sites:
Bradenham
Bradenham is a small and picturesque village in the Chiltern Hills, centred around a broad village green lined with mature trees and period cottages. Much of the village is owned and carefully managed by the
National Trust
, helping to preserve its unspoilt rural character. With its historic parish church, traditional cricket pitch and surrounding beech woodland, Bradenham retains a distinctly tranquil and quintessentially English atmosphere.
Hughenden Valley
Hughenden Valley is a wide, wooded valley typical of the Chiltern landscape, characterised by rolling pasture, mature beech woodland and gently rising chalk slopes. The valley maintains a peaceful rural character, with scattered farms and small settlements linked by quiet lanes and footpaths. Its open landscapes and far-reaching views make it one of the defining features of the surrounding countryside.
Hughenden Manor
Hughenden Manor is a Victorian country house set within landscaped parkland in the Chiltern Hills. It was the home of
Benjamin Disraeli
, who served twice as British Prime Minister in the nineteenth century. Now managed by the
National Trust
, the house combines period interiors with exhibitions exploring Disraeli’s life and legacy, set within extensive gardens and woodland. Although the walk will not include a visit inside the house, the route passes the façade, church and café, and continues through the freely accessible grounds.
West Wycombe
West Wycombe is a historic Chilterns village renowned for its elegant Georgian architecture and striking hillside landmarks. Much of the village was remodelled in the eighteenth century by
Sir Francis Dashwood
, giving it a distinctive and cohesive character. It is also home to
West Wycombe Park
and
St Lawrence's Church
, which crowns the hill above the village. Today it remains one of the best-preserved examples of planned Georgian village design in the Chiltern countryside.
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, there are a few busy roads to cross, stiles on route and quiet lane sections and crossings but generally the going is suitable for dogs to be off the lead for much of the time. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
The Route:
Starting from
Saunderton railway station
, the walk begins immediately with the first of four steady climbs, rising onto open hillsides with expansive views across the surrounding valley. The route then descends into
Bradenham
, passing the village green and historic cottages before climbing again through
Naphill Common
.
From the top of the common, the route descends into
Hughenden Valley
, later passing through the village of the same name before tackling a steeper climb on the opposite side of the valley towards
Cryers Hill
. The route then follows higher ground along the valley edge before descending to
Hughenden Manor
, the former home of
Benjamin Disraeli
.
Although the house itself will not be visited, lunch will be taken in the grounds before continuing through the estate towards
Downley
. The route crosses
Downley Common
and continues through farmland before descending into
West Wycombe
.
A short detour along the village high street allows time for refreshments at a pub or tea room before the final steep climb to the
Dashwood Mausoleum
, which offers fine views across the surrounding countryside towards
West Wycombe Park
.
The final 2.6 miles follow a gently undulating ridge with views back towards
Bradenham
, before the route descends back into the valley and returns to
Saunderton railway station
.
The route can be found
here
:
All photos the leader's own, with permission given to OutdoorLads for use.
Difficulty & Distance: 13.5 miles with 550 meters of ascent and terrain of dirt and tarmac

Title: High Cup Nick - England's Grand Canyon
Summary:
A 10.5 mile circular walk taking in the most dramatic feature of the entire Pennine Range - High Cup Nick, England's Grand Canyon.
Leaving the pretty village of Dufton, we climb up into a steep sided valley with evidence of former mining all around, with some ruins to explore.
Continuing up towards the top of the Pennines, we soon reach the watershed, where one stream heads to the Irish Sea, and the next takes the much longer journey, via the Tees, to the North Sea.
Walking across boggy terrain, following Rundale Tarn as it begins its journey to the North Sea, we head slowly downstream, with views over wild moorland terrain. In a couple of miles, we reach the highlight - High Cup Nick.
A deep chasm on the Pennine fellside, this famous nick, a dramatic geological formation at the top of High Cup Gill is part of the well-known Whin Sill, and overlooks the best glaciated valley in Northern England.
Here you can see the grey-blue dolerite crags which also form High Force and Cauldron Snout. It is a breathtaking sight. We will pause a while to take in the views.
We then head down, at first by the edge of High Cup, returning to Dufton for a drink in the village pub.
Image / photo credits:  The leader's and another leader's images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.

Title: York Pride 2026 - Join or follow the City Parade
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at York Pride!
Pride day gets off to a fabulous start with the Parade March from Parliament Street through the streets of York City Centre to the Festival site on the Knavesmire.
Join Ru and Steve to celebrate diversity and inclusion as we march through the vibrant streets of York for one of the North’s most colourful and welcoming Pride events. Whether you're a regular or new to OutdoorLads, come along, show your pride, and enjoy a fun day of community, visibility, and adventure. All are welcome—let’s make it a day to remember! York Pride Parade is one of the longest in the country at 2.4 miles, and takes around 60-90 minutes to complete, so please wear appropriate comfortable footwear.
The parade ends at the Pride Festival site on The Knavesmire, where you will be free to do what you want.
After we meet on parliament Street, you can join us in the parade, or watch and follow down to The Knavesmire.
There will be live entertainment on the Main Stage, a Cabaret or second music stage. There are usually numerous local acts and some local celebrity to headline with going on until about 7.00pm.
Pride Market stalls, plenty of food and drink vendors and even a fun fair. You can also bring your own food and drink if you want to make a picnic of it. It is a free event, but you can support it by buying a wristband that gets you discount entry to some bars and discounts with some shops.
After the festival ends at the Knavesmire it's just 10-15 minutes walk back to York station.
There are usually a couple of after-parties in the town centre and even a club night if you're up to it. (TBC nearer the date ).
There is potential for an evening social, to be confirmed nearer the date, which we'll add as a separate event.
More information about York pride can be found on  the
York price website
and Facebook pages.
Image credits: all images provided by OutdoorLads members. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Either the gentle walk with the parade, or walk from York city centre to the Knavesmire appx 2.5 miles

Title: The Pre-BIG Spring Camp Halfway House Warm-up (Chester)
Summary:
Whether you’re brand new to OutdoorLads, passing through Chester on your way to bigger adventures, or just fancy a relaxed evening out then this one’s for you.
This event is primarily intended for new(ish) members and passersby on their way to
BIG Spring Camp
. However, if there are still spare spaces, we may be able to open to a wider, local veteran audience nearer the time.
We’ve bagged a table at the brilliant
That Beer Place
in Chester - an independent craft beer and cider taproom that’s a little bit different from your average night out - for an informal get-together the evening before BIG Spring Camp kicks off. They have wide range of beverages, including non-alcoholic options, as well as a new, bespoke food menu.
It’s a chance for new faces to meet a friendly bunch in a smaller, low-key setting before diving into anything bigger, and for anyone travelling up from further afield to break their journey, grab some great drinks and good food, and put some faces to names before the weekend.
New to OutdoorLads?
You’ll be very welcome here. This is exactly the kind of event designed with you in mind - a relaxed evening where you can find your feet and discover just how friendly this lot are.
Heading to the BIG Spring Camp?
Come early, stop off in Chester, and start your weekend here.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list.  Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
Photos used by kind permission of That Beer Place

Title: Nottingham to Beeston Lock Circular Walk
Summary:
The Nottingham & Beeston Canal
was a vital part of Nottingham's industrial history. Built in the late 18th century, it connected the city to the River Trent at Beeston, facilitating the transport of goods, particularly coal, from the Nottinghamshire coalfields.
The canal played a significant role in the region's economic growth for many years. However, with the advent of railways in the 19th century, its importance gradually declined. Today, while much of the canal has been filled in, sections remain, and the canal's legacy can still be seen in the city's landscape.
The Beeston Lock and Canal is a particularly picturesque spot, offering a glimpse into the canal's past. You can explore the area, admire the historic lock, and learn about the canal's history at the Canalside Heritage Centre.
Victoria Embankment
is a grand, landscaped park alongside the mighty
River Trent
in Nottingham. This historical public space was built at the turn of the 20th century with a Grade-II listed bandstand still in use today.
At the end of the walk we will go for a drink a nearby pub.
Dogs are welcome
but owners must ensure they are kept under control at all times.
Waitlist:
If this event is fully booked and you really want to attend, add yourself to the waitlist because places often become available. If you are on the list but can no longer come, please cancel your attendance as soon as possible to allow waitlisted people to attend in your place.
Image Credits:

Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 16 km (10 miles) with 25m (84 feet) of ascent. Approximately 5 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: Adlington to Parbold canal walk (LLC#6)
Summary:
This is a linear walk from Adlington to Parbold along a stretch of the 204km (127.25 miles) Leeds & Liverpool Canal, blending industrial heritage with peaceful countryside.
This is walk
stage 6 of 8
spanning the full length of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
The canal eases us out of Adlington with quiet water, and old stone bridges. The occasional narrowboat drifts by, setting a relaxed pace as the countryside begins to open up.
White Bear Marina adds a brief burst of life—moored boats, gentle clatter, and a sense of heritage—before the path settles again into calm stretches of reeds and hedgerows. As Parbold Hill appears, the canal curves toward the village, where stone cottages and the old windmill give the walk a warm, welcoming finish.
We'll return by train (for those that need to get back to Adlington) - however the best option will depend on your individual onward travel plans.
Stage 1: Leeds to Bingley - 30 Aug 2025
Stage 2: Bingley to Gargrave - 20 Sep 2025
Stage 3: Gargrave to Burnley - 21st Feb 2026
Stage 4: Burnley to Blackburn - 21st March 2026
Stage 5: Blackburn to Adlington - 11th April 2026
Stage 6: Adlington to Parbold - 9th May 2026
Stage 7: Parbold to Maghull - 6th June 2026
Stage 8: Maghull to Liverpool - 27th June 2026
Dogs:
No dogs on this leg of the walk due to the length of walk and narrow towpaths which will be busy with cyclists and other walkers.
If the event is fully booked:
Please
join the wait list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
If this is your first OutdoorLads walk event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.

Title: Shell Island Summer Camp
Summary:
Join us as we revisit one of the most popular camp events on the OutdoorLads calendar !
Nestled along the breathtaking coastline of North Wales, between the charming towns of Harlech and Barmouth, Shell Island offers a truly special escape into nature. With sweeping views across Cardigan Bay and the dramatic peaks of Eryri National Park, it’s a place where sea, sky, and mountains come together in perfect harmony.
Known locally as
Mochras
, Shell Island has a fascinating past. What we now experience as a peaceful peninsula was once part of the mainland landscape, home to a small historic settlement. In the early 19th century, a major engineering project altered the course of the nearby River Artro. By redirecting the river to flow north of the land rather than south, it gradually carved out the distinctive peninsula we see today—effectively setting Shell Island apart and shaping its unique identity. Traces of its past still linger, giving the area a quiet sense of history beneath its natural beauty.
True to its name, Shell Island is renowned for the extraordinary variety of shells that wash ashore. The powerful tides of the Irish Sea bring in an ever-changing treasure trove, making every walk along the beach a chance for discovery. Whether you’re a casual beachcomber or simply enjoy the rhythm of the waves, the shoreline here is endlessly captivating.
Beyond the island, there’s no shortage of places to explore. The impressive Harlech Castle stands as a striking reminder of Wales’ medieval past, while from nearby Porthmadog you can step aboard the historic Ffestiniog Railway or Welsh Highland Railway—both offering unforgettable journeys through spectacular landscapes, inspired by the elegance of Italianate design.
For those who love the outdoors, the possibilities are wide open. Wander along miles of golden sands, paddle in the refreshing sea, or venture inland for scenic walks into the hills and valleys of Eryri and the surrounding countryside. Whether you prefer a gentle stroll or a more adventurous hike, there’s something here for every pace and mood. Plans can be shaped together on Friday evening—so you’re free to do as much or as little as you wish.
And as the day draws to a close, Saturday evening brings a simple but memorable pleasure: gathering together on the beach for a fire, sharing stories under the open sky, with the sound of the waves as your backdrop.
Dogs
Well behaved dogs are welcome, but must be
kept on a
lead at all times
and owners must clean up after them.
Please note that wardens patrol the grounds regularly throughout the day and night and make strict checks regarding this.
XL Bully dogs and dogs listed in the Dangerous Dogs Act (1991) are not permitted, even if registered.
There is no additional charge for dogs on this event.
Caravans
This site is not suitable for caravans, but motorhomes and campervans are accepted. There are no extra charges as part of this event for motorhomes or campervans.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.

Booking for this event closes on Monday 8th June 2026 - 12 noon.

Title: Bristol Board Games Sunday Social
Summary:
Join us for a Sunday afternoon socialising and playing games
Time to bring out your competitive side with a fun afternoon socialising in Bristol's lovely Chance & Counters games cafe.  If you're new to Outdoorlads it's a great way to hang out with and get to know other members but in a cosy indoor setting. We're a friendly bunch and it's always a good laugh.
I've booked a table at 1pm for four hours. The cafe has over 850 new and old games to choose from so there should be something for everyone (although I could quite happily play UNO all day). To keep your game-playing energy up the café serves food, soft drinks and alcohol. We usually have lunch there and then start playing (but there's no obligation to eat, if you don't want to).
Important -
Payment required
There's a £14 gaming fee for each attendee. Annoyingly the cafe has changed their pricing policy and now I've had to pay upfront. If you sign up please can you send me the £14 asap via paypal using
this paypal link
, or I can send you my bank details. (If you cancel before the booking closing date you'll get your money back).

Title: An Amble to Amberley to Gaze at Gardens
Summary:
'Dear Amberley', as the village's website represents it and visitors fondly remember it, is a delightful hodge-podge of architectural styles and building materials perched on a ridge above the River Arun. Flowers cascade down its ancient walls, the modern age passes it by, the traditional rhythms of rural life beat on, and long, loose, pretty perspectives of cottages line a quadrilateral of lanes, one of which goes to the 12th-Century church and the 14th-Century castle. These quiet lanes will be busier on Sunday 14th June when the biennial opening of the village's gardens happens (see the event's website
here
). £8 gains entry to many gardens of different sizes and styles with the proceeds going to charity.
The plan:
There will be no set plan for visiting the gardens and we won't go round in a big group: you're welcome to go wherever and with whomever you choose. You're also welcome to leave when you want, but there will be an agreed meeting place and time should you wish to depart as part of a group. You don't even need to book on to this event to see the gardens of course; we'll just happily just bump into you in the village! If you do come by train (or car but want a little more exercise), Amberley Railway Station is some way outside of the village, so we'll have a pleasant short walk along the River Arun to get there. Please see the route
here
.
Dogs:
This event is less suitable for dogs as they are not permitted in the gardens, but the walk to the village along the river would be highly suitable if they could be looked after by someone at each of the gardens.

Title: A Bimble to Bramshott to Gaze at Gardens
Summary:
Scattered among the sunken lanes, flowery meadows and mixed woodland of East Hampshire is the ancient village of Bramshott. The claim that it is England's most haunted village (a total of 27 ghosts including a cat, a pig, a grey lady, a horseman, a milkmaid and a horse and carriage) will be of little significance in the sparkling sunshine of a delightful June day when around 17 varied gardens will beguile guests. More corporeal residents included Boris Karloff who retired to the village, and latterly Antonio Carluccio, Bill Bryson and Sandi Toksvig, all of whom have recently opened the gardens in the event's 39-year history. A regrettably early start will allow us to meet whichever notable it is this year.
Plan:
There will be no set plan for visiting the gardens and we won't go round in a big group: you're welcome to go wherever and with whomever you choose. You're also welcome to leave when you want, but there will be an agreed meeting place and time should you wish to depart as part of a group. You don't even need to book on to this event to see the gardens of course; we'll just happily just bump into you in the village! If you do come by train (or car but want a little more exercise). See the event's website
here
. Liphook Railway Station is some way outside of the village, so we'll have a pleasant short walk through fields and the village to get there. Please see the route
here
.
Dogs:
This walk is less suitable for dogs as they are not permitted in the gardens, but the walk to the village would be quite suitable if they could be looked after by someone outside of each garden.

Title: A Coastal walk alongside Englands biggest seabird city
Summary:
Excited about seeing nesting sea birds? This is a lovely area of the Yorkshire coastline to walk along, that just happens to be the biggest nesting site for sea birds in England. (source: RSPB Bempton)
An easy and linear route that heads along East Yorkshire’s stunning chalk cliffs of Thornwick Nab, Cat Nab, Scale Nab, Wandale Nab Crab Rocks, Gull Nook and Mazey Shelves, who knew they all had names?
We’ll walk along the coastal footpath to RSPB Bempton, where you can see the breeding birds fly overhead or from the sea feeding grounds into their nesting points clinging to the cliffs, or their grass burrows. If you’re lucky enough you may spot the puffins. There are information boards and further viewing platforms at Bempton, where there’ll be an opportunity for a refreshment break at the cafe before we head back along the same route in reverse giving twice the opportunity to take photos and take in the sea views. You might want to bring a zoom camera or binoculars to get really close. I have, however, just taken the pictures here included with a regular phone camera. Total distance there and back is about 10km (6 miles).
Image credits:
all photos provided are taken by the event leader, and OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Difficulty & Distance: 10km max on walkable flat coastal footpaths and tracks,

Title: The Tolkien Trail
Summary:
Join Mike and OutdoorLads for a 7 mile walk in the footsteps of J. R. R. Tolkien, famous author of ‘The Lord of the Rings’ trilogy and ‘The Hobbit’. We will cross farmland and some woodland and walk along the banks of the River Ribble and River Hodder. Points of interest include Stonyhurst College, where Tolkien is said to have spent a lot of time whilst writing the books, drawing inspiration from the surrounding areas. We will also pass the historic landmark, ‘Cromwell’s bridge’ on the River Hodder, named after Oliver Cromwell, who marched his army over the bridge in 1648 to fight in the Battle of Preston. This beautiful part of Lancashire is full of history, and with stunning views of Pendle Hill and the surrounding countryside, what more could you ask for?
We will stop for lunch at the midway point of our walk and, for those who wish to, there will be an opportunity to have a post-walk drink in one of the local establishments in Hurst Green.
Your First OutdoorLads Walk Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.

Title: Alresford creek  walk and Regatta
Summary:
En route, we encounter the serene beauty of Alresford Creek before concluding our journey in lively Wivenhoe. Arriving during the bustling
Wivenhoe Regatta
adds a festive touch, inviting us to linger for a drink and soak in the vibrant atmosphere."
Is it dog friendly?
Dogs are very welcome but please note and abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
).  Please also remember not everyone loves your dog as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
New Members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "
My First Event
" section under "About Us".
Image / photo credits:
All leaders’ own images (Tim Healey) with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.

Title: Tiptree Jam & Bluebell Woods
Summary:
Waitlist
:
This event may become fully booked, however, if you'd like to attend and there are no spaces left,
add yourself to the waitlist
, places often become available closer to the date, and chances are you may still get a place.
This circular walk is approx 11.5 miles (18.5 kms)  in total and starts from the pretty North Essex village of Kelvedon which has a number of listed character buildings. Once though the village, we will head out through the Essex Countryside on a very gentle incline via an area known Windmill Hill (yes Essex isn't completely flat!) on our way to Tiptree.
Our route will take alongside some of the fruit fields of the renowned Wilkinsons Jam Factory. We'll then stop by the tea rooms here to get a coffee and maybe a scone with some of the famous jam you can sample. There is a gift shop there too with jams of course and some pretty good gins!
Next we'll head though Tiptree back out into the countryside again on our alternative route back to Kelvedon. We'll pass through the lovely Pods Wood which subject to seasonal conditions, should be carpeted with bluebells. We'll then pass through the picturesque little village of Messing with a possible stop at the lovely little pub there.
From there it's across fields and passing Prested Hall Country House Hotel and grounds before arriving back into Kelvedon via its sister village of Feering.
The end of the walk will of course see the opportunity of a visit to one of the village pubs for a beverage or two.
Is it dog friendly?
Yes!!  4 legged friends are welcome but please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and note there are a couple of stiles to cross. The Tea Room has an outside seating area. Please remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
For those wishing to arrive to this event by train, Kelvedon has a mainline railway station with services from London Liverpool Street.
New Members - I would like to warmly welcome any newbies looking perhaps to try this as their first Outdoor Lads event. You may find this link useful
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
or if you have If you have any questions then feel free to message me.
Image credits: Photos are all the property of Andy Gent and I give full consent for these to be used for OutdoorLads purposes at any time
Difficulty & Distance: Most of the terraine is flat although there is a slight but very gentle incline over the course of about 1.5 miles near the start of the walk

Title: Froggatt and Padley Gorge Day Walk
Summary:
A 12km walk taking in the beautiful scenery of Froggatt Edge and Padley Gorge. Starting at the Longshaw Estate National Trust Car Park, we head south onto Froggatt Edge, one of several gritsone edges that characterise this area of the Peak District, popular with outdoor climbers. From there we will descend quite rapidly to the River Derwent which we will follow for a short distance to the pretty village of Froggatt, where we will stop for lunch by the river. After lunch we will head north, roughly following the course of the river to Grindleford and Nether Padley. We may stop for refreshments at the Maynard in Gindleflord to prepare ouselves for the last section of our route, which is an ascent through the stunning scenery of Padley Gorge. Further refreshments will be avaiable at the Longshaw Cafe when we reach the end of the walk.
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs should be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted, whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request.
If the event is fully booked:
Please
join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
If this is your first OutdoorLads walk event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to contact the leader if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.
image credits -
all images provided by the event leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: 12Km walk with one steep descent and a moderate ascent.

Title: Farleton Fell Day walk
Summary:
Join Stephen on a 9 mile walk in and around Farleton Fell in North Lancashire
Highlights of the walk will be
Walking along the limestone pavements of Dalton and Hutton Roof Crags
Passing the Uberash Breast (part of this pavement)
Dropping down below Holmepark Fell
Ascending up to Farleton Knott with views across to the Lake District and the Howgills
From there we will see if we can find Turtle Rock an obviously named small boulder
We will head towards Hutton Roof past Hanging Scar a huge boulder/erratic that is defying gravity
Wander through Hutton Roof and return back to the car park
Afterwards if you fancy, we can all drive to  the Longlands pub en route back to the M6/A6
https://longlandshotel.co.uk/
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do
sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Difficulty & Distance: A couple of medium climbs and some uneven terrain

Title: A Summer Social in Blackpool
Summary:
Join the OutdoorLads for A Saturday Night in Blackpool for A Summer Social !
Join us for an unforgettable evening in Blackpool, where the promenade truly comes alive after dark. We’ll begin with a relaxed walk along the seafront, soaking up the electric atmosphere as the lights shimmer against the Irish Sea.
As the evening unfolds, we’ll head into some of Blackpool’s popular local brew houses, and explore the new bars on the energetic LGBTQ+ scene.
Of course, no great night out is complete without good food. There will be opportunities to grab a bite along the way — whether that’s classic seaside fish and chips, late-night street food, or something a little more substantial to keep us going. And for a true taste of tradition, why not indulge in ice cream or fluffy candyfloss as we stroll? After all, you’re never too old for a little seaside sweetness !
This is set to be a lively, friendly, and flexible evening — perfect for enjoying Blackpool’s legendary nightlife at your own pace, discovering new favourites, and making brilliant memories along the way.
For more information about events in the area during this time, please click
here...
There are plenty of car parks in Blackpool, and there is free parking in the streets. A charge applies if you wish to park on the prom. Blackpool North and Blackpool South train stations are easily accessible. There are, of course, the trams which you can use. Alternatively, you may wish to utilise a horse-drawn fairy princess carriage !
The meeting point is located in between North and Central Pier. Blackpool North Train Station is approximately 15 mins walk away.
Smart casual clothing. No track suit bottoms or shorts please !
Many of us usually book accommodation over this weekend, so why not  make a weekend of it and take advantage of the variety of hotels and B & B's in the area ?!
The link to the daytime event, summer gay day walk is
here...
A Telegram group will be set up nearer the time.
New to OutdoorLads ?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
All photos taken by the event leader
Difficulty & Distance: Social and leisure walk

Title: A Gay Summer Day in Blackpool
Summary:
Join the OutdoorLads for A Gay Saturday Summer Stroll in Blackpool !
Join us for a delightful stroll along the famous promenade of Blackpool, where the fresh sea air, lively atmosphere, and sweeping views across the Irish Sea create the perfect setting for a memorable day out !
We will wander along the seafront, and take in the iconic sights and sounds that make this classic seaside resort so special — from the cheerful chime of amusement arcades to the laughter drifting from the beach.
Along the way, we’ll pass some of Blackpool’s most beloved landmarks, including Blackpool Tower rising proudly above the skyline, (a symbol of the town since 1894). There will be plenty of opportunities to pause for photos and have a look in the shops.
We usually stop for refreshments at the beautiful North Pier, the oldest and most tranquil of Blackpool’s three Victorian piers. Here, we can relax with a drink, and enjoy traditional seaside treats.
After soaking up the atmosphere and stunning views, we’ll make our way back at a comfortable pace and grab some lunch.
This is a fun, friendly, and flexible day out, tailored to what the group would most enjoy. If there’s somewhere else you’d like to visit — whether it’s the dazzling promenade lights, a cosy café, or another local attraction — we can adapt and make the day truly our own.
There are plenty of car parks in Blackpool, and there is free parking in the streets. A charge applies if you wish to park on the prom. Blackpool North and Blackpool South train stations are easily accessible. There are, of course, the trams which you can use. Alternatively, you may wish to utilise a horse-drawn fairy princess carriage !
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
Please note that many establishments in Blackpool are dog friendly, however, some establishments may not allow dogs on the premises.
Many of us usually book accommodation over this weekend, so why not  make a weekend of it and take advantage of the variety of hotels and B & B's in the area ?!
The link to the evening summer social is
here...
A Telegram group will be set up nearer the time.

All photos taken by the event leader

Title: Fire Bird Double Weekend - Day 2 of 2 Walk
Summary:
This is a circular walk in the beautiful countryside of West Sussex. Meeting in the delightful village of Rudgwick we cross the upper reaches of the River Arun, then through fields and woods, passing through the lovely village of Slindon.
Fancy making a weekend of it?
We are camping at Riverside Farm for the weekend and you are more than welcome to join us.
http://www.riversidefarm-camping.uk/
Day 1 walk is also live:
https://www.outdoorlads.com/events/fire-bird-double-weekender-day-1-2-walk-260523
Rudgwick and the Low Weald
Around Rudgwick, the landscape is defined by the
Low Weald
. It has a 'patchworky' feel with small, irregular fields are bordered by thick, ancient hedgerows and "shaws" (narrow strips of woodland). Expect to see dense woods with oak, hazel, and hornbeam. In spring, these woods are famously carpeted in bluebells. Our route takes us along the
Downs Link
—a long-distance bridleway following a dismantled railway line that offers a flat, green corridor through the countryside.
Slinfold
Slinfold is a quintessential Wealden village located about 4 miles west of Horsham. It is often cited as one of the most picturesque villages in West Sussex, characterized by its "linear" layout and a deep sense of historical continuity. We'll see a mix of timber-framed medieval cottages, Georgian houses, and Victorian brickwork. The village is home to over 50 Listed Buildings.
St. Peter’s Church
was rebuilt in 1861 by Benjamin Ferrey, this handsome church with its prominent spire sits at the village's center. It was built on the site of an earlier medieval church and remains a focal point for the community.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks. I just ask that owners please ensure they are kept on a lead as appropriate, especially around livestock and when near busy roads. Please, also remember that not everyone is a dog lover.
This particular walk does have stiles so please ensure your dog is comfortable with these.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
Timings
Whilst every endeavour is made to make the timings of this event accurate, it cannot be guaranteed that we will finish on time and there may be delays.
All photos used for this event have been taken from the Outdoorlads image library with deemed permission from the contributors of those images.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is approximately 15 km with a total elevation (climb) of 173 metres. There are no steep hills but paths may be narrow or overgrown in places.

Title: Reading Indoor Bouldering
Summary:
This bouldering session presents an ideal opportunity for a fun evening of what I call sociable workout where we take on a wide range of physical (and in fact mental) challenges with the atmosphere always friendly and cheerful. All levels are welcome!
The beauty of bouldering lies in its simplicity: you don't need ropes or a harness, just some climbing shoes which can be hired at the centre and a drive to get the top and overcome some trepidation. It's also a fundamentally social experience, with most of the session spent discussing how to solve particular climbing routes, aka problems, or catching up with each other in between climbs. Bouldering is a great way to get into climbing, come and see what all the fuss is about!
All skill levels are welcome
. If you're new to climbing, we've got you covered with tailored guidance, boosting your skills and confidence while ensuring your safety on and around the wall. Don’t worry about needing to invest in gear before you try. The Climbing Hangar offers
free shoe rentals
so you don’t have to shell out for shoes until you know bouldering is for you.
Experienced boulderer? Dive in and embrace the challenges independently. Just remember to follow the centre's rules for your own safety.
Our ethos is inclusivity. I keep our group tight-knit to foster connection and ensure personalised coaching for everyone, regardless of your experience level. Many climbers started their journeys relatively “late” in life, incl. some session leaders
🤫
We convene at the
Climbing Hangar in Reading
, one of the largest climbing centres in the region. The generously spacious and friendly cafe/lounge is my personal favourite where you can find us socialising, having a cuppa, snacks or even pizza before and after the climbs.
If you have never climbed at the Climbing Hangar in Reading before, you must book an induction slot:
https://www.theclimbinghangar.com/locations/reading
- follow instructions to book an induction slot around the time that this event starts.
You also need to
complete the waiver form that is linked in the confirmation email
once you've booked an induction slot.
This only needs to be done once.
Want to Get Out More?
To be informed of all our North West Home Counties* events please click on this Telegram link
https://t.me/+EoB--tkEV3s4ZWJk
* Berkshire, Oxfordshire, Buckinghamshire, Hertfordshire & Bedfordshire
Photo Credits:
All photos taken by OutdoorLads Members and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: Difficulty can be adapted depending on experience

Title: Derwent Water, Back Tor and Lost Lad
Summary:
This 10-mile walk in the Upper Derwent Valley, often referred to as Derbyshire's Lake District, features beautiful landscape views from the rugged wilderness, peace and tranquility of open moorland.
From Fairholmes, the walk begins from the mighty Derwent Water (reservoir) dam constructed between 1902 and 1916 to supply fresh water throughout the region. It was used during WWII as a practice run by 617 Squadron for the famous "Dam buster’s" bouncing bombs. We take in great views from the water's edge towards Howden dam before we head upwards along a hidden valley and up onto open moorland.
Howden Moor and Howden Edge form one of the wildest, least-disturbed upland landscapes in England. This remote expanse of heather moorland, peat bog, and gritstone outcrops is a haven for wildlife such as grouse, mountain hares, golden plovers, and short-eared owls. Howden Edge offers sweeping views across the Upper Derwent Valley and towards Bleaklow.
We head southwards onto Cartledge Stones Ridge to the gritstone outcrop of Back Tor, the highest point of the walk, at 538 metres (1765 feet). There is a short scramble up to the trig point.
Nearby, Lost Lad, standing at 518 metres (1,699 feet) has scenic views towards the reservoirs and the surrounding peaks, and a legend about a shepherd boy who became lost in a blizzard, with the words "Lost Lad" found nearby after his body was discovered.
Back Tor and Lost Lad are two of the 95 "Ethel" hills, a tribute to Ethel Haythornthwaite who pioneered the establishment of the Peak District as Britain's first national park in 1951.
From the ridge we descend via Walker's Clough and back to Derwent Water.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are welcome but owners must ensure they are kept under control at all times.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in the Midlands / ODL Central Region?
Join our
Central Region Telegram Chat Group
, or the more more local
East Midlands Telegram Chat Group
and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link:
https://outdoorlads.events/central
Difficulty & Distance: Distance 16 km (10 miles). Total ascent 457 m (1499 feet). Approximately 5.5 hours. Some paths are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: A Cut-Acre Above the Rest, with Optional Home Cooked Lunch
Summary:
Get ready for a fantastic Saturday adventure with
Andy
and
OutdoorLads
as we explore the breathtaking
Cutacre Country Park
!
This stunning 7-mile walk takes us through 530 acres of rolling countryside, tucked behind the impressive Logistics North development. Expect sweeping panoramic views stretching from the
Derbyshire Dales
and
Manchester
in the east, across the
Cheshire Plain
, all the way to the
Clwydian Mountains
and the sparkling
Mersey Estuary
in the west. It’s big skies, open landscapes, and fresh air at its very best.
We’ll set off across the park, pausing at peaceful Swan Lake for a relaxed coffee break and a chance to soak in the scenery. After a short stop at Astley Park, we’ll continue on to the historic town of
Tyldesley
.
Here, you’ll have the option to round off the day at the ever-popular
The Union Arms
, famed for its excellent real ales and delicious, freshly prepared home-cooked meals. It’s the perfect reward after a satisfying day on the trail!
Arriving early? There’s also a lovely bakery at the start point serving hot drinks, bacon butties, fresh sandwiches, pies, pasties — and of course, cake.
Lace up your boots, bring your appetite, and join us for a day of great company, great views, and great food! 🌿🥾
For more information visit:
https://www.lancswt.org.uk/nature-reserves/cutacre
(The Wildlife Trust)
https://www.leighjournal.co.uk/news/24279390.union-arms-brings-together-regulars-dementia-groups-tyldesley/
https://www.facebook.com/UnionArmsTyldesley/
(Union Arms)
https://unionarmstyldesley.co.uk/
(Union Arms menu's, including non-gluten and vegan options)
https://www.firstbus.co.uk/greater-manchester/routes-and-maps/vantage
(GM Guided Bus Info.)
Please see comments at the bottom of the event page for further information nearer the time.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
This is a dog friendly event so please bring your pooches along - the more the merrier. The Union is also dog friendly and they love our furry friends !
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
Food pre-orders will be requested approximately one week before the event. This allows the kitchen to prepare accordingly and helps minimise the risk of items selling out on the day.
All photos taken by Growler
Plenty of free parking in the large car park.
