Title: Chateaus and Chagalls - A Topnotch Tour around Tonbridge
Summary:
This walk includes a visit to a church to see its stained-glass windows. A donation of £3 towards the church's upkeep is requested if you visit it.
"
C'est magnifique! Je les ferai tous!
"
(
"It's magnificent! I will do them all!"
) said artist Marc Chagall at the dedication of his window in the small church of Tudeley. And he gradually did just that, making Tudeley the only church in the world with all its windows designed by him. This is just one attraction on a walk that starts and ends in the historic town of Tonbridge and takes in oast houses and estate houses (Jacobean Somerhill House, Tonbridge Castle), and public houses and God's houses (more stained glass, this time by William Morris). The natural attractions abound too, including the banks of the River Medway and the splendid views across the High Weald Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.
The sights:
Tonbridge:
A market town on the River Medway, dominated by the C13 castle gatehouse. The core of the town still contains many buildings dating from the C15: the oldest is The Portreeve's House. Tonbridge rose against Queen Mary's marriage to the King of Spain in 1554 and as a result saw the burning of a number of Protestants. During the Civil War, the town was garrisoned by the Parliamentarians who refortified the castle. Royalists failed to retake the town. From 1740 the Medway Navigation Company allowed coal and lime to be transported to the town, and gunpowder, hops and timber to be carried downriver to Maidstone and the Thames. Some of the original warehouses and the wharves remain today. Somerhill Park is a Grade-I listed Jacobean Mansion, built 1611-1613 and now a private school.
All Saints, Tudeley:
Mostly C13 and C14, restored in the C18. In 1963, Sarah, the daughter of Sir Henry and Lady D'Avigdor-Goldsmid of Somerhill House, drowned off Rye, East Sussex. The couple commissioned Russian-French Cubist-Fauvist artist Marc Chagall to design a memorial stained glass window. When Chagall came for the dedication of the window in 1967 and saw the church for the first time, he exclaimed
"It's magnificent! I will do them all!"
. He designed the remaining eleven windows over 10 years. The last windows were installed in 1985 after Chagall's death.
Southborough:
A large village in a formerly heavily wooded setting on the edge of Tunbridge Wells. The Common is a Site of Nature Conservation Interest (SNCI). St Peter's Church was designed by Decimus Burton, architect of Marble Arch, some of Regent's Park and Kew's Temperate House. Southborough Common will be our lunch stop and the Hand and Sceptre pub is adjacent.
Bidborough:
A village on Bidborough Ridge overlooking the Medway Valley and northern High Weald. St Lawrence is a small Norman sandstone church, elevated above the village. A north aisle was added in the C13, and Ewan Christian built one to the south in 1876. Grade II* listed. Most windows made by William Morris and Company, some designed by Edward Burne-Jones.
Haysden Country Park:
64 hectares of countryside with two lakes and a stretch of the River Medway. The park is home to a variety of wildlife including waterfowl, wild flowers, and insect life. A Site of Nature Conservation Interest, and Local Nature Reserve.
Tonbridge Castle:
In 1067 William I charged Richard Fitz Gilbert with guarding the Medway. He erected a motte-and-bailey castle on the site. In 1088, the de Clare family (descendants of Fitz Gilbert) rebelled against William II. His army besieged the castle, which fell, and as punishment the king had both the castle and the town burnt to the ground. Before 1100, the de Clares replaced the wooden castle with a stone shell keep. This was reinforced during the C13, and in 1295 a stone wall was built around the town. The twin-towered gatehouse of was built by Richard de Clare, completed in 1260. The castle was not lived in between 1521 and the late C18. The mansion was added in 1793. Both castle and mansion are Grade I listed buildings. The motte can be climbed up and the bailey is now a park.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them. They risk getting bored while the group sees the Chagall stained-glass windows in the church, although obedient dogs are welcomed in the church. The walk is of moderate length, but there will be fields with livestock and a number of stiles and roads to negotiate and lanes to walk along. A dog must be obedient if it is off the lead.
The
route
:
We'll set off north from the station as far as the bridge in the centre of the town. We'll then pick up the Wealdway east as far as Postern Lane and The Postern and take that. heading east, as far as Tudeley Hale, before heading south to Tudeley proper. We'll then south along Five Oak Green Road and follow the Tunbridge Wells Circular Walk (TWCW) south to Dislingbury Road where we'll go under the A21 and follow a footpath under the railway line. We'll then rejoin the TWCW to Southborough. After lunch and crossing Southborough Common, we'll follow the Eden Valley Walk to Bidborough. This will lead us along the B2176, then north to Haysden, under the A21 and through Haysden Country Park over and along the Medway back to Tonbridge where we'll have a quick look around the sights before heading back to the station.
(All images were taken by the leader on his recce.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 21 km (13 miles); total ascent is 318 m; terrain is hilly but with two main ascents and descents and small ascent; surfaces are dirt, grass and tarmac.

Title: Hertfordshire via Hitch Wood
Summary:
Join Alex for this 11-mile circular walk to Hitch Wood's blossoming bluebells
Our route begins at Stevenage station and we will weave our way westwards via the town's dual carriageways and underpasses for a short while until we cross into open countryside. We head towards the small settlement of Langley across undulating countryside of fields and woodland.
After Langley we will head across open fields once more until we reach the village of St Paul's Walden. Here we will turn northwards and skirt around the edge of St Paul's Walden Bury estate before ascending towards the tower of All Saints' church, its medieval façade contrasting with its Georgian interior.
From here we will head around the estate of Stagenhoe, now a centre for neurologucal care, towards the Hitch Wood nature reserve. A waymarked path, the Hitchwood Trail, leads us through the woods, which should be bursting with bluebells in late April. We will stop for lunch somewhere around here.
We will then continue our journey north and past the atmospheric ruins of the 14th-century Minsden Chapel. From here our route turns southeast and takes us back downhill to Stevenage across open farmland. We will head through the Old Town area and may stop at a local pub on the way back to the station.
Don't waste that space!
Please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Dogs are welcome but please bear in mind not everyone likes dogs as much as you. Some of the fields we cross may contain livestock so please make sure this will not be an issue.

All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse

Participation Statement
,
in addition
to booking your event space before attending an OutdoorLads event. You only need to complete this Participation Statement once, not for each event you attend.
Difficulty & Distance: Undulating terrain with just over 200m ascent. Around 4-5 hours walking time.

Title: The Llyn Peninsular AONB: Yr Eifl and Nant Gwrtheyrn
Summary:
This is a figure of 8 Walk so also suitable for those who would prefer a shorter day.  If you are ONLY coming for Part B the afternoon, please message the leader in advance. If you are coming for the whole event, or just the morning, there is no need to make special contact. You must be registered to take part in either part.  Both parts start at the same place.
Part A. (Departing 10:45) 4.5 miles,  1200' of height gain
Yr Eifl, translates from Welsh to mean "the forks" or "the trident". The name describes the distinctive shape of its three peaks (Garn Ganol, Tre'r Ceiri, and Garn Fôr) as they appear from a distance.  The peaks are also known known as 'The Rivals' perhaps because they appear to rival the big peaks on the main ranges. The Rivals stand right on the coast and create a most impressive backdrop when seen from the north, or from Yns Mon (Anglesey).
After leaving the meeting point we will embark on a circular route to the summit of Garn Ganol, 564m. This is the highest peak on the Peninsular .  The route to the summit is only a mile and a half from the car park, however much of it is a gradient of 25% (1 in 4) and there's 1000' of ascent to this point. We will take it steady and wait for slower walkers to catch up. From the top if the visibility is clear The Wicklow mountains of Ireland, The mountain of Snaefell on The Isle of Man, and the Lake district might be seen. Much of Eryri from Yr Wddfa down to Cadair Idris and right across Cardigan bay may be visible too. Bring your Binoculars. Next we will see the Tre’r Ceiri Hill fort inhabited between 150 BC and 400 AD. Little is known about these early inhabitants, other than they depended heavily on the local iron ore. It is one of the best preserved in the UK, and a fitting place for lunch. The route back down is a bit easier on the knees.
Part B (Assemble at 13:00 ) 3.5 miles 950' of height gain
Setting off in the opposite direction we will embark on a 2nd circular walk with good views of the coastline where you might spot grey seals and plenty of marine birdlife. After a short steep descent we find ourselves at the quaint settlement of Nant Gwrtheyrn which has a Heritage Centre (free) and a rather nice cafe
(details here)
. Once a quarry and for a while a 'hippy commune', the settlement is now a residential school for those learning the Welsh Language. The setting couldn't be nicer. Revived by tea and cake we will circle around for a couple more miles of ascent, back up to the car park.
If you are thinking of making a weekend of it the Llyn Peninsular has some amazing camping sites
like this one
a few miles down the road.

AndyM, event leader, except those as stamped and licensed for re-use under the Creative Commons license at Geograph.org.uk

Title: BIG20: THE Celebration Event - Hostel
Summary:
Where did the 20 years go!? It's been an epic tens of thousands of miles walked, climbed, skied, kayaked, cycled; thousands of friendships made; and an immeasurable amount of laughter and incredible memories.
We hope you can join us in the Peak District to celebrate this epic milestone!
There are three accommodation types for this event, so please ensure you book on the correct one:
Hostel
(this event page)
Pods
Camping
All accommodation types include the catering and entertainment in the main building.
The hostel has a range of modern, all-ensuite rooms with bunk beds, sleeping between up to 6 people each. They are spacious and well appointed, this is a lovely hostel!
The weekend will include a formal AGM which all Full/Concession Members are very much encouraged to attend to help us formally celebrate our amazing volunteers and members. (If you want to attend just the AGM and not stay at the hostel or for the party, that will be possible and details will be made available in due course, along with details of the AGM live stream.)
You can also expect amazing walks and other events offered by our volunteers, on-site activities (is that a zip wire on top of the building!?), spectacular food, a fully-stocked bar, a late night disco with DJ Dan, and we're going to party like its 2016 with a Ceilidh with Triple Scotch (yes, they want to come back!)!
Whether you have been a member since 2006 when we formed or only just joined us today - all are welcome and all have played a part creating, shaping and continuing this wonderful organisation which has changed lives, brought lifelong friends together (and even husbands!), and given everyone the chance to try something new with our wide range of activities for all.
You don't want to miss out on this epic weekend, so get booking!
Part Payment
If you'd like to pay in instalments, please contact us on
support@outdoorlads.com
and we can arrange that. We'll discuss with you how much you'd like to pay each month and a time frame over which you'd like to pay. You will need to have paid in full by the 31 August.
OutdoorLads Foundation
If this event is not financially viable to you, check out the
OutdoorLads Foundation
, and hopefully we can get you celebrating with us!
Dogs:
Unfortunately, dogs are not permitted at this venue, sorry.
Campervans/Caravans/Motorhomes:
This event is not suitable for campervans, motorhomes or caravans.
Photos provided by the venue and by OutdoorLads Members with permission for use.
Difficulty & Distance: Hostel celebration event with other ancillary events

Title: Swanley to Lullingstone Castle with a Pub Lunch
Summary:
Enjoy a mid-Spring walk through the
Kent countryside
exploring war memorials, fields, farms, castles and the remains of an ancient Roman villa.
This is a circular walk from Swanley station, following the tracks for a very short distance before passing through farms and a field or two, then pass under the M25, until (oh look...) more fields and farmlands!!
After about 3.5 miles we will arrive at the
Lullingstone Roman Villa.
It is believed that the first building on the site was built in AD 100.  Little is known of this original structure, but in the early 2nd century a shrine and bathhouse existed here.  The villa served many purposes including that of a Roman governor's home, an enlarged 3rd century villa to its conversion to a Christian temple / church.  Sadly in the 5th Century a fire destroyed the villa, leading to its abandonment and eventual decay.  However many of the Roman mosaics and artifacts were preserved throughout history and are now on display in the museum.  Although closed at the time of publishing, it is hoped that the museum will be open when we arrive.
We will then continue toward the village of Eynsford and the
Norman "enclosure castle"
that gives the village its name.  Built by William de Eynsford between 1085 and 1087, its purpose was to protect the lands of Lanfranc, the Archbishop of Canterbury at the time.  The family owned and occupied the castle until 1261, when the last of the family died, with no line of succession. Access into this English Heritage site is free so we will spend a few minutes exploring the ruins.
We will leave Eynsford to the north, heading along Sparepenny Lane, with a brief stop at the memorial to Flight Lieutenant James A. Paterson, MBE, whose Spitfire X4422 crashed near the location of the memorial.
As we continue along Sparepenny Lane we come to the village of Farningham, which will include a pub lunch stop at
The Pied Bull
, right on the High Street.  Should you prefer something other than the pub lunch, there are two small shops in Eynsford we can pop into or you can bring something from home..  I have arranged pre-ordering with the pub, but as such the event booking will need to close on Friday 10 April so that orders can be selected and sent to the pub.  I will send the menu choices as the event gets closer, but feel free to check out the menu in the link.  Also please note that all food and drink will be at your expense.
After lunch we will make our way back toward Swanley, stopping briefly to view the Farningham Cattle Screen.  Although the true purpose of this unique structure is unknown, it is believed to prevent cattle from "wandering downstream" while crossing the River Darent.
The walk back to Swanley is along the edge of the A20 so care will need to be taken.  This will be roughly 2 miles and will cross several round-abouts and will go under the M25.  Please note that slight route alterations may be needed along this stretch.  We will then cross over the M25 onto London Road making our we back to Swanley station.
I love having our furry friends on my events; they will need to be kept on leashes at all times.
Photos are courtesy of
www.unsplash.co.uk
, and
www.outdoorlads.com
and are used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Relatively flat

Title: Blaenau Ffestiniog: A UNESCO World Heritage Landscape
Summary:
Explore the amazing mountainous slate landscape of NW Wales on this walk.
Introduction: The Slate Landscape of Northwest Wales was given UNESCO World Heritage status in 2021.  Recognised for its profound impact on the industrial revolution, this site encompasses quarries, mines, mills, and transport systems that operated primarily from 1780 to 1940. In these quaries for decades thousands of men toiled to cut the precious stone that is so durable and lightweight as a roofing material, and has many other uses. At the station the narrow gauge steam railway was used to take the slate down to the coast at Porthmadog, from where is was taken by ship to many destinations. The railway initially ran by gravity downhill, and then a team of horses pulled the empty wagons back up. The steam locos that came later are a unique design with two boilers to create enough power for the gradient.We will be fortunate to have ODL member Bedwyr taking part who lives in the area. Bedwyr will share his knowledge of the area as we progress. During the middle of the walk we go further into the Eryri landscape and if visibility permits, capture stunning vistas including the Crib Goch, Moelyns and Rhinog ranges.
Route description: Starting from world famous Blaenau Ffestiniog ‘The town that roofed the world’ our route threads its way up ancient mining tracks and inclines. Straight away the gradient reminds us that this is a walk in mountain country as we ascend around 1300’ before levelling off. We observe the scarred landscape and huge slopes of slate fragments. Our route then heads away across wet grassland to pass a pair of reservoirs and from where Eryri's bigger peaks will be visible.  We will circle around the quarry at Manod Mawr, where during the WW2, the contents of the National Gallery were stored, to protect them from bomb damage. Explosives were used to enlarge the entrance, so that lorries could be unloaded inside the hillside. The project was quite secretive, and many of the smaller paintings arrived either in Cadbury delivery trucks or in Post Office vans, so that their contents were not obvious. Of course the locals soon got wind of what was going on.
After that we will pass down the steep valley of Cwm Teigl, initially rewarded with a most stupendous view towards the Rhinog Mountains and following the rushing river as we go.  Our way back into Blaenau passes a natural lake, and then follows down an incline, being the route of an old tramway used to carry away the slate.
All in all this will be a fascinating day walk, and one that will deliver some good exercise on the hilly mountainsides.
Waitlist?
there's usually a turnover of places in the week leading up to an event. If there's still a waitlist we can often allow extra spaces, so please join the waitlist and you'll amost certainly get a place.

taken by AndyM, event leader.

Title: Spring in Saddleworth
Summary:
A new hostel for OutdoorLads and a slightly different location!! A stones throw from the Dark Peak area of the Peak District, but only a short hop from Manchester.
Our accommodation is situated on the north western edge of the Peak District yet with easy road and rail links (20 mins walk from Greenfield railway station!). It sits on the hillside just outside Greenfield near Dovestone Reservoir.
The centre caters for those who love outdoor pursuits and the website states "fell walking, rock climbing, caving, cycling, pony trekking, swimming, canoeing, sailing, running, angling, orienteering and even llama trekking are all catered for in the area". Not that we are going to be doing all of those activities, but there quite a few options!! Plus we are near the lovely villages of Uppermill, Marsden or a 20 mins train ride into Manchester. A short trip over the hills and you could visit Standedge canal tunnel (highest, deepest, longest canal tunnel) to do the 30mins boat tour (separate booking needed and costs £12/£10 concession).
The centre is a single storey building with four bunkhouse style dorms. There is a large kitchen, 2 toilet blocks, a dinning/social area and a private garden.
For a plan of the weekend, I expect we'll do something like:
Friday
: Arrive from 5pm. Get settled in and get chatting with others to reconnect or get to know new people. We will have the usual OutdoorLads buffet. So please bring along some food to add to the buffet (enough for 1-2 people and please don't all just bring pizza/quiche/sausage rolls, please).
Saturday
: A nice breakfast to set the day off.
Then there will be the option for a lovely walk across Saddleworth (to be decided). I'm not a walk leader so if anyone is and can help to lead a walk, please message me and we'll be ever so grateful! The walk around Dovestone Reservoir and Dovestone Edge is beautiful though.
Other options, will be at your own arrangement unless there is sufficient interest from a large group.
We'll of course have an included evening meal I will cook, I do love to cook so I'll try and make something special!!!!
Sunday
: another provided breakfast. Then you are either free to go home or join in a possible activity locally.
Dogs:
Sorry but the event is not suitable for dogs.
Photos:
Provided by OutdoorLads members and Saddleworth Outdoor Pursuits Association and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Relatively strauightforward hiking

Title: SE500 The Winchester Downs
Summary:
An incredible mixture of medieval city, rolling downs, hill fort, chalkland flowers, habitat of rare blue butterflies and a range of less common birds - all in one walk!
The walk begins in the ancient capital of Winchester, which was actually the capital of England in 900CE. The streets are full of history, with Roman remains, King Alfred the Great’s city walls and a grand medieval cathedral. Jane Austen is buried here.
Our route
takes us south beside the River Itchen. This clear, chalk river naturally filters the water, making it ideal for fly fishing and growing watercress. We then climb the slopes of St Catherines Hill. You may notice giant mounds of the ancient defences on the way up and down.
The land here is based on chalk, laid down in warm seas around 98 million years ago. This produced soil ideal for orchids and bell flowers. The hill has been designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest. Look out for the yellow Rock Rose and the uncommon chalkhill blue butterfly in summer. Other flowers include fairy flax, clustered bellflower, autumn lady's tresses orchids and frog orchid; basil and wild carrot. Species uncommon for southern England found here include bastard toadflax and juniper.
Descending to the south, we cross a dry valley to a hill on the south side and then return a little further along. This gives us the best view of St. Catherine’s Hill. The dry valley was used in the 14th century when plague devastated Winchester and the graveyards were full, so bodies were piled in here.
Continuing on our route we pick up the Pilgrim’s Trail and head over the rounded hills of Twyford Down and then climb steadily over Fawley Down. The whole area is known as the Winchester Cluster of Downs. The ancient Pilgrims' Way begins at Winchester and goes all the way to Canterbury and the shrine of Thomas Becket. The pathway under our feet is older than that; at least 4000 years and likely as old as 10000, as old as the stone age.
The highest point on the route is Cheesefoot Head at 176m. There is a naturally rounded amphitheatre here in the down. In 1944 American and British troops were stationed near here and, just before D-Day, it was here that US President Eisenhower made his famous speech: “The eyes of the world are upon you. The hopes and prayers of liberty-loving people everywhere march with you.”
We pick up the South Downs Way over Chilcomb. This area is excellent for bird watching. Skylark, Red Kit, Kestrel and Peregrines are here, as well as Barn Owl and Tawny Owl. Passing the Norman Church, the route turns downhill, crossing the road to rise again over Magdalen Down Hill with it’s Bronze Age burial barrows. Adonis Blue butterflies may be seen here.
Entering Winchester from the East, we rise to the viewpoint on St. Giles Hill. There used to be a medieval fair here on St Giles’s day, 1st of September. You get an excellent view over the city from here before we return to complete the walk.
The ups and downs add up to 500m of ascent, and so this walk qualifies as an entry in the 'SE500' series of walks; if you attend you'll improve your fitness and burn 2100 calories in the process. This walk will be fairly challenging, but we'll take it at a sensible pace and in manageable stages and we'll do each rise with plenty of time to have rests.
The route involves rolling hills, ridges and fields and is suitable for dogs.

Title: Alpine Lakeside Hiking Challange in Northumberland
Summary:
In one of the most remote, scenic and tranquil parts of England, join Lee for a 43km (27 mile) (ish) walk in one of his favorite hiking spots. This challenging walk takes us through forests and pastures on gravel tracks along the waterside of Kielder reservoir offering tremendous views!
So, if you think you can manage it...what are you waiting for!
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Image credits: all images provided by event Leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: This is approximately 43km (27 miles) along undulating gravel paths. No really steep slopes but be prepared for a fair amount of up and down hills; estimated total of around 230m of assent..

Title: East London architecture - Victorian, Brutalism & Hi-Tech!
Summary:
This walk spans Stratford, Bow and Poplar which has seen much change over the years. From the Victorian slums, to the devastation caused during the Blitz, and the subsequent post war rebuilding programme. The former London Docks, which saw goods arriving from all over the world, to the creation of the Canary Wharf metropolis and the transformation of Stratford for the London Olympics in 2012.
I've really enjoyed researching this walk, and I hope you'll enjoy it too. There's plenty of architecture and history and a number of hidden gems along the way, plus it's accessible via a myriad of East London's lesser known waterways, which lead us to the Thames Path.
Walking route and itinerary
This is a linear walk starting at
Stratford Station
rail, underground station exit (Westfield entrance) and ending at the Jubilee Line underground station in
Canary Wharf.
This route map can be viewed in
OS Maps
.
Points of interest:
Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park
Aquatics Centre
London Stadium
ArcelorMittal Orbit
Former Bow Gasworks
Three Mills island Watermill
Brutalist Balfron Tower
City Island
Trinity Buoy Wharf
Canary Wharf
Waterways we'll follow:
Water Works River
City Mill River
Three Mills Wall River Weir
Channelsea River
Bow Creek
Limehouse Cut
River Thames
We meet by the entrance to the Westfield shopping centre where you can buy food and drink and use the toliets. We'll walk through the centre towards the Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park.
After passing the Aquatics Centre on our left we'll cross Water Works River and head for the London Stadium and pick up the path adjacent to the City Mill River. As we exit the park we cross Stratford High Street and take the path adjacent to Three Mills Wall River Weir. We continue to Three Mills Green where we navigate our way around Three Mills Island. We'll stop to admire the beautifully restored watermills. Next to the Channelsea River you'll see some of the last surviving Victorian gasholder frames in the former Bow Gas Works. We take a slight detour from the river to visit a memorial to hundreds of employees of the Gas Light and Coke Company killed in both world wars.
We continue along Bow Creek, before reaching Limehouse Cut. Here we leave the river and walk through the housing estates in Poplar. We'll stop to admire the magnificent brutalist Balfron Tower by Ernő Goldfinger, sister building to the Trellick Tower in Kensington.
From here we'll head towards City Island, one of London's newest neighbourhoods, accessible via Canning Town station. We once again cross Bow Creek, where you can pick up refreshments from Sainsburys and buy coffee etc. From here we follow the path to the historic Trinity Buoy Wharf, where we'll stop for lunch and take in the views. There's a cafe and toliets and a restored lightship you can visit, plus a free view of London from the former lighthouse and numerous sculptures and history information boards dotted around the place.
We then follow the Thames Path back to Canary Wharf.
Afterwards
We'll head to a bar for a post walk drink.
New members
New members are always welcome. Please message me in advance if you have any questions before the walk.
Waitlist: If this event is fully booked, but you'd like to attend, please add yourself to the waitlist. Places often become available closer to the date.
All photos taken by the event leader.
Difficulty & Distance: 12 km/7.5 miles flat terrain on paved and gravel paths. Some steps as we negotiate bridges.

Summary:

James.Couldry@outdoorlads.com

Title: The BH5K Naked Run!
Summary:
When it comes to running you've possibly done a parkrun, the Great North Run and maybe if you've been very lucky, the London Marathon. However it is unlikely that you've enjoyed the fun and the challenge of baring all to compete in the Naturist Foundation Naked 5K.
Taking place in the beautiful Northwest Kent countryside on a
private
50 acre site, the course winds its way through woodland paths and stunning surroundings. All this is done while running exactly as nature intended (although running shoes are very highly recommended!!).  Please note that this is mixed gender, all ages event.
You must register for the event via this
link
; booking onto the ODL website does not get you into the run.  The cost is £22.00 per participant, paid through the link when registering.
Registration opens at 9.30, with a course "familiarisation" walk at 11.00 and the run starting at 11.30.  The run is timed, with all participants receiving a participation medal, and trophies to the top 3 males over 50 and the top three overall finishers.
After the run, stick around for the afternoon and make the most of a carnival atmosphere with music and stalls. Enjoy our fantastic facilities — relax in our 18 metre heated covered swimming pool, unwind in the sauna, or freshen up with our hot showers (20p). The Teapot Café serves hot food and our licensed outdoor ‘Sunshine Bar’ will also be open, perfect for refuelling and socializing.
Naturism is the practice of non-sexual social nudity. For those new to naturism, you’ll soon find that everyone respects you for who you are and will treat you with dignity whatever shape or size.
All photos kindly provided for use by the Naturist Foundation, BrockenHurst, Kent.
Difficulty & Distance: You need to be able to walk or run a 5K

Title: Poets, Peaks & Beavers
Summary:
"
Is this the hill? is this the kirk? / Is this mine own countree? / O let me be awake, my God! / Or let me sleep alway."
> —
Samuel Taylor Coleridge, The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
Step into "Coleridge Country" for a day hike that perfectly balances Devonshire history with stunning coastal vistas. We’ll be starting in the ancient town of
Ottery St Mary
—a place so historic it’s name-checked in the Domesday Book.
Whether you're a fan of Romantic poetry, a lover of high ridgelines, or a wildlife enthusiast, this walk has a bit of everything.
The Hike Highlights
Historic Ottery:
We’ll begin by exploring the town and its "Miniature Exeter Cathedral"—the stunning Parish Church—before leaving the flagstones behind for the forest.
The Big View:
We’ll climb through the atmospheric
East Hill Woods
to reach the ridgeline. From
Beacon Hill
, you’ll be treated to a breathtaking panorama of the Jurassic Coast, stretching from the red cliffs of Sidmouth down toward Budleigh Salterton and Exmouth.
The River Otter & The Beavers:
We’ll descend into the lush valley to follow the winding River Otter back to town. Keep your eyes peeled! This river is famous for its
wild beaver population
. We’ll be looking for gnawed branches, lodges, and—if we’re lucky—the residents themselves.
The Day’s Plan
Meet Up:
We’ll gather in the center of Ottery St Mary (check location pin for exact spot) ready for a prompt start.
The Pace:
This is a social ODL walk. We’ll take the hills at a steady pace and stop for a proper hill-top lunch to soak in those coastal views.
The Finish:
We’ll aim to be back in Ottery in time for a post-hike drink at a local pub—the perfect way to toast a successful day on the trail.
New to OutdoorLads?
Day hikes are the perfect way to "test the waters" with the group. You’ll find a warm welcome, plenty of conversation, and a leader who ensures no one gets left behind. Just turn up and enjoy the fresh air!
Dogs
Dogs are welcome to join us on this event but we do ask the following:
Please appreciate the fact that not everyone is a dog owner or lover- especially when we stop to eat
Please ensure you adhere to the Countryside Code at all times - see (
Keeping Dogs Under Effective Control)
If your dog is uncontrolled and strays in open land frightening other animals or livestock, the leader is supported by the OutdoorLads board of trustees to ask you to leave the event as this is not acceptable behaviour.
Photo Credits:
All photos taken by OutdoorLads members and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: Roughly 10 mile hike through fields and forests. Some steep sections.

Title: Miles of Marbury
Summary:
Hi everyone,
Come and join me for a relaxed, easy circular walk in the beautiful Marbury area.
We head to Marbury to enjoy the big views from the top of the hill, and a gentle stroll along the canal, through several open fields, and around Marbury Big Mere Lake. The walk will finish at Marbury Church.
This easy, casual leisure walk is ideal if you’re new to OutdoorLads, or if you’re simply looking to meet new guys and make friends in a relaxed setting.
We’ll stop at the Willeymoor Lock Tavern for lunch, then finish back at the car park, with the option of a refreshing drink or meal at the pub afterwards.
Distance:
10 km (6 miles)
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised, and fully under-control dogs are welcome. Dogs must be kept on a lead where signposted and whenever we are near sheep or livestock.
New Members:
Please feel free to sign up to this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don’t hesitate to message me, or visit the “My First Event” section under “About Us”.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
If the event is fully booked, please join the waiting list, as places often become available in the week leading up to the walk.
Image/Photo Credits:
All images are the event leader’s own and are shared with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: Mainly  flat, with a small number of gentle hills

Title: A Springtime Social in Bolton - 2026
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for a springtime
Saturday
evening stroll in Bolton for our vibrant
springtime social !
Join us as we welcome the arrival of spring with an unforgettable evening exploring some of the area’s most characterful brew houses. We will visit the historic
Ye Olde Man & Scythe
, a pub steeped in centuries of stories and tradition. Believed to date back to before 1251, it’s famed not only for its remarkable heritage but also for its spine-tingling tales of hauntings and ghostly goings-on. With its cosy corners, timbered charm, and warm, welcoming atmosphere, it’s the perfect setting to relax with a drink in hand. Add to that the fantastic regular live bands, and you have the ideal backdrop for a lively spring celebration !
After soaking up the history and atmosphere, we’ll head out to explore more of the local area, embracing the vibrant, fun, and easy-going spirit of the evening. There’ll be plenty of opportunities to sample a fine ale (or your drink of choice), enjoy some tasty nibbles, and share laughs with great company. For those who feel like keeping the night going, there’ll be the chance to throw some shapes on the dancefloor and really make the most of the springtime celebrations.
Our socials are a brilliant way for both new and long-standing members to connect. If you’ve been curious about joining
OutdoorLads
, wondering whether it’s the right fit for you, or feeling unsure about fitness levels or experience — this is the perfect opportunity to come along and see what we’re all about. There’s no pressure, just a friendly group of people, a relaxed environment, and the chance to make genuine connections.
So why not give us a try? Come and share a drink, a chat, and a few laughs — and discover why so many members have built lasting friendships through OutdoorLads. We’d love to welcome you. 🌸🍻
Smart/casual dress code. Please no shorts or trackies !
Transport:
For easy access to/from Manchester and surrounding areas, the train and bus station/interchange is located in the town centre and is only a few minutes walk to the meeting point.
For more info, click
here...(https://tfgm.com/)
Photos taken by Growler, with permission for OutdoorLads to reuse.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Waitlist
: If this event is fully booked and you want to attend, just add yourself to the waitlist, because places inevitably become available closer to the date.
Difficulty & Distance: Relax and enjoy

Title: Explore Wales’ Largest Ancient Woodland (located between Bristol and Cardiff)
Summary:
Going through mixed woodland and seeing lots of bluebells, we will pass the highest point in Wentwood (309m) on our way to the  Curley Oak (close to the most recent (1996) execution), then to the Foresters Oaks, where twice yearly Law Courts were held, with the last offender hanged in 1829.
We will then go past the Wentwood Reservoir and up to the summit of Gray Hill (273m) where we will find a Megalithic alignment of standing stones, stone circle and  neolithic or Bronze age enclosure.
We will then head back to the car park via the “Five Lanes” crossing, where it is possible to head in 10 different directions, which was the scene of Gwent’s biggest ever illegal rave (3000 people, for 36 hours) in 2007.
The area is home to thousands of wildlife species and we may be lucky enough to spot wild deer.
Dogs:
Dogs under close control are welcome on this event, so long as owners are able to lift them over locked gates and high stiles.
Photo Credits
: All photos taken by Paul Ridealgh; permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12km (7.5 miles) in 4.5 hours

Title: BikeOut Festival 2026
Summary:
You are cordially invited to join the UKs first queer festival of the bicycle!  This will be run in conjunction with a whole range of other LGBT bike groups and is supported by Rapha and All In Racing.  There will be a whole range of cycles offered across the weekend - road and gravel are the name of the game.  There will also be some social events across the weekend, so it should be  a great way to get some miles in and meet some new people.  They plan to cater for most abilities - if you are used to cycling at least 40km on a road/gravel mix then there should be a ride for you.  If you are a super-fast road demon then that's no problem either - I am reliably informed there will be some advanced level rides too.
This event is being run by the BikeOut team, but OutdoorLads are an invited member group.  You will, however, have to register directly with the festival itself in order to attend the events: you can do so at this link
https://bikeout.cc/
You can also sign up here, on the outdoorlads website, so that we know who is coming from OutdoorLads and to help co-ordinate some social events over the course of the weekend.
There are cycles planned for both the Saturday and Sunday of the weekend.  There will also be an introductory, social event on the Friday night.
Bike hire is possible from AlpKit in Hathersage - please contact and arrange this early.
There are lots of accommodation options in the area -the bikeout website has details of them.  I, however, will be staying at North Lees campsite -
https://www.peakdistrict.gov.uk/visiting/things-to-do/camping/camping-northlees
This has both pods and camping pitches, and has the advantage of being the base for the rapha team for the weekend and also the planned site of a Saturday night pizza/bbq event.  The website says that you can take a campervan, but they don't have hard parking or electric hook-up - you need to park it in a standard parking space.
Difficulty & Distance: There will be a range of rides on offer, so you can choose from easier to harder

Title: Ramble above Bury
Summary:
Join us for this dog friendly walk in Bury. We see the woods, hills and rivers of Bury. It's really amazing to have lovely countryside so close to a city. The walk is undulating so walking boots are a must. The walk is about
16km
long. You will be able to see the tall buildings of Manchester from our vantage point along with Ramsbottom and a small section of the river and woodland.
If the walk is full please join the waiting list as often a space becomes available
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.

Title: BSC26: Rent a Tent
Summary:
This is for renting a tent at
BIG Spring Camp 2026.
If you're travelling light, you may like to borrow a tent from OutdoorLads. If that's the case, just sign up to this event page, and we'll provide you with a tent when you register on arrival.
For £15 we will provide you a tent and a sleeping mat.
You will need to bring a sleeping bag
or other sleeping medium. You will need to collect the tent on arrival at registration - it is very easy to put up. Whilst it is a two man tent, be aware that is actually is quite small and if there are two people in it, it will be extremely cosy!
At the end of the event, all we ask is that you please take the tent down and pack it away, and return it to the reception area where you poicked it up from.
This is real convenience so take advantage and make your BIG Spring Camp that little bit easier!
Photos kindly supplied by and used with permission from OutdoorLad Sam.

Title: Waterfalls, Caves and Lime kiln
Summary:
Join us for a 15km walk from Settle to Stainforth, exploring some of Yorkshire's hidden gems. Our adventure begins in the charming market town of Settle. As we head east, we’ll visit Scaleber Force waterfall, nestled in a beautiful wooded ravine. The cascading waters run over limestone steps create a picturesque setting, perfect for photography enthusiasts.
Next, we'll head around Sugar Loaf Hill to explore the intriguing Victoria and Jubilee caves, rich with fascinating limestone formations and historical significance. These caves offer a unique glimpse into the area's geological and human past. Continuing our journey, we’ll discover Catrigg Force, a secluded waterfall in a tranquil woodland glade. The twin plunges of water provide a peaceful spot to relax and appreciate nature's beauty.
As we approach Stainforth, we'll then encounter the Hoffman Kilns, remnants of the region's industrial heritage. These towering structures, once used for lime burning, stand as a testament to the area's history. This walk is a perfect blend of natural beauty and historical intrigue, making it an ideal walk. Join us and experience the wonders of the Yorkshire Dales!
When we finish we can grab a brew and cake from the Naked Man Cafe.
(All photographs taken by the walk leader and with permission to use)
Difficulty & Distance: Well marked paths and tracks, 15km  400m height gain.

Title: Ride to the Sun 2026 🌅 🚴‍♂️ 🌙
Summary:
Ride to the Sun 2026 is your chance to experience one of the UK’s most magical cycling adventures—100 miles through the shortest night, crossing borders and chasing the solstice sunrise.
Set off from Carlisle on Saturday evening, and ride 100 miles through quiet country roads to Cramond Beach in Edinburgh, arriving just in time for the 04:26 sunrise. This free, unsupported ride blends camaraderie, history, and spectacle—bagpipes at the Devil’s Beef Tub, the CycloRave™ in Tweedsmuir, and a celebratory beer at the finish. Ride to the Sun isn’t just a ride - it’s a solstice tradition!
The terrain is mostly flat / gentle until Moffat, where we then have a 6-mile climb to the Devil's Beef Tub, after which it is mostly downhill or undulating.
For more details and to register for free visit: -
https://www.ridetothesun.co.uk
Note that this is not a supported ride and you should be self-sufficient and equipped to deal with any minor breakdowns. The leader will have a van available at the end of the ride to deal with any more serious mechanical failures en route.
Make sure to bring spare lights (or a battery pack / spare batteries) as over half of the ride will be in darkness.
It's not a closed road event, but traffic will be relatively quiet during the night, even on the A-road sections and with around 1000 participants, there will be cyclists' tail lights in eyeshot for most of the ride.
ROUTE:-
https://ridewithgps.com/routes/53144089
https://www.strava.com/routes/3417844667953890036
https://www.komoot.com/tour/2655547556
Photos:
Taken by the leader on a previous RTTS in 2024.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in Scotland?
Join our Telegram
chat
and
notification
groups and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: 162km (101 miles) with 1473m of climbing

Title: River Dove and Home by Bunster Hill Circular
Summary:
Starting from Alstonefield, which is one of the prettiest villages in the Peak District, we will take a steep descent to meet the River Dove at Milldale and here have a coffee break and a chance to feed the ducks.
Crossing the footbridge, we will head South, beside the gushing River Dove, where we'll meet a variety of rock features: Dove Holes Caves, Ilam Rock, Lover's Leap and finally the stepping stones that cross the river. Then it's up to the lofty heights of Bunster Hill for stunning views, before crossing the open fields towards Stanshope and back to Alstonefield where a refreshing pint can be had at The George Pub.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in the Midlands / ODL Central Region?
Join our
Central Region Telegram Chat Group
, or the more local
East Midlands Telegram Chat Group
and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link:
https://outdoorlads.events/central
All photos supplied by the Mike Wingfield and permission is granted to ODL for their use and re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 8 miles 4.5 hours

Title: May’s Chester Social
Summary:
A great opportunity for anyone interested in OutdoorLads to come along and find out more.
We will start at the Brewhouse & Kitchen to enjoy a few drinks and a fantastic atmosphere as we meet up with old and new friends from OutdoorLads.
This is simply a relaxed evening enjoying a few drinks with a great opportunity to chat to existing members about the group, what events we have coming up and find out how friendly and welcoming we are.
We may explore more of the city as the evening goes on, but we can decide that on the night.
More Chester Socials coming up 👋
Our Chester socials take place bi-monthly, so this won’t be the only chance to join us. If you’d like to meet more people and enjoy relaxed social events in Chester, we also have socials planned for
January
and
March
. Everyone is welcome, whether it’s your first event or you’ve joined us before.
Photo credit: all taken by Phil_30.
Difficulty & Distance: Social event

Title: Beddgelert Mountain Weekened Day 1: Moel Hebog
Summary:
Join us up Moel Hebog and get some spectacular views across Snowdonia ⛰️
____________________________________________________
This is the first day of a pair of mountain walks in the Beddgelert area of Eryri. If you just want to come for this walk, that's no problem; though if you'd like to make a weekend of it, join us for part two on
Snowdon’s South Ridge
If you do want to make a weekend of it, there are several campsites nearby, including
Beddgelert Camp Site
,
Cau Du Campsite
and the
Cwellyn Arms Campsite
, which also has a
bunkhouse and private rooms
. For those wanting more glamorous lodgings, there are plenty of nice hotels and B&Bs in Beddgelert and nearby towns and villages such as Caernarfon, Porthmadog and Portmeirion.
I'll set up a Telegram group for both events nearer the time to arrange car sharing and a possible meal on the Saturday evening
_____________________________________________________
From our meeting point we'll head straight up the biggest peak of the day,
Moel Hebog
(782m). We'll have the hardest, most
breathtaking
, part out of the way pretty soon. From the top of Moel Hebog we should have some spectacular views, including of Yr Wyddfa/Snowdon. Perfect for a little photo shoot 🤳
From here we'll descend and then head up the next peak,
Moel Yr Ogof
(655m) with some pleasant, easy scrambling involved. Then on to
Moel Lefn
(638m), with some boulders to clamber over. We'll get some great views of the stunning Nantlle Ridge from here, and of the Forest below, through which we'll descent and head back to our start point.
Once finished, you're welcome to join us for a well earned drink at a pub in Beddgelert.
Please note that this is a fairly substantial walk that will take us
up to
7 hours, depending on the group. Please come prepared with an appetite for a big day out. There are some scrambly sections on this route, but experienced hill walkers should find it perfectly achievable.
Dogs
: Capable dogs are very much welcome on this event, though will need to be kept on a lead through fields with livestock and when passing close to private properties.
Photos: Taken by event leader and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: This hike is about 14km (9 miles) with ~950m (3115ft) of ascent. A good level of fitness is required, especially at the start of the walk for the steep ascent. There are some short, easy scrambly sections.

Title: Beddgelert Mountain Weekend Day 2: Snowdon's South Ridge
Summary:
Join Outdoor Lads on an epic traverse of Wale's highest mountain via the stunning South Ridge
_________________________________________________________
This is the second day of a pair of mountain walks in the Beddgelert area of Eryri. If you just want to come for this walk, that's no problem; though if you'd like to make a weekend of it, join us for part one on
Moel Hebog
If you do want to make a weekend of it, there are several campsites nearby, including
Beddgelert Camp Site
,
Cau Du Campsite
and the
Cwellyn Arms Campsite
, which also has a
bunkhouse and private rooms
. For those wanting more glamorous lodgings, there are plenty of nice hotels and B&Bs in Beddgelert and nearby towns and villages such as Caernarfon, Porthmadog and Portmeirion.
I'll set up a Telegram group for both events nearer the time to arrange car sharing and a possible meal on the Saturday evening.
__________________________________________________________
Points of interest on the route:
The Bwlch Cwm Llan Quarries:
As we make our way to the ridge, we'll travel through some of the old slate quarries, giving interesting insights into North Wales's industrial heritage
The South Ridge:
This is the most thrilling part of the walk, with great views down to Cwm Clogwyn and Tregalan. These beautiful glacial valleys drop down steeply from both sides of the ridge, so we'll make sure to take our time during this section of the walk.
Yr Wyddfa's Summit:
Snowdon, or Yr Wyddfa, is Wales's highest peak, reaching 1085m. While the summit will no doubt be busy with fellow hikers; should the weather be clear, we'll have stunning views over the National Park and beyond. As there is a café on the summit, we'll stay here for our lunch break, before descending via the Snowdon Ranger Path.
The Welsh Highland Railway:
As we cut off the Ranger path to head back to Rhyd Ddu, we'll cross the tracks of the Welsh Highland Railway. This heritage line runs regularly from Porthmadog to Caernarfon, and if we're lucky, we'll see it as we cross.
Other Infomation:
Dog Suitability:
Dogs are always welcome on my walks, though their behaviour and safety is their owner's responsibility. This includes cleaning up after them, controlling their behaviour around other animals or people, and assisting them over any stiles or streams we may cross.
New Members
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
What if there is a Waiting List?
If this event is fully booked and you want to attend, just add yourself to the waitlist, because places inevitably become available closer to the date.
All photo taken by the event leader, with permission given for further use by OutdoorLads.
Difficulty & Distance: This hike is about 15km (9.5 miles) with ~1000m (3280ft) of ascent. The South Ridge involves simple scrambling over rocky terrain, with mild exposure (big drops), so you'll need a head for heights. The rest of the walk follows established mountain paths.

Title: Bruges Blast: A North Sea Adventure
Summary:
OutdoorLads are sailing across the North Sea to Belgium!
We're delighted to offer this event following the success of OutdoorLads x CatZero's "Dutch Dash" in 2025!
You do not need any past sailing experience for this. This is an incredible opportunity to spend five days aboard CatZero, as we take on this 400-mile round-trip across the North Sea from the Humber estuary to Zeebrugge and back (a "Bruges Blast")!
CatZero is a 72-foot Challenge Round the World Racing Yacht. Category Zero is the rating of the vessel, meaning she can sail anywhere in the world in all conditions. CatZero is also the name of the charity that own and operate the vessel.
On this voyage, you will be crewing the boat under the instruction and direction of the fantastic CatZero team. Absolute beginner with no sailing experience, or total sailing pro? It doesn't matter if you've never set foot on a boat before or if you're a qualified sailor, these trips are for everyone. You'll learn what you need to know to sail the ship.
This is not a leisure holiday! Voyages aboard CatZero are interactive, fun and rewarding. You'll be living in very close proximity with the rest of team, and working together throughout the five days. Whilst sailing (approximately 24 hours each way) we will split into two watches, alternating every four hours to rest/work, including through the night. You'll take your role in helping run the boat, from cooking, cleaning, scrubbing the decks and helping sail throughout your trip. Team work and camaraderie will be in abundance, and we're sure you'll have an absolutely fantastic time on board. You'll be exhausted by the end of the week, but you will have had the absolute time of your lives, learn a lot about sailing, yourself, and each other - this is the kind of trip that creates special memories.
Past sailing experience is not necessary - there will be an experienced crew on board who will show you everything you need to do. If you've never sailed before but think you'd enjoy it, and are prepared to get stuck in and give it your all for a five days, then this is a perfect event for you! Included as part of this trip, you can even complete the
RYA Competent Crew qualification
!
On arrival into Zeebrugge on the Saturday morning, we'll put the sails away and then be able to have a shower, refresh ourselves, and have a restful explore of the city, or just plonk ourselves somewhere for some refreshments in the July sunshine. Depending on timing, passport control, and transport available, we may be able to venture into the centre of Bruges. You are also free to do your own thing.
If you have any questions before you book please feel free to contact the event Leader.

Some Ts&Cs
CatZero's and OutdoorLads' Liability
CatZero or OutdoorLads will take no responsibility for loss or damage of personal belongings on this event, and will accept no liability.
CatZero or OutdoorLads have no liability to the participant in the event the participant fails to board the yacht prior to sailing, unless caused by the negligence of CatZero or its representatives.
Health
You must be mentally and physically fit for the sail. All participants must be in general good health.
You will be required to complete a medical questionnaire prior to sailing, and you must make CatZero aware of any injuries, illnesses and/or medications you are taking. You must notify CatZero of any changes between comlpetion of the medical questionnaire and the sailing.
CatZero can refuse to accept you if they believe that the safety and/or smooth running of the sail would be at risk due to any injuries or illness a participant has, or due to any other medical reason.
Safety
The safety of all participants is of paramount importance to CatZero. Sailing can be dangerous and hazardous, and all participants must accept those risks and always have regard to the safety of themselves and third parties.
Whilst on the yacht, all participants must comply at all times with the authority, decisions, and instructions of the Skipper. All participants must comply with with all safety guidelines and instructions given by the Skipper and other CatZero representatives.
All participants must not engage in any activity, whether shore based or on board the yacht, which is likely to compromise their safety or the safety of any other person.
Failure to comply may result in the Skipper ask the individual to vacate the yacht.
The Skipper or CatZero will have the absolute authority, without providing any prior notice, ask an individual or individuals to vacate the yacht if the safety or smooth running of the sail is deemed to be at risk.
In the event that an individual is asked to vacate the yacht, CatZero will not be liable for costs incurred.
Insurance
CatZero or OutdoorLads do not provide any insurance covering the participant.
The participant is responsible for making their own arrangements for adequate insurance to cover the cost of personal accident, personal liability, medical expenses, and emergency repatriation during the sail, including travel to/from the yacht.
The participant agrees to indemnify CatZero in respect of any costs or expenses claimed against CatZero or incurred by CatZero which would ordinarily be covered by adequate insurance taken out by the participant.
Behaviour
Throughout the period of the sail, both when ashore and on board the yacht, all participants shall:
behave in a moderate and reasonable manner, taking into account of the people around them
do nothing which may render the yacht liable to arrest or impounding
not take or consume personal stocks of alcohol on board the yacht
not behave in a manner that is or is likely to be disruptive, abusive, antisocial, dangerous, or likely to cause upset or distress to any third party or damage to property
Not use, carry, or store any illegal drugs, substances or any other illegal items
Not engage in any activity which is likely to compromise the reputation of CatZero
If in the Skipper's reasonable opinion, any participant is in breach of the above, in any way, they shall have the absolute authority, without providing prior notice, to ask them to vacate the yacht.

Title: Dorset Double, Day 2: St Aldhelm's Head and Dancing Ledge
Summary:
Introduction:
Last year it was an 'All-New Dorset Double' based in Lyme Regis, but this year it's back to the classic one based in Purbeck. As usual, there are two walks over a bank holiday weekend, and you can do either or both, but you have to organise your own accommodation. Campsites, hotels and B&Bs are plentiful, although they will be booking up fast. The 'Double' walks normally take place in the second May Bank Holiday weekend, but I will be away then, and by doing it on the first weekend you can go to Big Spring Camp on the second. Despite the weather being slightly cooler at the start of May, I hope you can come and experience the best of the Jurassic Coast Unesco World Heritage Site - some of the finest coastal scenery in the country (nay the world) - and just two hours from London.
Schedule:
Day 1 (Saturday 2 May): Corfe Castle to Kimmeridge, Kimmeridge Ledges, Chapman's Pool and Kingston - circular walk (or linear with bus back)
Day 2 (Sunday 3 May): Corfe Castle to Kingston, St Aldhelm's Head, Winspit, Dancing Ledge and Swanage - linear walk with bus back
Day 2: Corfe Castle to St Aldhelm's Head, Winspit, Dancing Ledge and Swanage
Our survey of the spectacular scenery of the Jurassic Coast continues with a linear walk where the gentle heaths of Corfe Common give way to the immense limestone cliffs of Purbeck, culminating in the breathtaking prominence of St Aldhelm's Head. It also takes in the scarred and eerie landscape of Winspit Quarries, the remarkable quasi-natural stage of Dancing Ledge and the Victorian earnestness of Durlston Country Park. After a journey through geological deep time, industrial recent time, and science-fictional space-time, we'll end up in Swanage, where cafés, ice cream parlours and amusement arcades will evoke family holidays before the bus gets you quickly back to Corfe Castle.
The sights:
Corfe Castle:
Built by William the Conqueror and one of the first castles to be built with stone. Updated in the C12 and C13. Sold by Elizabeth I in 1572 to Sir Christopher Hatton, and then in 1635 to Sir John Bankes whose wife, Lady Mary Bankes, led the defence of the castle when it was twice besieged by Parliamentarian forces. The first siege, in 1643, was unsuccessful, but the second, in 1645, succeeded. In March that year the castle was slighted on Parliament's orders. A Grade I-listed building and Scheduled Ancient Monument.
Kingston:
Set on high ground with wide views toward Corfe Castle and Poole Harbour. The name is from 'king’s farm', reflecting Saxon royal ownership before later monastic control. The colossal Church of St James, built in 1873 by noted architect George Edmund Street, dominates the village. It was funded by Lord Eldon as a private chapel and became the parish church in 1922 when the former parish church (now a private residence) became too dilapidated.
St Aldhelm’s Head:
A dramatic limestone headland famous for sheer coastal cliffs, seabirds, and St Aldhelm’s Chapel, a square stone vaulted building from the C12, thought to have served as a beacon or place of pilgrimage, with sweeping sea views.
Winspit Quarries:
An abandoned limestone quarry complex, worked from the early C18 to the late C20. The quarries are cut directly into the cliffs, with galleries and tunnels opening onto the sea. Winspit is also culturally notable as a filming location for classic science fiction, including
Doctor Who
(as Skaro in 'Destiny of the Daleks' from 1979),
Blake’s 7
(as Mecron 2 in 'Games' from 1981) and more recently the series
Star Wars: Andor
, giving it an enduring cult reputation.
Dancing Ledge:
A broad wave-cut platform at the foot of the cliffs near Langton Matravers, created by a combination of natural erosion and quarrying. Quarrymen once loaded stone directly onto boats from the ledge, and cut pools remain as evidence. The name may refer to the movement of waves across the flat rock.
Tilly Whim Caves:
A group of former limestone quarries cut into the cliffs at Durlston, worked mainly in the C18 and C19. The caves supplied building stone for local use and were later opened as a Victorian tourist attraction. Quarrying ceased and the caves were closed in the 1970s due to rockfall risk. Although inaccessible, they remain an important wildlife site, supporting bats and seabirds, and illustrate the industrial exploitation of Purbeck geology. Anvil Point Lighthouse is from 1881 and is fully automatic.
Durlston Head:
A prominent limestone headland south of Swanage. The cliffs expose a sequence of Purbeck and Portland limestones that record changing environments from lagoons to shallow seas. The area bears clear evidence of historic quarrying, alongside later maritime and industrial features such as coastal defences and navigation aids. Now managed as Durlston Country Park, the headland is valued for its geology, biodiversity and panoramic coastal views. A notable landmark is the Great Globe, built in 1887 by George Burt: a massive stone sphere over 3 metres in diameter, carved with a detailed map of the world and astonishingly accurate astronomical data, and mounted on a Purbeck stone plinth, celebrating Victorian science and craftsmanship.
Swanage:
A coastal town on the eastern edge of the Isle of Purbeck, nestled between bays of limestone and chalk. Its development was shaped by nearby stone quarries and later by Victorian tourism, transforming it into a seaside resort. The town retains strong maritime and cultural traditions, centred on its pier, heritage railway and annual festivals. Swanage also serves as a gateway to the Jurassic Coast, with close connections to fossil hunting, coastal walking and the wider geological heritage of Purbeck.
The
route
(please click here to check the route on the Ordnance Survey website and see elevations and ascents)
:
Heading south out of Corfe Castle, along West Street, we'll reach Corfe Common and use the Hardy Way to get to Kingston. We'll walk along South Street and reach the Purbeck Way which will link up with the South West Coast Path, which will take us all the way to Swanage.

(click the links).

If the cost of travelling and staying over is daunting, do consider using the OutdoorLads Foundation. This enables paying members to receive up to £200 in total a year towards any expense they incur participating in an OutdoorLads event. Just apply simply and confidentially

Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this one may be suitable for dogs with lots of stamina. It is long, has some fields with livestock and will partly be along lanes. Much of the route is exposed to the sun so a dog may overheat, although they will be able to go in the sea at Dancing Ledge. Your dog must be responsive if it is off the lead.

Title: Dorset Double, Day 1: Kimmeridge Ledges and Chapman's Pool
Summary:
Introduction:
Last year it was an 'All-New Dorset Double' based in Lyme Regis, but this year it's back to the classic one based in Purbeck. As usual, there are two walks over a bank holiday weekend, and you can do either or both, but you have to organise your own accommodation. Campsites, hotels and B&Bs are plentiful, although they will be booking up fast. The 'Double' walks normally take place in the second May Bank Holiday weekend, but I will be away then, and by doing it on the first weekend you can go to Big Spring Camp on the second. Despite the weather being slightly cooler at the start of May, I hope you can come and experience the best of the Jurassic Coast Unesco World Heritage Site - some of the finest coastal scenery in the country (nay the world) - and just two hours from London.
Schedule:
Day 1 (Saturday 2 May): Corfe Castle to Kimmeridge, Kimmeridge Ledges, Chapman's Pool and Kingston - circular walk (or linear with bus back)
Day 2 (Sunday 3 May): Corfe Castle to Kingston, St Aldhelm's Head, Winspit, Dancing Ledge and Swanage - linear walk with bus back
Day 1: Corfe Castle to Kimmeridge Ledges and Chapman's Pool
Our survey of the spectacular scenery of the Jurassic Coast starts with a circular walk, where a range of landscapes from sandy heaths to chalk downs and limestone wolds will culminate in a stretch using the South West Coast Path from Tyneham Cap to Chapman's Pool. It'll be a bit longer than Sunday's walk, but you could make it more manageable by getting the bus back to Corfe Castle from Kingston (no matter if we've just missed it; we'll have a drink at The Scott Arms while waiting). Among the highlights of the route will be the Kimmeridge Ledges, which are banks of rock sticking out into the sea, and Chapman's Pool, an idyllic oval of turquoise water, framed by vividly verdant cliffs.
The sights:
Corfe Castle:
Built by William the Conqueror and one of the first castles to be built with stone. Updated in the C12 and C13. Sold by Elizabeth I in 1572 to Sir Christopher Hatton, and then in 1635 to Sir John Bankes whose wife, Lady Mary Bankes, led the defence of the castle when it was twice besieged by Parliamentarian forces. The first siege, in 1643, was unsuccessful, but the second, in 1645, succeeded. In March that year the castle was slighted on Parliament's orders. A Grade I listed building and Scheduled Ancient Monument.
Kimmeridge:
An attractive village of limestone cottages. Within Kimmeridge parish there are twenty-five listed buildings. The parish church was mostly rebuilt in 1872, though it has C12th origins; the south door and parts of the west wall of the nave are still original. The south porch is early C13.
The Etches Collection
(The Museum of Jurassic Marine Life) is an independent fossil museum, based on the collection of Steve Etches, a plumber and fossil hunter after whom some of his finds were named. The collection contains plesiosaur (including the 'Sea Rex' that Sir David Attenborough helped excavate in 2022) and ichthyosaur fossils as well as ammonites and other molluscs. The museum opened in 2016 and costs £9.50 to enter. If that's not your thing, you could have a drink at Clavell's restaurant in the village.
Kimmeridge Bay:
The bay is roughly semicircular and backed by low cliffs of Kimmeridge clay, and beneath the cliffs is a large wave-cut platform and a rocky shore. Within the clay are bands of bituminous shale, which in the history of the village have been the focus of several attempts to create an industrial centre. An oil well with nodding donkey has operated since 1959 and was producing 100,000 gallons a week in its early years. The village is globally recognised as the archetype of the Kimmeridgian Stage of the Late Jurassic Period.
Clavell Tower:
A folly high on the cliff eastwards of Kimmeridge Bay, built in 1830 in a Tuscan style. It inspired PD James's novel
The Black Tower
and was used in the 1985 ITV adaptation starring Roy Marsden.
At risk from falling into the sea, it was recently dismantled and then reassembled 35 m further back from the cliff edge. It is available as a holiday let.
Kimmeridge Ledges:
A set of Kimmeridge clay wave-cut platforms, their geology a highly important part of the Jurassic Coast World Heritage Site. Famous for the quality of the fossils they yield as they reveal an entire Late Jurassic marine ecosystem with sea reptiles, bivalves and ammonites, with many finds held in The Etches Collection.
Chapman's Pool:
A beautiful semicircular cove between two headlands. The rocks that form the cove are the upper parts of the Kimmeridge Clay, and are rich in fossils of bivalves and ammonites.
The
route
(please click here to check the route on the Ordnance Survey website and see elevations and ascents)
:
We'll pass Corfe Castle and walk along West Hill, Knowle Hill and Ridgeway Hill before dropping down to footpaths across fields to Kimmeridge. After a break in Kimmeridge village we'll use footpaths heading southwest to get to Kimmeridge Bay. We'll then be on the South West Coast Path, which we'll follow all the way along the coast to Chapman's Pool. At Chapman's Pool we'll use a footpath heading north to Kingston, where you can get the bus back or walk the full route using the Hardy Way to go back to Corfe Castle.

Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this one may be suitable for dogs with lots of stamina. It is long, has some fields with livestock and will partly be along lanes. Much of the route is exposed to the sun so a dog may overheat, although they will be able to go in the sea at Kimmeridge. Your dog must be responsive if it is off the lead.
